criticAssessment:
COVERAGE IS STRONG AND BROAD. The 15 tracks form a well-architected corpus with three notable strengths: (1) excellent attribution rigor — the recurring flags that the rainbow chakra scheme is a ~1977 Western synthesis (Leadbeater 1927 -> Hills 1977 -> Judith 1987), that Huna is a Max Freedom Long invention, that Holotropic Breathwork is an LSD-era synthesis, and the Candace Newmaker/Rebirthing disambiguation are all exactly the kind of cultural care this brief demands; (2) good comparative scaffolding (the Samuel & Johnston volume anchors a genuine "don't flatten these into one master chart" track); (3) the phenomenology/interoception track (Nummenmaa body maps) and the modern-science track (Zaccaro, Lehrer-Vaschillo, Balban/Huberman, Fincham) give an evidence-graded spine.

The major world systems are present: Indic prana/chakra/kundalini (twice, with overlap), Daoist qi/dantian/neidan, Tibetan tsa-lung/tummo, Buddhist anapanasati across schools, Japanese hara/ki/zazen, Sufi/Islamic nafas/lataif, Greek/Western-esoteric pneuma->Theosophy->orgone, modern breathwork, science-informed breathing, meditation styles, and visual grammar. There is meaningful redundancy between Tracks 1, 2, 12, and 15 on the Indic chakra system (acceptable for an app but it inflates apparent coverage).

GAPS fall into three buckets. (a) ABRAHAMIC/WEST-ASIAN beyond Sufism: Jewish (Hebrew neshamah/ruach/nefesh, Kabbalistic sefirot got named in the comparative track but never researched as a standalone breath/contemplative tradition; the four-worlds breath of Genesis 2:7; Hesychasm/Jesus Prayer and the Eastern Orthodox "breath + heart" technique from the Philokalia is entirely absent and is one of the most important breath-prayer traditions in the world). (b) CULTURES UNDER-REPRESENTED: Jain (a major Indian dharmic tradition with its own prana/preksha-meditation and a distinct ahimsa-based breath ethic — completely missing); Sikh (simran/naam japna, Anhad/Anahad Naad, swaas-swaas naam japna "breath by breath"); pre-Patanjali Vedic/ritual breath (the breath in Vedic chant/recitation, the agnihotra); Korean (Sun Do / Kouk Sun Do Daoist breathing) and Vietnamese; Polynesian/Māori beyond the brief mention; Australian Aboriginal; ancient Egyptian (the ankh/breath-of-life, "ka"); Andean/Mesoamerican (no coverage of e.g. Q'ero, or Aztec ihiyotl). (c) PRACTICE & DESIGN specifics: physiological/safety detail on retention practices and contraindications consolidated for app gating; the actual app-design/sonic/haptic translation layer is only lightly touched (one visual track + one phenomenology track) and a dedicated audit of how shipping apps (Calm, Headspace, Apple Breathe, Othership, Open, Breathwrk, Wim Hof app) actually implement pacers, plus accessibility for non-visual users; and the singing/chanting-as-breath-control family (overtone/Tuvan throat singing, Gregorian chant, Vedic/Quranic recitation, kirtan, Om chanting) as a cross-cutting breath practice. VERIFICATION TARGETS center on the cluster of "ancient-sounding but actually modern or physiologically dubious" claims the corpus partly flags but should be hardened with primary sources.

research.topic: Indian Yogic Prana & Pranayama (life-force, the five vayus, pranamaya kosha, classical pranayama, bandhas & kumbhaka)
research.summary:
Prana (Sanskrit प्राण, prāṇa, "breath / vital air / life-force") is the central animating principle of Indian yogic physiology. It is conceived not as breath alone but as the subtle vital energy that pervades the body, mind and cosmos; in Hindu literature it is said to originate from the Sun and to link the five elements. Breath is its grossest, most accessible expression, which is why breath-regulation (pranayama, prāṇāyāma = "extension/restraint of prana") becomes the principal lever for working with this energy. The concept is ancient: prana is discussed in the Atharvaveda and across the Chandogya, Katha, Mundaka, Prashna and Brihadaranyaka Upanishads (roughly mid-first-millennium BCE onward), formalized as the fourth of Patanjali's eight limbs in the Yoga Sutras (c. 2nd–4th c. CE), and elaborated into a detailed practical system of kumbhakas (retentions) and bandhas (locks) in the medieval Hatha-yoga corpus — chiefly the Hatha Yoga Pradipika of Svatmarama (15th c.), the Gheranda Samhita and Shiva Samhita.

Within the body, prana is said to differentiate into five functional "winds" (pancha vayu) — prana, apana, samana, udana, vyana — each with a seat, direction and physiological domain, supported by five minor sub-winds (upa-pranas: naga, kurma, krikara, devadatta, dhananjaya) that govern reflexes like belching, blinking, sneezing and yawning. Prana circulates through subtle channels (nadis, traditionally numbered 72,000), of which three are primary: ida (left, lunar, cooling), pingala (right, solar, heating) and sushumna (central, along the spinal axis). The practitioner's aim is to balance ida and pingala so that prana enters sushumna and rises, awakening kundalini — energy imaged as a coiled serpent at the base of the spine — upward through the chakras to the crown.

Prana also names the second of the five "sheaths" (koshas) of the self in the Taittiriya Upanishad's pancha-kosha model: the pranamaya kosha (vital-energy sheath) sits between the annamaya (physical/food) sheath and the manomaya (mental) sheath, with vijnanamaya (wisdom) and anandamaya (bliss) deeper still. Pranayama is therefore not merely a breathing exercise but a means of refining this vital sheath and moving inward toward the Self.

Note on framing: these are living religious/contemplative beliefs and traditional models, not biomedical claims. Sources frequently disagree on chakra colors, vayu-element correspondences and exact technique counts, and many "standard" associations (e.g., the rainbow chakra colors) are substantially shaped by 20th-century, often Western, syntheses rather than uniform classical doctrine.
research.historyAndOrigin:
Prana appears in the earliest layers of Indian thought. The Atharvaveda hymns prana as the force that "quickens" plants and sustains all life. The major early Upanishads (Chandogya, Katha, Mundaka, Prashna, Brihadaranyaka; c. 8th–4th c. BCE) develop prana as the chief vital breath, debate its primacy among the senses, and enumerate the vital airs. The Prashna Upanishad in particular treats the breaths and their distribution in the body. The Brihadaranyaka mentions 72,000 nadis; the Katha Upanishad references 101 channels radiating from the heart. The pancha-kosha (five-sheath) doctrine, including the pranamaya kosha, is set out in the Taittiriya Upanishad (2.1–5), commonly dated to roughly the 6th century BCE.

Patanjali's Yoga Sutras (c. 2nd–4th c. CE) codify pranayama as the fourth of the eight limbs (ashtanga). Sutras 2.49–2.53 define it as the cessation/regulation of the movement of inhalation and exhalation once a steady posture is attained, describe its three operations and a transcendent "fourth," and state its fruit as the thinning of the veil over inner light and fitness of the mind for concentration.

The detailed, practice-oriented systematization of named kumbhakas, bandhas, nadi-purification and kundalini belongs to the medieval Hatha-yoga and Tantric streams. Svatmarama's Hatha Yoga Pradipika (15th c.) is the principal reference, alongside the Gheranda Samhita and Shiva Samhita; these draw on earlier Nath and Tantric lineages. Pranayama also entered Tibetan Buddhist practice, where breath/wind work (rlung) underlies inner-heat (tummo) yoga. The modern global form of these practices was heavily mediated by 19th–20th century teachers (e.g., the Bihar School of Yoga / Satyananda, Sivananda lineage, Krishnamacharya and his students Iyengar and Pattabhi Jois), who standardized counts, ratios and the popular chakra-color scheme.

coreConcepts.aliases: prāṇa (प्राण); cf. Greek pneuma, Chinese qi, Latin anima
coreConcepts.meaning: Vital life-force / breath / animating energy pervading body and cosmos; both the universal energy and, specifically, the first of the five vayus.

coreConcepts.aliases: prāṇāyāma; prana + ayama (extension) or prana + yama (restraint)
coreConcepts.meaning: The practice of regulating, extending and restraining the breath/prana; fourth limb of Patanjali's eight-limbed yoga.
The five functional currents of prana within the body: prana, apana, samana, udana, vyana.
Five secondary/subordinate vital airs — naga, kurma, krikara, devadatta, dhananjaya — governing minor reflexes.
Five enveloping layers of the self (Taittiriya Upanishad): annamaya (food/physical), pranamaya (vital energy), manomaya (mind), vijnanamaya (wisdom), anandamaya (bliss).
The second sheath, made of prana; the energetic body experienced through breath, lying between the physical and mental sheaths.
Subtle channels through which prana flows; three primary ones are ida, pingala, sushumna.
Left/lunar/cooling channel; right/solar/heating channel; central channel along the spinal axis through which kundalini rises when ida and pingala are balanced.
Breath retention; antara kumbhaka after inhalation, bahya kumbhaka after exhalation; the heart of classical pranayama.
Muscular-energetic locks (mula, uddiyana, jalandhara; combined as maha bandha) that seal and redirect prana during retention.
Energy centers strung along sushumna where the nadis meet; classically six plus the crown, each a lotus with a set number of petals.
Dormant spiritual energy imaged as a coiled serpent at the base of the spine, awakened to rise through sushumna to the crown.
Three psychic knots (Brahma, Vishnu, Rudra granthi) along sushumna said to block prana's ascent until pierced.
Purificatory practices (e.g., Kapalabhati) classed in Hatha texts as cleansing kriyas rather than pranayama proper.
The science of nostril-dominance and breath-flow, noting which nostril/nadi is active and balancing them.

energyCenters.name: Muladhara (Root)
energyCenters.associations: 4-petalled lotus; color red; element earth (prithvi); bija mantra LAM; yellow square + downward red triangle; deities Ganesha and Dakini; seat of dormant kundalini and of apana vayu
energyCenters.meaning: Foundation, survival, grounding, stability; root of the central channel and resting place of the coiled serpent energy.

energyCenters.name: Svadhisthana (Sacral)
energyCenters.associations: 6 petals; color orange; element water (apas); bija VAM; crescent-moon symbol; deities Brahma and Rakini
energyCenters.meaning: Creativity, sexuality, emotion, fluidity and pleasure.

energyCenters.name: Manipura (Solar plexus)
energyCenters.associations: 10 petals; color yellow; element fire (agni); bija RAM; downward red triangle; deities Vishnu (or Rudra in some texts) and Lakini; seat of samana vayu and the digestive fire
energyCenters.meaning: Personal power, will, transformation, metabolism and the 'inner fire' that digests food and experience.

energyCenters.name: Anahata (Heart)
energyCenters.associations: 12 petals; color green (sometimes pink); element air (vayu); bija YAM; hexagram of two interlocking triangles; deities Rudra/Isha and Kakini; often given as the chief seat of prana vayu and hub of vyana vayu
energyCenters.meaning: Love, compassion, balance; the 'unstruck sound' (anahata nada) and the meeting of upward and downward energies.

energyCenters.name: Vishuddha (Throat)
energyCenters.associations: 16 petals; color blue/sky-blue; element ether/space (akasha); bija HAM; downward triangle with white circle; deities Sadashiva and Shakini; associated with udana vayu
energyCenters.meaning: Communication, expression, purification, truthful speech.

energyCenters.name: Ajna (Third eye)
energyCenters.associations: 2 petals; color indigo; element mind/light (manas, beyond the five gross elements); bija OM (AUM); deities Shiva/Sadashiva and Hakini; meeting point of ida, pingala and sushumna
energyCenters.meaning: Insight, intuition, perception, the 'command' center where the three main nadis converge.

energyCenters.name: Sahasrara (Crown)
energyCenters.associations: 1,000-petalled lotus; color violet/white; transcends the elements (pure consciousness); silent OM or beyond sound; abode of Shiva, union with Shakti
energyCenters.meaning: Pure consciousness and liberation; the goal-point of kundalini's ascent and union of individual and universal.

research.energyFlowModel:
Prana is believed to enter and circulate as five directional winds. PRANA vayu draws energy inward and upward, seated in the chest/head and governing inhalation and the heart-lung region. APANA vayu moves downward and outward, seated in the pelvis/lower abdomen, governing exhalation, elimination, reproduction and the downward 'grounding' pull. SAMANA vayu is the balancing, churning current at the navel that 'cooks' and assimilates (digestion, the inner fire). UDANA vayu rises from the heart/throat upward, governing speech, exhalation's upward lift, growth and the upward movement toward the head at death and in spiritual ascent. VYANA vayu pervades and distributes throughout the whole body, coordinating the other four and radiating prana outward to every part. A core practical aim is to reverse the natural tendency of prana to rise and apana to sink: by uniting prana and apana at the navel (often via bandhas during retention) the practitioner kindles inner heat that drives energy into the central channel.

This circulation runs through the nadis. Of the 72,000 channels, ida (terminating in the left nostril, lunar, cooling, parasympathetic-like, associated with the mind/emotion) and pingala (right nostril, solar, heating, sympathetic-like, associated with vitality/activity) spiral around the central sushumna, crossing at the chakras like two serpents around a staff. Normally one nostril/nadi dominates and alternates over the day. When breath flows evenly in both nostrils, ida and pingala are balanced and prana can enter sushumna. Practices such as Nadi Shodhana purify and balance these channels; retention with bandhas concentrates and redirects prana. As prana enters and rises in sushumna it is said to awaken kundalini at muladhara; this energy ascends, piercing the chakras and the three granthis (knots) — Brahma granthi (lower, at muladhara/the pull of physicality), Vishnu granthi (heart/middle, attachment) and Rudra granthi (ajna, ego/duality) — until it reaches sahasrara, producing states of absorption (samadhi) and, in the tradition's terms, liberation. Energy thus 'rises' through openings (chakras/granthis) and is hindered by 'blocks' (impure nadis, unpierced knots); it 'settles' and is calmed through cooling breaths and exhalation/apana, and is 'raised and concentrated' through heating breaths, retention and locks.
research.feltSenseAndTransitions:
Traditions describe pranayama in strongly somatic terms. In slow practices (Nadi Shodhana, Ujjayi) the breath should feel smooth, fine, elongated and 'soundless or softly sounding,' with no jerkiness; the body settles, the heartbeat steadies and attention turns inward — a sense of even, oceanic flow (Ujjayi's throat-constriction makes the breath audibly wavelike and felt at the back of the throat). Cooling breaths (Sheetali/Sitkari) are felt as literal coolness: air drawn over the wet, curled or clenched-teeth tongue produces evaporative coolness sensed in the mouth, throat and then as a spreading calm and lowering of internal 'heat' and agitation. Bhramari's humming is felt as vibration resonating in the skull, sinuses and 'third eye,' described as quieting mental chatter. Heating/forceful breaths (Bhastrika, Kapalabhati, Surya Bhedana) are felt as warmth, tingling, alertness and energization — a 'stoking' of the navel fire, sometimes lightheadedness if overdone.

Retention (kumbhaka) is where energetic transition is most emphasized: holding after inhalation (antara) is described as a building, expansive pressure and concentration of prana in the torso 'like a sealed pot,' while holding after exhalation (bahya), especially with uddiyana bandha, gives a hollow, drawn-up, vacuum-like sensation in the belly and an upward suction of energy. The bandhas are felt as deliberate seals — mula bandha as a subtle lift at the pelvic floor, uddiyana as the abdomen drawn back and up under the ribs, jalandhara as a chin-to-chest lock that 'caps' the throat — together giving the sensation that breath/energy is being prevented from leaking and instead pressed into the central axis. Advanced practitioners report the felt union of prana (rising) and apana (sinking) at the navel as inner heat, and the movement into sushumna as a rising, spreading or 'electric' current along the spine. Patanjali frames the fruit as the thinning of a 'veil over the inner light' and a mind made fit, steady and clear for concentration.

practices.name: Nadi Shodhana / Anulom Vilom (Alternate-nostril breathing)
practices.aliases: nāḍī śodhana ('channel purification'); anuloma viloma
practices.tradition: Hatha Yoga (Hatha Yoga Pradipika, Gheranda Samhita, Shiva Samhita); foundational/preparatory
practices.description: Alternate-nostril breathing that purifies and balances ida and pingala so prana can enter sushumna. Distinction often drawn: with retention = Nadi Shodhana; without retention = Anulom Vilom.
practices.howPracticed: Seated upright; use the right hand (often Vishnu/Nasagra mudra) to alternately close nostrils. Inhale left, close left and exhale right, inhale right, close right and exhale left = one round. Classical ratio inhale:retain:exhale = 1:4:2 (Gheranda Samhita: 12:48:24 counts under a guru). Beginners start 1:1:1, progress to 1:2:2, then 1:4:2.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Purify the nadis, calm and balance the nervous system and mind, harmonize solar/lunar energies, prepare for meditation and for prana entering sushumna.
practices.feltExperience: Even, fine, quiet breath; growing calm, mental clarity and balanced energy; sense of both nostrils flowing freely.
practices.safetyNotes: Build ratios slowly; retention only when comfortable; ease off with respiratory or cardiovascular conditions; ideally learned from a teacher.

practices.name: Ujjayi (Victorious / Ocean breath)
practices.aliases: ujjāyī ('victorious'); 'ocean breath,' psychic breath
practices.tradition: Hatha Yoga (one of the eight kumbhakas of the Hatha Yoga Pradipika)
practices.description: Breath made with a gentle constriction of the glottis so it produces a soft oceanic/snoring sound, through both nostrils.
practices.howPracticed: Slightly contract the throat (as if fogging a mirror with the mouth closed) on both inhalation and exhalation, breathing through the nose to create a steady wavelike sound; often combined with jalandhara bandha and retention.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Calms and focuses the mind, warms the body mildly, lengthens and smooths the breath, said to remove phlegm and increase digestive fire; used to sustain attention in asana and meditation.
practices.feltExperience: Audible, resonant breath felt at the back of the throat; soothing, steadying, oceanic rhythm.
practices.safetyNotes: Keep the constriction gentle; avoid strain or throat tension.

practices.name: Bhastrika (Bellows breath)
practices.aliases: bhastrikā ('bellows')
practices.tradition: Hatha Yoga (one of the eight kumbhakas)
practices.description: Rapid, forceful inhalation AND exhalation driven by the diaphragm, like a blacksmith's bellows, usually in rounds followed by deep inhalation and retention.
practices.howPracticed: Sit firmly; perform rapid, equal, forceful in- and out-breaths (e.g., ~10–20 per round, classically up to fast rates), then a deep inhalation held (antara kumbhaka), often with bandhas, and a slow exhalation; repeat in rounds.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Generates heat and vitality, said to balance the three doshas, clear the nadis, pierce the three granthis and 'quickly awaken kundalini.'
practices.feltExperience: Strong warmth, energization, vigorous abdominal pumping, heightened alertness; possible lightheadedness if overdone.
practices.safetyNotes: Vigorous and heating; not for pregnancy, high blood pressure, heart disease, hernia, epilepsy, glaucoma, or during menstruation; stop if dizzy. Best learned with a teacher.

practices.name: Kapalabhati (Skull-shining breath)
practices.aliases: kapālabhāti ('skull-shining'); a shatkarma/kriya, not strictly pranayama
practices.tradition: Hatha Yoga — classed among the six cleansing kriyas (shatkarma)
practices.description: Active, sharp exhalations driven by abdominal contraction with passive inhalation; a cleansing technique milder than Bhastrika.
practices.howPracticed: Sit upright; perform short, forceful exhalations by snapping the lower abdomen in, letting inhalation happen passively (~60–80 per minute typical); done in rounds, sometimes ending with retention on empty lungs.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Cleanses the respiratory tract, nasal passages and sinuses; said to clear phlegm and 'brighten the skull'/mind; energizing and purifying preparation for pranayama.
practices.feltExperience: Rhythmic abdominal pumping, clearing of the head, lightness and alertness.
practices.safetyNotes: Emphasis is on exhalation; same cautions as Bhastrika — avoid in pregnancy, hypertension, heart conditions, hernia, glaucoma, menstruation; stop if dizzy.

practices.name: Bhramari (Humming-bee breath)
practices.aliases: bhrāmarī (from bhramara, 'bee')
practices.tradition: Hatha Yoga (one of the eight kumbhakas)
practices.description: A breath in which the exhalation is made into a steady humming/buzzing sound like a bee.
practices.howPracticed: Inhale fully through the nose; on exhalation produce a low, continuous humming (often with eyes closed, sometimes ears closed with the fingers — shanmukhi mudra). Repeat for several minutes.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Soothes the nerves, calms the mind, reduces stress, anxiety and anger, aids sleep; the resonance is said to still mental agitation and lead toward inner sound/absorption.
practices.feltExperience: Vibration resonating in the head, sinuses and brow; deeply quieting and meditative.
practices.safetyNotes: Gentle and widely accessible; avoid forcing the sound; caution with acute ear infection.

practices.name: Sheetali (Cooling breath)
practices.aliases: śītalī ('cooling')
practices.tradition: Hatha Yoga (one of the eight kumbhakas)
practices.description: Cooling breath drawn in across a curled, tube-shaped tongue.
practices.howPracticed: Curl the tongue lengthwise into a tube protruding past the lips; inhale slowly through the tube with a soft hissing sound; close the mouth and exhale through the nose (often with jalandhara/retention).
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Cools the body, mind and emotions; said to reduce excess heat (pitta), thirst and agitation; calming.
practices.feltExperience: Distinct coolness over the tongue, throat and palate spreading into a calm, cooled sensation.
practices.safetyNotes: Avoid in cold weather, low blood pressure, respiratory congestion, asthma or excess mucus; not all can roll the tongue (use Sitkari).

practices.name: Sitkari (Hissing breath)
practices.aliases: sītkārī (from the 'sit' hissing sound)
practices.tradition: Hatha Yoga (one of the eight kumbhakas)
practices.description: A cooling breath drawn in through clenched teeth, for those who cannot curl the tongue.
practices.howPracticed: Lightly clench the teeth, part the lips, optionally fold the tongue tip back toward the soft palate; inhale through the teeth with a hissing 'sssss'; close the mouth and exhale through the nose.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Cools the body, lowers temperature, said to reduce blood pressure and soothe the nervous system; quenches heat and thirst.
practices.feltExperience: Cool air over teeth and tongue, a hissing sound, followed by calm and cooling.
practices.safetyNotes: Same cooling-breath cautions: avoid with low blood pressure, cold/congested conditions, sensitive teeth.

practices.name: Surya Bhedana (Sun-piercing / right-nostril breath)
practices.aliases: sūrya bhedana ('sun-piercing'); right-nostril breathing
practices.tradition: Hatha Yoga (one of the eight kumbhakas)
practices.description: Inhaling through the right (solar/pingala) nostril and exhaling through the left to generate heat and vitality.
practices.howPracticed: Close the left nostril; inhale through the right nostril; retain with bandhas (antara kumbhaka, often jalandhara); exhale through the left nostril. Repeat.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Heating and energizing; stimulates pingala/solar energy, increases vitality and alertness, counters cold, lethargy and dullness; said to 'destroy decay and death' (HYP).
practices.feltExperience: Rising warmth, alertness, increased energy.
practices.safetyNotes: Heating — avoid with hypertension, heart conditions, fever, excess pitta/heat; not in the evening if it disturbs sleep.

practices.name: Chandra Bhedana (Moon-piercing / left-nostril breath)
practices.aliases: candra bhedana ('moon-piercing'); left-nostril breathing
practices.tradition: Hatha Yoga (counterpart to Surya Bhedana; given in later texts)
practices.description: Inhaling through the left (lunar/ida) nostril and exhaling through the right for a cooling, calming effect.
practices.howPracticed: Close the right nostril; inhale through the left nostril; optional retention; exhale through the right nostril.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Cooling and calming; activates ida/lunar energy, promotes rest, relaxation and reduction of heat.
practices.feltExperience: Coolness, settling, relaxation.
practices.safetyNotes: Avoid if already cold/lethargic or congested; not ideal for low energy/depression where heating breath is preferred.

practices.name: Murccha (Swooning breath)
practices.aliases: mūrcchā ('fainting/swoon')
practices.tradition: Hatha Yoga (one of the eight kumbhakas) — advanced
practices.description: A retention practice with jalandhara bandha and gaze at the brow, inducing a 'swooning,' blissful, semi-fainting absorption.
practices.howPracticed: Inhale, apply jalandhara bandha, hold the breath with attention at the third eye, then exhale slowly; produces a light, euphoric, withdrawn state.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Withdrawal of mind (pratyahara) and blissful inner absorption; quieting the mind toward meditation.
practices.feltExperience: Light-headed, dreamy, blissful, swoon-like detachment from external awareness.
practices.safetyNotes: Advanced and potentially destabilizing; learn only under a qualified teacher; not for those prone to fainting, low blood pressure or seizures.

practices.name: Plavini (Floating breath)
practices.aliases: plāvinī ('that which floats')
practices.tradition: Hatha Yoga (one of the eight kumbhakas) — rare/advanced
practices.description: Filling the abdomen/belly with air so the body can reportedly 'float' on water; the most obscure of the eight.
practices.howPracticed: Swallow/draw air to fill the stomach and abdomen, retaining it; described in HYP as enabling the body to float like a lotus leaf.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Demonstrative/advanced control of prana; said to enable floating and sustain the body.
practices.feltExperience: Distended, buoyant abdomen.
practices.safetyNotes: Rare, esoteric, rarely taught; not a beginner practice.

practices.name: Bandhas (Energy locks): Mula, Uddiyana, Jalandhara, Maha Bandha
practices.aliases: mūla bandha (root lock), uḍḍīyāna bandha (upward/abdominal lock), jālandhara bandha (chin/throat lock); mahā bandha (the three together)
practices.tradition: Hatha Yoga (Hatha Yoga Pradipika ch. 3 and within ch. 2 kumbhaka practice)
practices.description: Muscular-energetic locks applied (especially during retention) to seal prana, prevent its 'leakage,' and redirect it into sushumna; HYP says pranayama without the locks is like a fire without a hearth.
practices.howPracticed: Mula bandha: contract/lift the perineum and pelvic floor to seal apana and turn it upward. Uddiyana bandha: after full exhalation, draw the abdomen back and up under the ribcage (lifts prana). Jalandhara bandha: drop the chin to the chest to lock the throat/seal the upper channel. Maha bandha applies all three simultaneously, typically on retention.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Unite prana and apana at the navel, kindle inner heat, pierce the granthis and drive kundalini up sushumna; conserve and concentrate energy.
practices.feltExperience: Mula = subtle pelvic lift; uddiyana = hollow upward suction in the belly; jalandhara = capped, sealed throat; together a sense of contained, upward-pressing energy.
practices.safetyNotes: Uddiyana and maha bandha done only on empty lungs (bahya kumbhaka) and an empty stomach; avoid in pregnancy, with hypertension, heart disease, ulcers, hernia or during menstruation; learn from a teacher.

practices.name: Kumbhaka (Breath retention)
practices.aliases: antara/antar kumbhaka (internal, after inhalation); bahya/bahir kumbhaka (external, after exhalation); kevala kumbhaka (spontaneous/automatic); 'sahita' (with breath manipulation)
practices.tradition: Hatha Yoga (HYP ch. 2, the central technique uniting the eight kumbhakas) and Patanjali (the 'third operation,' stambha-vritti, and the transcendent 'fourth')
practices.description: The retention phase that is the energetic core of classical pranayama; the eight named pranayamas are themselves called kumbhakas because retention is their defining act.
practices.howPracticed: Hold the breath after inhalation (antara) or after exhalation (bahya), usually with bandhas, for measured counts regulated by place (desha), time (kala) and number (sankhya); classical ratios place retention as the longest phase (e.g., 1:4:2).
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Concentrate and 'cook' prana, generate heat, still the breath and mind, and ultimately suspend breath spontaneously (kevala kumbhaka) leading toward samadhi; Patanjali: thins the veil over inner light and prepares the mind for dharana.
practices.feltExperience: Internal hold = building, expansive fullness 'like a sealed pot'; external hold = drawn-up vacuum/hollowness; deepening stillness and inward focus.
practices.safetyNotes: The most demanding element — increase gradually, never strain or gasp; avoid long retentions in pregnancy, hypertension, heart/respiratory disease, glaucoma; classically taught only under a guru.

visualMotifs: Subtle body / 'tantric anatomy' diagrams: an upright human figure (often seated in meditation) with the spinal axis marked by the central sushumna channel and the ida (left) and pingala (right) nadis spiraling around it like two intertwined serpents crossing at each chakra — visually echoing a caduceus/double-helix; viewed from above the crossings can resemble a yin-yang or swastika spiral.

Chakras drawn as lotuses/wheels along the spine, each with a fixed number of petals (4, 6, 10, 12, 16, 2, 1000) inscribed with Sanskrit (Devanagari) bija syllables and seed-mantras (LAM, VAM, RAM, YAM, HAM, OM).

Geometric yantras inside each lotus: yellow square (earth/muladhara), crescent moon (water/svadhisthana), downward red triangle (fire/manipura), hexagram/six-pointed star of interlocking triangles (air/anahata), circle/space (ether/vishuddha), downward triangle (ajna).

The popular rainbow chakra color scheme — red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet from root to crown — though this spectrum mapping is largely a modern (often Western) standardization rather than uniform classical doctrine (classical texts give different colors, e.g., smoky/whitish or varied hues).

Kundalini as a coiled serpent (often three-and-a-half coils) sleeping at the base of the spine in muladhara, then rising as an awakened snake or column of light/energy up sushumna to a radiant thousand-petalled lotus at the crown.
Deities seated within the chakra lotuses (e.g., Ganesha at muladhara, Shiva and Shakti at the crown), and the union of Shiva (consciousness) and Shakti (energy) at sahasrara.
The thousand-petalled lotus (sahasrara) blazing at or above the crown, often radiating light, symbolizing pure consciousness and liberation.

In meditation/visualization practice: breath and prana imagined as light or luminous current flowing up and down the channels, warmth/fire at the navel, and coolness (lunar light, nectar/amrita) descending from the crown.

Traditional and contemporary devotional art (Tibetan/Newar thangka-style and Indian tantric paintings, e.g., works popularized via artists like Robert Beer) depicting the chakra-nadi system in stylized, color-coded form.
research.attributionNotes: These practices are part of living Hindu (and overlapping Tantric, Nath, and—via related lineages—Buddhist and Sikh) religious and contemplative traditions, not generic wellness techniques. Key sensitivities: (1) Prana, the chakras, the nadis, kundalini and pranayama are framed within a soteriology aimed at self-realization/liberation (moksha), not merely stress relief; reducing pranayama to secular 'breathwork' or chakras to a mood/color chart strips their religious meaning, and traditions and scholars note this as a common form of decontextualization. (2) The system was classically transmitted from guru to student, often with initiation; texts repeatedly insist advanced kumbhaka and bandha practice (and kundalini work) be undertaken under a qualified teacher 'under the guidance of a guru,' both for safety and as a matter of sacred transmission. (3) Sanskrit terms and transliterations should be rendered carefully (e.g., prāṇa, prāṇāyāma, suṣumnā, kuṇḍalinī, kumbhaka, bandha); diacritics and spellings vary across lineages and Romanization schemes. (4) Sources genuinely disagree and much is contested: the standardized rainbow chakra colors, fixed element/vayu/chakra correspondences, exact breath ratios and counts, and even the number/identity of the eight kumbhakas vary between classical texts (Hatha Yoga Pradipika vs. Gheranda Samhita vs. Shiva Samhita) and especially between classical sources and 20th-century systematizations — present these as tradition-specific beliefs, noting where they are modern syntheses. (5) Mantras and some named systems carry distinct lineage ownership — e.g., much of modern 'Kundalini Yoga' as taught in the West (Yogi Bhajan / 3HO) draws mantras from Sikh scripture, and Sikh and Indian voices have raised both appropriation and abuse-of-power concerns; such material should not be presented as generic or detached from its community. (6) Present all energetic and physiological claims as the tradition's beliefs and felt-experience models, explicitly NOT as biomedical fact, and include safety cautions (pregnancy, hypertension, heart/respiratory conditions, glaucoma, menstruation, psychiatric vulnerability) honestly while respecting the practices' spiritual seriousness. (7) Attribute to the tradition and, where relevant, to specific texts and lineages rather than to individual modern brands.
