research.topic: Yogic Subtle Body: Nadis, Chakras, and Kundalini
research.summary:
The yogic "subtle body" (sukshma sharira) is a non-physical map of the human being central to Hindu and Buddhist Tantra and to Hatha Yoga. It posits that life-force (prana) circulates through a network of subtle channels (nadis), gathered and transformed at energy hubs (chakras), and that a latent spiritual power (kundalini) coiled at the base of the spine can be awakened to rise through the central channel (sushumna) to the crown, producing liberation (moksha/kaivalya). This is explicitly a contemplative-visionary and devotional model, not a claim about gross physical anatomy; classical authors themselves locate these structures in the "subtle" body experienced in meditation, not in dissection.

Historically the system is not a single fixed scheme. Scholar David Gordon White notes that "hierarchies of inner energy centers" appear from roughly the 8th century CE in Buddhist tantric texts (Hevajra Tantra, Caryagiti), and that early systems used four or five centers. The familiar "six chakras + sahasrara" arrangement crystallized in later Hindu Tantra (e.g., the Kubjikamata-tantra, c. 10th–11th c.), and the single most influential description is the Sat-Cakra-Nirupana, composed in 1577 (often cited as 1526) by Purnananda as part of a larger work, the Sri-Tattva-Cintamani. This text reached the West chiefly through Sir John Woodroffe's ("Arthur Avalon") 1918/1919 translation The Serpent Power. Crucially, the classical sources differ among themselves on petal counts, colors, deities, and even the number of chakras; uniformity is a modern, not classical, feature.

The model's practical core is pranayama (breath regulation), mantra, bandha (locks), mudra, and visualization (dhyana) aimed at balancing the lunar Ida and solar Pingala channels so prana can enter the dormant central Sushumna, awaken kundalini, and pierce the chakras and three "knots" (granthis). The felt experience is described in heat, tingling, vibration, spontaneous movement (kriyas), and bliss; traditions warn that forced or premature awakening is genuinely dangerous and must be guided.

A critical distinction for respectful presentation: the modern Western "rainbow" seven-chakra system — red root through violet crown, each with a tidy psychological theme — is NOT the classical tantric system. It was assembled in the 20th century: C.W. Leadbeater's Theosophical book The Chakras (1927) introduced clairvoyant color schemes; the seven-rainbow-color mapping plus the psychological qualities were fused only around 1977 (via Ken Dychtwald's Bodymind / Yoga Journal and later popularized by Christopher Hills and Anodea Judith's Wheels of Life, 1987). Kurt Leland documents that the "ancient" Western system is barely a few decades old. Classical chakras instead carry element-derived colors (e.g., earth/yellow square, fire/red triangle), Sanskrit-lettered petals, seed-syllables, and presiding Hindu deities, and are aimed at transcendence/liberation rather than emotional "balancing."
research.historyAndOrigin:
Origins lie in Indian Tantra (esoteric ritual-yogic traditions) of both Hindu and Buddhist forms. David Gordon White (cited in the Wikipedia Chakra article) dates the introduction of "hierarchies of inner energy centers" to about the 8th century CE in Buddhist texts such as the Hevajra Tantra and the Caryagiti, where systems of four or five centers appear. Earlier conceptual roots (nadis, prana, the channels) trace to Upanishadic and Vedic-era ideas (e.g., the heart with its channels in the Upanishads), but the chakra-as-lotus system is medieval tantric.

Key texts and dates: Kubjikamata-tantra (c. 10th–11th century), a Kaula/Shakta text often credited with consolidating a six-plus-one chakra arrangement and feminine deity associations; the Sat-Cakra-Nirupana ("Description/Investigation of the Six Centers"), composed in 1577 CE (some sources say 1526) by Purnananda Yati (a Bengali Brahmana of Kasyapa gotra) as the sixth chapter of his Sri-Tattva-Cintamani; and the companion Paduka-Pancaka. The Hatha-Yoga-Pradipika (15th c.), Goraksha Samhita/Shataka, Shiva Samhita, and Gheranda Samhita are central Hatha sources for nadis (the 72,000 figure, Ida/Pingala/Sushumna) and kundalini.

The system entered the English-speaking world through Sir John Woodroffe (pen name Arthur Avalon), a British judge in Calcutta, whose The Serpent Power (1918/1919) translated the Sat-Cakra-Nirupana and Paduka-Pancaka with commentary. Theosophy (H.P. Blavatsky, and especially C.W. Leadbeater's The Chakras, 1927) then reinterpreted the system for a Western occult audience, beginning the divergence into the modern Western model.

Lineages: the chakra/kundalini complex is shared (with variation) across Shaiva and Shakta Tantra, Nath/Hatha yogis (Gorakhnath/Matsyendranath lineage), Sri Vidya, and parallels in Buddhist Vajrayana (channels rtsa, winds rlung, drops bindu/thig-le; centers often four, with tummo/candali as the inner-heat analogue of kundalini). It is contested whether Hindu and Buddhist systems are genetically one tradition or convergent siblings.
research.energyFlowModel:
The body is permeated by prana (vital force/breath) flowing through nadis ("channels/rivers," from the root nad, to flow). Classical Hatha texts (Hatha-Yoga-Pradipika, Goraksha Samhita, Shiva Samhita) say there are 72,000 nadis radiating from a bulb-like root, the kanda, near the base of the spine/navel. Three are primary: Ida (left, lunar/chandra, cooling, "feminine," terminating at the left nostril), Pingala (right, solar/surya, heating, "masculine," right nostril), and Sushumna (central, running within/along the spinal axis from Muladhara at the base to Sahasrara at the crown). Ida and Pingala are commonly described as spiraling/crisscrossing around Sushumna at the chakra points (a caduceus-like image), beginning at Muladhara.

In ordinary life prana flows predominantly in Ida and Pingala, and breath alternates dominance between the nostrils (the "swara" cycle). The yogic aim is to balance and quiet Ida and Pingala — classically by nadi shodhana (channel-purifying alternate-nostril breathing), retention (kumbhaka), bandhas (locks: mula, uddiyana, jalandhara), and mantra — so that prana withdraws from the side channels and enters the normally closed Sushumna (whose inner subtle channels are named Vajra, Chitra/Chitrini, and the finest Brahma-nadi).

When Sushumna opens, the dormant kundalini-shakti (pictured as a serpent coiled three-and-a-half times around the svayambhu-linga at Muladhara) is roused, straightens, and ascends Sushumna. As it rises it "pierces" each chakra in turn (the process called shat-chakra-bheda), dissolving each lower element into the next (earth into water, water into fire, etc.), and must break through three granthis (knots) — Brahma (lower), Vishnu (middle/heart region), and Rudra (upper/ajna) — which are points where prana is bound and ascent is blocked. Reaching Sahasrara, Shakti unites with Shiva (pure consciousness), nectar (amrita) is said to flow, and the practitioner attains samadhi/liberation. Afterward kundalini is brought back down and "settled." Openings, blocks, and the directional flow (rising on awakening, settling after) are thus central; an un-purified channel or a forced rising is held to cause disorder.
research.feltSenseAndTransitions:
Practitioners are told to FEEL prana as subtle movement, warmth, or current along the channels rather than as ordinary muscular sensation. In nadi shodhana the emphasis is on a smooth, silent, even breath and, when done correctly, a calming, cooling, balancing sensation; one is taught to "notice" airflow and a sense of clearing in the left vs. right channel. Concentration on a chakra is described as awareness gathering at a precise bodily point (e.g., the perineum/base for Muladhara, the navel for Manipura, the heart for Anahata, the throat pit for Vishuddha, the point between the eyebrows for Ajna).

Kundalini awakening is described experientially as: intense heat or a burning/warm current beginning at the base of the spine and moving upward; tingling, prickling, or "electric" sensations along the spine; vibrations and waves of energy; spontaneous involuntary movements, jerks, or postures (kriyas); altered/automatic breathing; pressure or sensation at the crown and brow; inner light, sound (nada), and bliss/ecstasy. Transitions are framed as the energy "rising" through stages and chakras and then needing to be grounded/"settled" back down; the heart-opening (Anahata) and brow (Ajna) stages are emphasized as turning points (the latter being where, per the Sat-Chakra-Nirupana tradition, real attainment begins).

Because the experience can be overwhelming, traditions stress gradualness, purification, and a teacher's guidance. Reported difficulties include heat, shaking, palpitations, numbness, sleep disruption, anxiety/fear arising "for no reason," and emotional release — a cluster sometimes called (in modern usage) "kundalini syndrome" or, in clinical literature, associated with "spiritual emergency." These are presented as belief/experience reports, not medical claims.

energyCenters.name: Muladhara (root)
energyCenters.location: Base of the spine / perineum (region of the anus-genital base)
energyCenters.meaning: Root support; seat of dormant kundalini coiled around the svayambhu-linga; foundation of the subtle body; associated with survival/grounding in modern readings.
energyCenters.associations: Element: Earth (prithvi). Geometry: yellow square within the lotus; downward triangle containing kundalini. Petals: 4 (carrying Sanskrit letters vam, sham, shham, sam). Petal color: classically crimson/deep red. Bija (seed) mantra: LAM. Deity (devata): Brahma (child form); Shakti: Dakini. Animal vehicle (vahana): elephant (Airavata, often 7-trunked). Modern Western color: red.

energyCenters.name: Svadhisthana (sacral)
energyCenters.location: Root of the genitals / lower abdomen, a few finger-widths above Muladhara
energyCenters.meaning: "One's own abode"; seat of subconscious tendencies, fluidity, desire/creativity; water principle.
energyCenters.associations: Element: Water (apas). Geometry: white crescent moon. Petals: 6 (letters bam, bham, mam, yam, ram, lam). Petal color: classically vermilion/orange-red. Bija: VAM. Deity: Vishnu; Shakti: Rakini. Vehicle: makara (crocodile/sea-creature). Modern Western color: orange.

energyCenters.name: Manipura (navel/solar plexus)
energyCenters.location: At the navel / solar plexus along the spine
energyCenters.meaning: "City of jewels"; center of fire, digestion (agni), willpower, vitality and transformation.
energyCenters.associations: Element: Fire (tejas/agni). Geometry: red downward-pointing triangle (often with swastika/T-shaped fire marks). Petals: 10 (letters dam through pham). Petal color: classically blue-tinged / dark like rainclouds (texts vary). Bija: RAM. Deity: Rudra (old, ash-smeared); Shakti: Lakini. Vehicle: ram (mesha). Modern Western color: yellow.

energyCenters.name: Anahata (heart)
energyCenters.location: Center of the chest, at the heart
energyCenters.meaning: "Unstruck" (the unstruck sound/anahata-nada); seat of feeling, compassion, love; site of Vishnu-granthi and of an inner lotus (Ananda-kanda) with a wish-fulfilling tree.
energyCenters.associations: Element: Air (vayu). Geometry: hexagram / two interlocking triangles (shatkona). Petals: 12 (letters kam through tham). Petal color: classically vermilion/deep red or golden (sources differ: Garuda Purana golden, Shiva Samhita deep red, Sat-Chakra-Nirupana vermilion). Bija: YAM. Deity: Isha/Ishana; Shakti: Kakini. Vehicle: black antelope/gazelle. Modern Western color: green.

energyCenters.name: Vishuddha (throat)
energyCenters.location: Throat / pit of the throat along the spine
energyCenters.meaning: "Especially pure"; center of purification, sound, speech, ether/space; where amrita (nectar) is purified.
energyCenters.associations: Element: Ether/Space (akasha). Geometry: white circle/full moon within a downward triangle. Petals: 16 (the 16 Sanskrit vowels). Petal color: classically smoky purple / smoke-colored. Bija: HAM. Deity: Sadashiva (often Ardhanarishvara half-male/half-female); Shakti: Shakini. Vehicle: white elephant. Modern Western color: blue.

energyCenters.name: Ajna (brow / third eye)
energyCenters.meaning: "Command/authority"; seat of intuition, the guru's command, mind (manas); where Ida, Pingala and Sushumna are said to meet; site of Rudra-granthi; contains the bija OM and the itara-linga.
energyCenters.associations: Element: beyond the gross elements (mind/manas tattva); sometimes "light." Geometry: white downward triangle; OM symbol. Petals: 2 (letters ham and ksham) per the Sat-Chakra-Nirupana — note the popular "96 petals" figure is a later/derived count and is contested. Petal color: white/moon-white. Bija: OM (AUM). Deity: Shambhu/Parama-Shiva (Hakini Shakti is the presiding Shakti). Modern Western color: indigo.

energyCenters.name: Sahasrara (crown)
energyCenters.location: Crown of the head / above the head (the "thousand-petalled lotus")
energyCenters.meaning: Not a chakra in the same sense as the six; the seat of supreme Shiva/consciousness and the goal of kundalini's ascent, where Shakti unites with Shiva and liberation occurs.
energyCenters.associations: Element: beyond elements (pure consciousness/transcendent). Geometry: thousand-petalled lotus (often described as containing all 50 Sanskrit letters repeated 20 times). Color: classically described as luminous white "whiter than the full moon," sometimes radiant/multicolored or golden bindu. Bija: silence / the transcendent (sometimes Aum or visarga). Deity: Parama-Shiva united with Shakti; the bindu/Supreme. Modern Western color: violet or white.

energyCenters.name: Minor / additional centers (selected, mainly Bihar School of Yoga & various tantras)
energyCenters.location: Various: chest, head, palate, back of head, hands, feet
energyCenters.meaning: Numerous secondary chakras and kshetrams exist; not standardized across schools. Examples below.
energyCenters.associations: Hrit (Hridaya/Surya) chakra: just below Anahata, an 8-petalled "wish-fulfilling" lotus (kalpataru), often linked to the sun and devotional visualization. Lalana/Talu (Talana): at the soft palate/uvula, catches the amrita nectar dripping from Bindu. Bindu Visarga: at the back/top of the head (the brahmin's tuft point), source/store of amrita, lunar nectar. Manas chakra: 6-petalled, on the forehead, seat of sense-mind. Soma chakra: above Ajna, lunar nectar center. Other texts add chakras in the palms, soles, and along the throat. These vary widely and should not be presented as a fixed canon.

coreConcepts.meaning: Subtle channel through which prana flows; classically 72,000 in number, radiating from the kanda. Not the physical nerves or vessels, though sometimes loosely correlated by modern writers.
Left channel; cooling, lunar, mental/parasympathetic-associated, "feminine"; ends at the left nostril.
Right channel; heating, solar, vital/sympathetic-associated, "masculine"; ends at the right nostril.

coreConcepts.aliases: suṣumṇā; central nadi; (inner channels Vajra, Chitra/Chitrini, Brahma-nadi)
coreConcepts.meaning: Central channel along the spinal axis from Muladhara to Sahasrara; normally closed; the path of kundalini's ascent.

coreConcepts.aliases: prāṇa; vital air/life-force; the five vayus (prana, apana, samana, udana, vyana)
coreConcepts.meaning: Life-energy carried by the breath and circulating in the nadis; its control is pranayama.
Subtle energy center/hub where nadis converge, visualized as a lotus with a set number of petals, a seed-syllable, element, deity and form.
Latent divine feminine energy (Shakti) coiled at Muladhara; awakened, it rises through Sushumna to unite with Shiva. Buddhist analogue: candali / tummo (gtum mo, "fierce one").

Three psychic knots/blocks along Sushumna that bind prana and must be pierced for kundalini to ascend: Brahma (lower, attachment to material/desire), Vishnu (heart region, attachment to action/emotion), Rudra (Ajna, attachment to thought/psychic powers/ego).
Single-syllable sound vibration assigned to a chakra/element: LAM, VAM, RAM, YAM, HAM (and OM for Ajna); recited/visualized to activate the center.
The yogic process by which kundalini pierces each of the six centers in ascent.
Three subtle "marks"/lingas within Sushumna — at Muladhara (svayambhu, around which kundalini coils), Anahata (bana), and Ajna (itara) — encountered as kundalini rises.

coreConcepts.aliases: mula bandha, uddiyana bandha, jalandhara bandha; khechari, shambhavi, etc.
coreConcepts.meaning: Energetic "locks" and seals used to contain and direct prana into Sushumna during practice.
Subtle nectar said to drip from a center in the head (Bindu Visarga), normally consumed by digestive fire; advanced practices aim to preserve/redirect it.
The non-physical body of nadis, chakras and prana in which these phenomena occur — distinct from the gross physical body (sthula sharira).

practices.name: Nadi Shodhana (alternate-nostril breathing)
practices.aliases: nāḍī śodhana; anuloma viloma (related); "channel purification"
practices.tradition: Hatha Yoga / classical pranayama (Hatha-Yoga-Pradipika, Gheranda Samhita lineage)
practices.description: Foundational breath practice to purify and balance Ida and Pingala so prana can enter Sushumna; preparatory to kundalini work.
practices.howPracticed: Seated upright (sukhasana/siddhasana/padmasana), spine erect. Using Vishnu mudra (right thumb on right nostril, ring/little fingers on left), close one nostril and inhale through the other, then close that and exhale through the opposite, alternating. Often without retention for beginners; advanced practice adds kumbhaka (retention) with bandhas. Classical ratios such as inhale:retain:exhale = 1:4:2 are taught (e.g., 4:16:8 counts), but beginners typically use equal or no-retention breathing. Breath is slow, smooth, silent.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Cleanse the nadis, balance lunar/solar energies, calm and focus the mind, and prepare the channels for prana to rise in Sushumna.
practices.feltExperience: A cooling, settling, balancing sensation; awareness of subtle airflow and of left vs. right channel; mental quiet and clarity.
practices.safetyNotes: Practiced without breath retention for those with high blood pressure, heart conditions, or in pregnancy. Avoid straining the breath; learn retention only under guidance. Presented as a contemplative practice, not a medical therapy.

practices.name: Chakra dhyana / visualization with bija mantra
practices.aliases: cakra dhyāna; laya yoga concentration; nyasa
practices.tradition: Tantra / Laya Yoga (Sat-Chakra-Nirupana tradition)
practices.description: Meditative concentration on each chakra in turn, visualizing its lotus, color, geometric form, seed-syllable and deity, and intoning its bija mantra.
practices.howPracticed: Fix awareness at the chakra's bodily point (e.g., base, navel, heart, throat, brow); visualize the lotus with its petals, central element-form and bija letter; mentally or audibly chant the seed mantra (LAM at root, VAM, RAM, YAM, HAM, OM at brow); may be combined with breath and with leading awareness up and down the spine.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Awaken and 'purify' each center, gather prana, and progressively raise awareness toward Sahasrara; ultimately the rising of kundalini.
practices.feltExperience: Sensation/warmth/pulsation localized at the point of concentration; inner color or light; resonance of the chanted syllable; deepening absorption (dharana into dhyana).
practices.safetyNotes: Traditionally taught only after purification and under a guru; over-forcing concentration (especially at Ajna) is cautioned against.

practices.name: Kundalini awakening practices (kriya / kundalini yoga, shat-chakra-bheda)
practices.aliases: kuṇḍalinī yoga; laya yoga; kriya yoga (tantric sense)
practices.tradition: Hatha/Tantra; modernized lineages (e.g., Bihar School of Yoga; Sivananda; Yogi Bhajan's Kundalini Yoga is a distinct 20th-c. lineage)
practices.description: Integrated regimen — asana, pranayama with kumbhaka, the three bandhas, mudras (e.g., maha mudra, khechari, shambhavi), mantra and visualization — intended to rouse kundalini and guide it up Sushumna through the chakras and granthis to Sahasrara.
practices.howPracticed: Build a stable seat and purified channels first; use retention plus mula/uddiyana/jalandhara bandhas to pressurize and direct apana upward to meet prana, igniting the inner fire at Muladhara; lead the awakened energy up the spine with breath, mantra and visualization, piercing granthis (Brahma, Vishnu, Rudra) in sequence; then settle the energy down afterward.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Union of Shakti with Shiva in Sahasrara; samadhi, expanded consciousness, and ultimately liberation (moksha/kaivalya).
practices.feltExperience: Heat/inner fire at the base rising up the spine; tingling, vibration, electric currents; spontaneous movements/postures (kriyas); inner sound (nada) and light; pressure at crown/brow; waves of bliss and, at times, fear or overwhelm.
practices.safetyNotes: Strongly emphasized: requires preparation, ethical/lifestyle foundation, and an experienced teacher. Forced or premature awakening is said to cause physical and psychological disturbance ('kundalini syndrome' in modern usage; overlaps with 'spiritual emergency' in clinical literature) — heat, palpitations, insomnia, anxiety, destabilization. Not advised to brute-force; contraindicated for the unprepared and for those with certain mental-health vulnerabilities. This is belief/experience, not medical fact.

practices.name: Bandhas and Mudras (locks and seals)
practices.aliases: mūla bandha, uḍḍiyāna bandha, jālandhara bandha; khecarī, śāmbhavī, mahā mudrā
practices.tradition: Hatha Yoga
practices.description: Physical-energetic techniques that seal off and redirect prana toward Sushumna; integral to kundalini practice rather than standalone.
practices.howPracticed: Mula bandha: contraction of the perineum/pelvic floor (root lock). Uddiyana bandha: abdominal lift after exhalation. Jalandhara bandha: chin lock compressing the throat. Khechari mudra: turning the tongue back/up toward the soft palate to access nectar. Shambhavi mudra: gaze drawn to the eyebrow center. Often applied together during retention.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Contain prana/apana, unite them, and force energy into the central channel; preserve amrita (nectar).
practices.feltExperience: Internal pressure, lift, and concentration of energy; a sense of energy being 'held' and rising.
practices.safetyNotes: Learn from a teacher; avoid with heart, blood-pressure, eye, or abdominal conditions and during pregnancy/menstruation as advised by the lineage.

practices.name: Trataka and inner-sound (nada) practices (supportive)
practices.aliases: trāṭaka (steady gazing); nāda anusandhāna (tracing inner sound)
practices.tradition: Hatha/Tantra
practices.description: Concentration aids that steady the mind and turn attention inward to subtle light and sound, supporting chakra/kundalini work.
practices.howPracticed: Trataka: unblinking gaze at a point/flame, then internalizing the after-image at the brow. Nada: listening for subtle inner sounds with ears closed (e.g., in shanmukhi mudra).
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Develop one-pointedness; awaken Ajna; perceive the anahata-nada (unstruck sound).
practices.feltExperience: Inner light at the brow; progressively subtle inner sounds; deep stillness.
practices.safetyNotes: Limit gazing duration to avoid eye strain; not for certain eye conditions.

visualMotifs: Lotus (padma/kamala): each chakra is a lotus whose petal-count is fixed (4, 6, 10, 12, 16, 2 ... 1000) and whose petals bear Sanskrit letters (the 50 varnamala distributed across the centers).

Element-geometry inside the lotus: yellow square (earth, Muladhara), white crescent moon (water, Svadhisthana), red downward triangle (fire, Manipura), hexagram/two interlaced triangles (air, Anahata), white circle/full moon (ether, Vishuddha), white downward triangle with OM (Ajna).
The coiled serpent: kundalini depicted as a snake wound three-and-a-half times around the svayambhu-linga at the base, asleep, then straightening and rising up the central axis.
The triple channel/caduceus image: Sushumna as a straight central staff with Ida and Pingala spiraling around it, crossing at the chakras — visually echoing a caduceus.
Bija-syllable lettering: the seed mantras (LAM, VAM, RAM, YAM, HAM, OM) written inside the central form, and petal-letters around the rim.

Presiding deities and their Shaktis/animals shown within or beside the lotus (Brahma/Dakini/elephant; Vishnu/Rakini/makara; Rudra/Lakini/ram; Isha/Kakini/antelope; Sadashiva/Shakini/elephant; Shambhu-Hakini at Ajna; Parama-Shiva at the crown).

Subtle-body cosmological charts: classical Indian (Rajput/Pahari/Tanjore) and Nepali paintings and manuscripts showing a seated/standing yogic figure with the spine as Mount Meru, the chakras as lotuses, and Ida/Pingala as sun/moon lines; Sri Yantra and other yantras as geometric meditation diagrams.

Vajrayana parallels: thangkas and mandalas (e.g., Kalachakra, Chakrasamvara) depicting channels (rtsa), winds, and seed-syllables on lotuses ringed by fire — a related but distinct Buddhist visual tradition.
Sahasrara as a radiant thousand-petalled lotus / luminous moon-white or golden bindu above the head, the seat of light and union.
MODERN motif (to label as modern): the rainbow column of seven colored discs (red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet) up the body — a 20th-century Western visualization, not classical.
research.attributionNotes:
This material belongs to living, sacred Hindu and Buddhist tantric traditions and should be presented respectfully as belief and contemplative experience, not as scientific or medical fact, and not as the app's own teaching. Key sensitivity points:

1) Do not flatten the diversity. There is no single "the" chakra system. Classical sources disagree on the number of chakras (four, five, six-plus-one, more), and on petal counts, colors, deities, and even the existence/placement of minor chakras. Presenting one tidy canonical chart as "ancient truth" misrepresents the tradition. Where sources conflict (e.g., Anahata's color: golden vs. deep red vs. vermilion; Ajna's petals: 2 in the Sat-Chakra-Nirupana vs. the popular "96"), say it is contested.

2) Clearly distinguish classical tantric sources from the modern Western model. The familiar red-to-violet "rainbow" chakras, each tagged with a single psychological theme (security, sexuality, power, love, communication, intuition, spirituality), are a 20th-century Western synthesis — Leadbeater's Theosophical The Chakras (1927) introduced clairvoyant color schemes; the rainbow-color-plus-qualities fusion appeared only around 1977 (Dychtwald; Yoga Journal), popularized by Christopher Hills and by Anodea Judith (Wheels of Life, 1987). Kurt Leland (Theosophical Society) documents this. Classical chakras instead use element-derived colors, lettered petals, seed-syllables and Hindu deities, and are oriented toward transcendence/liberation, not emotional "balancing." Conflating the two as both "ancient Indian" is a common appropriation error to avoid.

3) Transliteration and naming: use accurate Sanskrit (Muladhara, Svadhisthana, Manipura, Anahata, Vishuddha, Ajna, Sahasrara; Ida, Pingala, Sushumna; kundalini; granthi) and acknowledge IAST diacritics. The deities (Brahma, Vishnu, Rudra/Shiva, etc.) are objects of devotion, not mere decorations.

4) Kundalini is regarded as powerful and potentially dangerous. Traditions insist it be approached with preparation and under a qualified teacher (guru). The app should not present forced kundalini techniques as casual self-help, and should include the traditions' own safety cautions while being clear these are belief-based, not clinical guidance. If users report distressing "kundalini" experiences, that maps to "spiritual emergency" in some clinical literature and warrants professional support.

5) "Kundalini Yoga" as taught by Yogi Bhajan / 3HO is a specific modern lineage and brand, distinct from classical tantric kundalini; don't conflate. Likewise Buddhist Vajrayana channel/wind/drop practices (tummo) are a separate, initiation-bound tradition that should not be merged into the Hindu chakra chart.

6) Avoid commercializing or trivializing sacred symbols (deities, yantras, seed-mantras). Attribute to tradition and, where possible, to specific texts (Sat-Cakra-Nirupana, Hatha-Yoga-Pradipika) and reputable scholarship (David Gordon White, Georg Feuerstein, Gavin Flood, Kurt Leland).

sources.title: Chakra — Wikipedia (citing David Gordon White; historical dates, texts, classical vs. modern)
The Rainbow Body: How the Western Chakra System Came to Be — Kurt Leland, The Theosophical Society in America (Quest)
The Chakras — C. W. Leadbeater (1927), Theosophy World resource
Sat-Chakra-Nirupana (Purnananda; via The Serpent Power tradition) — chakra-by-chakra descriptions
The Serpent Power: Being the Sat-Cakra-Nirupana and Paduka-Pancaka — Sir John Woodroffe (Arthur Avalon)
The Three Fundamental Nadis — Ida, Pingala and Sushumna (Isha/Sadhguru); 72,000 nadis from three primary channels
What Are Nadis? The Three Main Nadis (Fitsri Yoga); Goraksha Samhita / Hatha Yoga Pradipika on 72,000 nadis and the kanda
The Three Granthis in Yoga: Brahma, Vishnu, and Rudra Knots Explained (Prana Sutra)
Three Granthis: Brahma, Vishnu & Rudra Knots Explained (Yogajala) — locations and bondage-of-desire/action/thought
Nadi Shodhana Pranayama — steps, ratios, felt effect, safety (Fitsri Yoga)
Kundalini Awakening — symptoms and safety (Fitsri Yoga)
The Real History of Chakra Colours: Why the Rainbow System Is Only ~50 Years Old (Divine Warrior) — corroborates Leland/Hills/1977
Moon Rhythms: The Bindu Chakra / Minor chakras (Bihar School lineage material)
Surya (sun) and minor chakras overview (TheLoneRider) — Hrit/Surya, Lalana/Talu, Manas, Soma

research.topic: Chinese Taoist (Daoist) Qi, the Three Dantian, Meridians, and Qigong / Neigong Energy Cultivation
research.summary:
Daoist energy cultivation rests on the concept of **qi (氣; chi; Japanese ki)** — the vital breath/energy that constitutes and animates all things and is understood as the dynamic manifestation of the Dao. Qi predates organized Daoism in early Chinese cosmology; its written character pairs "rice" (米) with "vapor/steam" (气), evoking nourishing breath, and it is expressed through the polarity of yin and yang and the Five Phases (wuxing). In the human body, classical theory describes three foundational energies, the **Three Treasures (sanbao 三寶): jing (精, essence), qi (氣, breath/energy), and shen (神, spirit/consciousness)**, stored respectively in the lower, middle, and upper **dantian (丹田, "cinnabar fields")**.

The body is threaded by a network of channels (jingluo 經絡, "meridians"): twelve primary organ-paired meridians (likened to rivers) and eight "extraordinary vessels" (qijing bamai, likened to reservoirs of source qi). For Daoist internal practice the two most important are the **Du Mai (督脈, Governing Vessel)** running up the spine (yang) and the **Ren Mai (任脈, Conception Vessel)** running down the front midline (yin). Joining these into a continuous loop is the **Microcosmic Orbit / Small Heavenly Circulation (xiao zhou tian 小周天)** — refined qi is led up the Du and down the Ren, with the tongue touching the palate as the "magpie bridge" to close the circuit. Extending circulation into the limbs and remaining vessels is the **Macrocosmic Orbit / Great Heavenly Circulation (da zhou tian 大周天)**.

These ideas drive **qigong (氣功, "energy work")** and the more inward, advanced **neigong (內功, "internal cultivation")**, and culminate in **neidan (內丹, internal alchemy)** — a Song-era systematization treating the body as a cauldron in which one "refines essence into breath, breath into spirit, and spirit back into Emptiness," gestating an "immortal embryo." Allied breath practices include **embryonic/fetal breathing (taixi 胎息)** — breathing as subtly as a fetus, eventually "without nose or mouth," centered at the navel/lower dantian — and the paired methods of **natural (Buddhist) abdominal breathing** versus **reverse (Daoist/pre-birth) abdominal breathing** used to accumulate qi.

The tradition visualizes the inner body as a sacred landscape, most famously in the **Neijing tu (內經圖, "Chart of the Inner Landscape/Warp")**, a Qing-dynasty stele at Beijing's White Cloud Abbey depicting the spine as a mountain range, the dantian as fields tended by an Ox/Buffalo and a Cowherd and Weaving Maiden, and qi circulation as waterwheels and rivers. This is a living religious and medical tradition with deep lineage roots; it should be presented as belief and practice, not medical fact, with care around safety (qi-deviation) and respectful attribution.
research.historyAndOrigin:
The concept of **qi** predates Daoism, arising in early Chinese natural philosophy/cosmology as the fundamental breath-substance of the cosmos; it is shared across Chinese philosophy, medicine, and religion. Daoism (the religious-philosophical tradition associated with the Laozi/Daodejing and Zhuangzi, c. 4th–3rd c. BCE) integrated qi into longevity and self-cultivation arts.

Key milestones: The **Huainanzi** (c. 2nd c. BCE) already frames body, qi, and shen as residence, filling, and controller of life. Breath-cultivation (xingqi, "circulating breath") is attested in the Han era. **Ge Hong's Baopuzi (c. 320 CE)** praises embryonic breathing (taixi) as life-extending. The **Shangqing (Highest Clarity)** and later schools developed visualization-based inner practice. Wei Boyang's **Cantong qi (Token for Joining the Three, trad. 142 CE)** became the root text later read alchemically. The term **neidan (internal alchemy)** and the systematic three-stage refining model became widespread in the **Song dynasty (960–1279)**, associated with the **Zhong-Lü tradition** (legendary immortals Zhongli Quan and Lü Dongbin, 8th c.) and the **Quanzhen (Complete Reality/Perfection) school** founded by Wang Chongyang (1113–1170). Embryonic breathing was reconceived in the **Tang dynasty (618–907)** from "external qi" (waiqi) to "internal qi" (neiqi) theory, linked to the Buddhist-derived "sacred embryo" (shengtai). The modern umbrella term **qigong** was popularized only in the mid-20th century to group these older nourishing-life (yangsheng) traditions; **neigong** is the older term for the same inner work. Dating of the term "dantian" goes back to texts of the Later Han (references c. 165 CE) and the **Scripture of the Yellow Court (Huangting jing, 3rd–4th c.)**.

coreConcepts.aliases: chi; ch'i; ki (Japanese); gi (Korean); also written 气 (simplified)
coreConcepts.meaning: Vital breath/energy that constitutes and animates the cosmos and the body; the dynamic manifestation of the Dao. Character combines 'rice' (米) + 'vapor/steam', evoking nourishing breath. Expressed through yin/yang and the Five Phases.
The ineffable source and pattern of the universe; qi is its dynamic expression and the goal of advanced practice is to return shen to the Dao/Emptiness.
The densest of the Three Treasures; the material/physical foundation of life, stored in the lower dantian; conserved (not depleted) and refined into qi.
The subtlest Treasure; spirit/consciousness, seated in the upper dantian (head); qi is refined into shen, and shen reverts to Emptiness.

coreConcepts.aliases: san bao; the Three Jewels (of the body)
coreConcepts.meaning: Jing–Qi–Shen: the triad of essence, energy, spirit that internal alchemy progressively refines and 'transmutes' upward.

coreConcepts.aliases: tan t'ien; cinnabar field; elixir field; tanden (Japanese)
coreConcepts.meaning: 'Cinnabar/elixir field' — three energy centers (lower/middle/upper) that store and concentrate qi; the cauldron-sites of inner alchemy.

coreConcepts.aliases: meridians; channels and collaterals (jing = channels, luo = collaterals/network)
coreConcepts.meaning: The network of pathways through which qi (and blood) circulate, linking organs to the body surface; site of acupuncture points.

coreConcepts.term: Eight Extraordinary Vessels (Qijing bamai 奇經八脈)
coreConcepts.aliases: eight curious/extraordinary meridians; eight psychic channels
coreConcepts.meaning: Du, Ren, Chong, Dai, Yin Qiao, Yang Qiao, Yin Wei, Yang Wei — deep 'reservoirs' of source/original qi (vs. the twelve 'river' meridians); Du, Ren, Chong, Dai are most used in inner alchemy.
Refining the Three Treasures within the body (vs. waidan/external alchemy with mineral elixirs); body as crucible, producing the 'golden elixir' (jindan) / immortal embryo.
The earlier laboratory alchemy of compounding mineral/herbal elixirs for immortality; contrasted with and partly internalized into neidan.

coreConcepts.aliases: chi kung; nei kung; energy work / internal cultivation
coreConcepts.meaning: Qigong: broad modern umbrella for qi-cultivation (postures, breath, meditation). Neigong: older term for deeper internal cultivation working from the inside outward; in many lineages near-synonymous.

coreConcepts.aliases: yuan chi; Original/Primordial Qi; prenatal qi; xiantian qi (先天)
coreConcepts.meaning: The original vitality received at conception/birth, depleted over life; inner practice (esp. embryonic breathing) aims to conserve/recreate it. Distinguished from postnatal (houtian 後天) acquired qi from breath and food.

coreConcepts.term: Jindan / Immortal Embryo (金丹 / 聖胎 shengtai)
coreConcepts.aliases: golden elixir; sacred embryo; spirit embryo
coreConcepts.meaning: The 'elixir' / spiritual embryo symbolically conceived, gestated and 'born' within the dantian — the perfected, deathless spirit-body of the adept.

energyCenters.name: Lower Dantian (下丹田, xia dantian)
energyCenters.location: Lower abdomen, commonly cited 1.3 / 2 / 2.4 / 3 / 3.6 cun (inches) below or behind the navel; deep in the belly. Sometimes ~3 cun below the navel ('Sea of Qi', qihai).
energyCenters.meaning: The dantian 'proper' and primary furnace/cauldron; seat/storehouse of jing (essence). First refining stage ('refine essence into breath') occurs here. Center of gravity, rootedness, and qi accumulation.
energyCenters.associations: Also called Mingmen (Gate of Existence/Life — though mingmen is also a back point), Guanyuan (Origin of the Barrier), Qihai (Ocean of Breath); element water/earth; associated with the kidneys, jing, and the 'stove' (golden stove) generating warmth; trigram Kan (water) in alchemical symbolism.

energyCenters.name: Middle Dantian (中丹田, zhong dantian)
energyCenters.location: Center of the chest (heart/sternum, around the heart), or between heart and navel depending on source; near the solar plexus/heart in some schemes.
energyCenters.meaning: Seat of qi (breath/energy) and of the emotions/heart-mind; second refining stage ('refine breath into spirit'). Associated with circulation of qi and emotional processing.
energyCenters.associations: Called Yellow Court (Huangting), Crimson Palace (Jianggong), Central Palace (Zhonggong), Mysterious Female (Xuanpin); linked to the heart, fire, and trigram Li; often depicted spiral-shaped in the Neijing tu.

energyCenters.name: Upper Dantian (上丹田, shang dantian)
energyCenters.location: Head — center of the forehead / between and slightly behind the eyebrows, or the brain region; associated with the 'third eye' area and the Niwan (Muddy Pellet) at the crown/center of brain.
energyCenters.meaning: Seat of shen (spirit/consciousness); associated with thinking, contemplation, perception, and spiritual realization. Final stage ('refine spirit and revert to Emptiness') culminates here.
energyCenters.associations: Called Niwan (Muddy Pellet) and Palace of Qian (Qiangong, 'Heaven' trigram); divided in some texts into 'nine palaces/chambers'; linked to the brain, the eyes (Sun and Moon), and the meeting point of yang at the crown (Baihui).

energyCenters.name: Du Mai (督脈, Governing/Governor Vessel)
energyCenters.location: Runs up the back midline from the perineum (Huiyin) along the spine over the crown (Baihui) to the upper gum/palate.
energyCenters.meaning: The great yang reservoir vessel; the 'rising' (yang) arc of the Microcosmic Orbit along which refined qi is led upward. 'Sea of the yang channels.'
energyCenters.associations: Yang; spine; depicted as a mountain range in the Neijing tu; contains the 'Three Gates/Passes' (san guan) where qi may stagnate: Weilu (coccyx), Jiaji/Lulu (mid-back, between scapulae), and Yuzhen (Jade Pillow, occiput).

energyCenters.name: Ren Mai (任脈, Conception/Directing Vessel)
energyCenters.location: Runs down the front midline from the lower lip/chin down the chest and abdomen to the perineum (Huiyin); ~24 classical points.
energyCenters.meaning: The great yin reservoir vessel; the 'descending' (yin) arc of the Microcosmic Orbit along which qi settles back to the lower dantian. 'Sea of the yin channels.'
energyCenters.associations: Yin; front of the body; governs nourishment and (in TCM) the uterus/conception; in the Neijing tu the figure of Bodhidharma stands by the 'Function/Conception' vessel.

energyCenters.name: Huiyin (會陰) — perineum
energyCenters.location: Perineum, between the genitals and anus; junction where Ren, Du, and Chong vessels meet.
energyCenters.meaning: The 'lower gate' / Gate of Life and Death; the bottom of the Microcosmic Orbit where qi turns to rise up the Du; gently held/closed to prevent leakage of qi/jing.
energyCenters.associations: Yin earth gate; the 'Sea-bottom' (haidi).

energyCenters.name: Chong Mai (衝脈, Penetrating/Thrusting Vessel)
energyCenters.location: Runs vertically deep in the body's core, in front of the spine from perineum upward.
energyCenters.meaning: Central core channel most closely associated with yuanqi (original qi); the 'Sea of Blood' and of the twelve channels; used in deeper/advanced (central channel) cultivation.
energyCenters.associations: Central axis; original/ancestral qi; sometimes likened to the central channel of Indic systems though distinct in conception.

research.energyFlowModel:
Classical Daoist physiology treats the body as a circulating system rather than fixed organs. **Postnatal qi** is acquired from air (breath) and food, processed and combined with **prenatal/original qi (yuanqi)** inherited at birth. Qi flows through the **twelve primary meridians** in a fixed 24-hour daily sequence (Lung → Large Intestine → Stomach → Spleen → Heart → ... → Liver → back to Lung), each meridian paired to a zang (solid/yin) or fu (hollow/yang) organ; the deeper **eight extraordinary vessels** act as reservoirs that fill or drain these 'rivers.' Health is smooth, abundant, unobstructed flow; illness is stagnation, blockage, deficiency, or counterflow.

In inner-alchemy/qigong practice, qi is consciously gathered and concentrated in the **lower dantian** (the 'stove'), generating warmth/'fire,' then led in the **Microcosmic Orbit**: UP the **Du Mai** (yang) along the spine — typically coordinated with inhalation — passing the three gates (Weilu at the coccyx, Jiaji at mid-back, Yuzhen/Jade Pillow at the occiput) where it can stagnate, over the crown (Baihui) to the upper dantian/Niwan; then DOWN the **Ren Mai** (yin) along the front midline — typically with exhalation — through the chest and navel back to the lower dantian. The **tongue touches the upper palate** ('building the magpie bridge,' que qiao) to bridge the gap between Du and Ren at the mouth and complete the closed loop; the **perineum (Huiyin)** is gently sealed as the lower bridge to prevent leakage. The **Macrocosmic Orbit (da zhou tian)** then extends circulation out into the arms, legs, and remaining vessels, integrating the whole body.

The overarching alchemical model is a *reversal* of ordinary life's outward, depleting flow: instead of essence/qi dissipating downward and outward (aging, death), the adept turns the flow inward and upward — **'refine jing into qi → refine qi into shen → refine shen and return to Emptiness/Dao'** — symbolized in the Neijing tu by children/water-wheels pumping water *up* the spine-mountain. Rising is associated with yang/fire/heaven, settling with yin/water/earth; the marriage of fire (heart/Li) and water (kidney/Kan) in the cauldron 'cooks' the elixir. **Embryonic breathing (taixi)** is the subtlest endpoint: breath becomes so fine it seems to cease, centered at the navel, recreating the self-contained, placenta-fed circulation of the fetus and conserving original qi.
research.feltSenseAndTransitions:
Practitioners are told the practice should feel calm, warm, and rooted rather than forced. As qi gathers in the **lower dantian**, the classic reported sensation is **warmth, heat, fullness, or pressure ('the golden stove')** in the lower belly. As the Microcosmic Orbit opens, commonly described sensations include **warmth, tingling, pulsing, flowing, or mild 'electricity'** moving along the spine and front midline; when the **tongue-to-palate connection** is made, practitioners often report a tingling or current passing over the palate, down the throat, to the chest/solar plexus and back to the lower dantian. Early, modest signs are emphasized over dramatic ones: deeper natural abdominal breathing, subtle warmth or tingling along the midline, steadier attention, calmer mind — with clearer circulation sensations developing only after weeks to months of consistent practice.

Transitions are described as a continuous rising-and-settling loop synchronized (loosely) with the breath: a sense of qi *rising* up the back on inhalation (lighter, yang, sometimes felt as the breath/awareness lifting along the spine) and *descending/settling* down the front on exhalation (heavier, yin, sinking and grounding into the belly). At the **three gates** along the spine, beginners often feel resistance, dullness, soreness, or 'sticking' until the points 'open.' In embryonic/reverse breathing the felt sense is of the breath becoming **slow, fine, silent, and centered at the navel**, with attention drawn inward; in deep taixi the practitioner feels they are barely breathing at all ('breathing through the whole body / the pores'). Teachers stress *intention guiding, not forcing* qi ('yi leads qi'), keeping the body relaxed; tension, over-concentration, or strained breath-holding is taken as a sign to ease off.

practices.name: Microcosmic Orbit / Small Heavenly Circulation
practices.aliases: Xiao zhou tian (小周天); Small Universe; Small Heavenly Cycle; opening the Ren and Du
practices.tradition: Daoist neidan / qigong (Zhong-Lü, Quanzhen lineages and modern qigong)
practices.description: Foundational inner-alchemy circuit that joins the Conception (Ren) and Governing (Du) vessels into a continuous loop of qi circulation.
practices.howPracticed: Sit (or stand) with spine erect but relaxed, eyes soft/closed, hands resting on lap or over the lower dantian; tongue tip rests on the upper palate behind the front teeth ('magpie bridge'); perineum gently lifted/sealed. First develop deep abdominal breathing into the lower dantian to build warmth. Then use gentle intention (yi) to lead qi up the Du Mai along the spine (often on inhalation) through the three gates and over the crown, then down the Ren Mai along the front midline (often on exhalation) back to the lower dantian, repeating in a smooth circuit. Breath is slow, quiet, and unforced; intention guides without strain.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Smooth, unobstructed circulation of refined qi; health, calm, and emotional balance; foundational stage for higher neidan (gestating the elixir/immortal embryo).
practices.feltExperience: Warmth/pressure building in the lower belly; tingling, warmth, pulsing or 'current' along spine and front midline; current over the tongue/palate to the chest; possible resistance at the three spinal gates until they 'open.'
practices.safetyNotes: Teachers warn against forcing qi with the mind, over-long sessions, or pushing through pain; improper or obsessive practice is traditionally said to risk 'qi deviation' (see safety). Best learned from a qualified teacher; not a medical treatment.

practices.name: Macrocosmic Orbit / Great Heavenly Circulation
practices.aliases: Da zhou tian (大周天); Large Heavenly Cycle; Grand Circulation
practices.tradition: Daoist neidan / advanced qigong
practices.description: Advanced extension of the Microcosmic Orbit that circulates qi beyond the Ren/Du loop into the limbs and the remaining extraordinary vessels and primary meridians, integrating the whole body (and, symbolically, the body with the cosmos).
practices.howPracticed: After stable Microcosmic Orbit, qi is led out along the arms, legs, and additional channels (e.g., Chong, Dai, Qiao, Wei vessels) so the entire body circulates as one field; coordinated with refined, subtle breathing and deep stillness. Methods vary by lineage.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Full-body energetic integration; deeper alchemical refinement (qi→shen); union of the practitioner's qi with heaven/earth ('macrocosm').
practices.feltExperience: Whole-body warmth/flow and a sense of expansiveness or boundary-dissolving; reported as subtler and more diffuse than the Microcosmic Orbit.
practices.safetyNotes: Considered advanced; should follow mastery of foundational stages and be guided by a teacher to avoid disorientation/qi deviation.

practices.name: Embryonic / Fetal Breathing
practices.aliases: Taixi (胎息); womb breathing; fetal respiration; tai hsi
practices.tradition: Daoist yangsheng (nourishing life) and neidan; documented from Ge Hong onward, transformed in the Tang
practices.description: Extremely subtle breathing that imitates the breathing of a fetus in the womb — eventually 'without using nose or mouth' — centered at the navel/lower dantian to conserve and recreate original (prenatal) qi.
practices.howPracticed: Sit quietly with attention drawn inward to the navel/lower dantian; let breathing become progressively slower, softer, finer and quieter until it seems almost to cease; in Tang+ practice, mentally circulate internal qi (neiqi) among the organs rather than relying on outer air, 'not letting the Original Breath exit the body'; often paired with swallowing saliva (traditionally several times). Early ('external qi') methods used breath-retention counted by heartbeats; later methods emphasize visualization and minimal apnea.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Conserve/restore yuanqi (original vitality); longevity and immortality cultivation; 'nourishing the sacred embryo' (shengtai); deep stillness.
practices.feltExperience: Breath so fine and silent it feels nearly absent; warmth and centeredness at the navel; sense of being self-contained/nourished from within ('breathing through the whole body / pores').
practices.safetyNotes: Historically the extreme breath-holding of early methods caused buzzing ears, vertigo, sweating, heat, and altitude-sickness-like symptoms; classical authorities (e.g., Sun Simiao) limited breath-retention. Modern practice favors gentle, non-forced breathing; do not strain or hold the breath dangerously.

practices.name: Natural (Abdominal/Buddhist) Breathing
practices.aliases: Buddhist breathing; normal abdominal breathing; zhengfu huxi
practices.tradition: Daoist and Buddhist breath cultivation; the usual foundation taught first
practices.description: Relaxed diaphragmatic breathing where the lower belly expands on the inhale and gently contracts on the exhale.
practices.howPracticed: Inhale: diaphragm descends, lower abdomen expands outward, qi sinks to the lower dantian. Exhale: abdomen relaxes inward, diaphragm rises. Slow, smooth, silent, through the nose; tongue on palate. Practiced for several weeks to establish ease before reverse breathing.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Calm the mind and nervous system; sink qi to the dantian; build the foundation for deeper breath and orbit work.
practices.feltExperience: Soft, full belly on inhale; relaxation and warmth gathering in the lower abdomen; settling, grounding feeling.
practices.safetyNotes: Low risk; the recommended starting point. Avoid forcing the belly or over-breathing (hyperventilation/dizziness).

practices.name: Reverse (Daoist/Pre-Birth) Abdominal Breathing
practices.aliases: Daoist breathing; reverse breathing; pre-birth / womb breathing; fanhu huxi
practices.tradition: Daoist qigong/neigong and martial internal arts (taiji, etc.)
practices.description: The 'reverse' of natural breathing, used to compress, pack and accumulate qi and to mobilize it for issuing power.
practices.howPracticed: Inhale: gently draw the lower abdomen inward toward the spine (and often lift the perineum), creating internal pressure; Exhale: let the lower abdomen expand/release outward. Tongue on palate; movements gentle, never strained. Typically learned only after natural abdominal breathing is comfortable (often recommended 2–4+ weeks of natural breathing first).
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Accumulate and condense qi in the lower dantian; build internal pressure/power; coordinate with martial issuing of force and with orbit circulation.
practices.feltExperience: Heightened pressure, fullness, and warmth in the lower abdomen; a sense of qi 'packing' or compressing; energizing.
practices.safetyNotes: More advanced; doing it too early or too forcefully can cause chest tightness, tension, or discomfort. Build up gradually under guidance; do not over-pressurize. Caution for those with hypertension or cardiovascular issues.

practices.name: Vase / Bellows-type Breath Retention
practices.aliases: Vase breathing; bottle breathing; biqi (breath retention)
practices.tradition: Daoist (and shared with some yogic) breath cultivation
practices.description: Holding/sealing the breath in the lower abdomen to consolidate qi (the lower belly imagined as a sealed vase).
practices.howPracticed: After inhaling into the lower dantian, gently seal above (light swallow/closing) and below (perineum), holding qi compressed in the 'vase' for a comfortable interval, then release. Duration kept moderate and comfortable.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Stabilize and concentrate qi in the lower dantian; deepen stillness; build internal heat.
practices.feltExperience: Strong sense of contained warmth/pressure in the lower belly; stillness.
practices.safetyNotes: Breath retention carries the clearest risk: classical sources document dizziness, ringing ears, heat and distress from excessive holding. Keep holds short and comfortable; avoid if pregnant, hypertensive, or with heart/lung conditions; learn from a teacher.

visualMotifs: The Neijing tu (內經圖, 'Chart of the Inner Landscape/Warp') — the iconic profile-view body-as-landscape diagram (Qing-dynasty stele, 1886, White Cloud Abbey / Baiyun Guan, Beijing): the body read bottom-to-top as a sacred terrain showing the three dantian and qi circulation
The spine depicted as a mountain range (often Kunlun-like peaks), representing the Du Mai / Governing Vessel and the upward path of qi
Waterwheels / treadmills turned by a boy and girl (yin and yang) at the base of the spine, pumping essence/water upward — the 'reversal' of downward-flowing essence
An iron Ox or Buffalo ploughing the earth and 'planting the golden coin/seed' in the lower field — the seed of the Golden Elixir
The Cowherd (Herd Boy) holding the Northern Dipper/Big Dipper, and the Weaving Maiden — celestial-lovers motif marking the middle field and the spinal handle
The middle dantian shown as a spiral; the upper dantian region with the 'Twelve-storied Pavilion' (the trachea), the Jade Pillow, and a multi-chambered palace (Niwan / nine palaces)
Sun and Moon as the two eyes; figures of Laozi and Bodhidharma seated by the Governing and Conception vessels in the head region
The companion Xiuzhen tu (修真圖, 'Chart for the Cultivation of Reality/Perfection') — a frontal diagram overlaying organs, meridians, deities, the Five Phases, and trigrams onto the body
Yin-yang Taiji symbol and the eight trigrams (bagua), especially Kan (☵ water/kidneys) and Li (☲ fire/heart) marking the alchemical 'marriage of water and fire'
Wu Xing (Five Phases) color-coding: green/blue-wood, red-fire, yellow-earth, white-metal, black/dark-water — mapped to organs and directions
Qi/breath classically rendered as cloud-vapor, mist, or steam (echoing the 'vapor over rice' of the character 氣); swirling cloud-scrolls in Daoist art
The 'immortal embryo' / sacred infant depicted seated within the lower abdomen, and a spirit-body emerging from the crown in advanced-stage illustrations
Circulation depicted as a closed loop/orbit up the back and down the front (the Microcosmic Orbit), sometimes drawn as a circle or figure tracing Ren and Du
research.attributionNotes:
This is a living religious, philosophical, and medical tradition — Daoism (a recognized world religion with ordained clergy, monasteries such as Baiyun Guan, and lineages like Quanzhen and Zhengyi) interwoven with Traditional Chinese Medicine and Chinese folk practice. It should be presented as belief, cosmology, and practice within its own framework, NOT as established biomedical or physical fact: qi, meridians, dantian, and the orbits are concepts of a traditional worldview, and the app's non-medical framing is appropriate and important.

Attribution and sensitivity points: (1) Use correct terminology and acknowledge it as Chinese / Daoist in origin; avoid flattening it into generic 'energy healing' or conflating it with Indic chakra/prana systems — dantian are not chakras and the Microcosmic Orbit is not kundalini, even where there are loose parallels (say so as parallels, not equivalences). (2) Many practices were traditionally transmitted orally from teacher to disciple, sometimes under initiation or secrecy; texts are often deliberately cryptic/symbolic (alchemical code language), so claims of a single 'correct' method are contested and lineage-dependent. (3) Transliteration is contested: pinyin (qi, dao, dantian, neidan) vs. older Wade-Giles (ch'i, tao, tan-t'ien, nei-tan); give both where helpful and be consistent. (4) Sources genuinely disagree on specifics — the exact location of the lower dantian, where the middle dantian sits, breath-to-direction coordination in the orbit, and the qigong/neigong distinction all vary by school; present these as contested rather than settled. (5) Safety/respect: real harm ('qi deviation,' zou huo ru mo) is reported from forced breath-holding and over-aggressive practice — the tradition itself counsels gentleness, qualified guidance, and not forcing qi. Do not promise health/immortality outcomes; frame intended effects as traditional aims/beliefs. (6) Avoid commercial/New-Age distortion and respect the sacredness of advanced practices (embryonic breathing, the immortal embryo) rather than presenting them as casual techniques.
