research.topic: Tibetan Vajrayana tsa-lung, tummo, channels and winds (subtle-body yoga)
research.summary:
Tsa-lung (rtsa rlung, "channels and winds") is the family of Tibetan Vajrayana subtle-body practices that work with an invisible "vajra body" made of three things: channels (tsa / Skt. nadi), winds or vital energies (lung / Skt. prana, vayu), and essence-drops (tigle / Skt. bindu). The central premise is that mind and wind are inseparable — "mind rides the wind like a rider on a horse" — so by controlling the breath/winds through posture, breath retention, movement, and visualization, the practitioner can gather the normally dispersed winds out of the two side channels and force them into the blocked central channel. When the winds enter, abide, and dissolve in the central channel, grosser levels of mind fall away and the subtlest "clear light" mind (the same mind that naturally manifests at death) can be made to realize emptiness (sunyata). This union of great bliss and emptiness is the goal — a swift path to Buddhahood, called Mahamudra ("Great Seal") in the Kagyu framing.

The most famous of these practices is tummo (gtum mo, "fierce woman"; Skt. candali), the yoga of inner heat, the first and foundational of the Six Yogas/Dharmas of Naropa (alongside illusory body, dream, clear light, bardo, and phowa/transference). Tummo combines "vase breathing" (a kumbhaka breath-retention held below the navel), visualization of the three channels and four chakras, and a fiery short-A syllable at the navel whose heat melts a white drop at the crown, sending cascading bliss down through the chakras. Trul khor / 'phrul 'khor (yantra yoga) is the associated set of physical movements and breath sequences that open the channels as preliminaries.

These teachings belong to Highest Yoga Tantra (Anuttarayoga / Niruttarayoga) and are not standalone breathing exercises: in their living context they require tantric empowerment (abhiseka), samaya vows, a qualified lineage guru, and substantial preliminary training. They are transmitted within specific lineages — most centrally the Kagyu (Tilopa > Naropa > Marpa > Milarepa > Gampopa), later absorbed into the Gelug via Tsongkhapa, and with parallel systems in Sakya, Nyingma, and the Bon tradition, plus the distinct Kalachakra Six Yogas. The app should present tummo/tsa-lung as a sacred initiatory tradition and belief system, not as a generic wellness hack.
research.historyAndOrigin:
The practices descend from the Indian Buddhist tantric (Vajrayana / Mantrayana) world of the great mahasiddhas, c. 8th-11th centuries CE, drawing on texts such as the Hevajra Tantra, Cakrasamvara Tantra, and Guhyasamaja Tantra, and on figures like Krishnacarya (Krishnacharya/Kanha), Nagarjuna, Luipada, Lawapa, and Shavari.

The Six Dharmas were compiled by the mahasiddha Tilopa (c. 988-1069), transmitted to his student Naropa (c. 1016-1100), who systematized them — hence "Six Yogas of Naropa" (Na ro chos drug). The Tibetan translator-yogi Marpa Lotsawa (c. 1012-1097) carried the lineage from India to Tibet; it passed to the yogi-saint Milarepa (1052-1135), famed for using tummo to survive Himalayan winters in a cotton cloth (hence "repa," cotton-clad), then to the physician-monk Gampopa (1079-1153), whose manuals (e.g., the tradition behind texts like "Closely Stringed Pearls") fixed the standard three-channel/four-chakra progression and seeded the Kagyu sub-schools (Karma Kagyu, Drikung, Drukpa, etc.).

The Gelug founder Je Tsongkhapa (1357-1419) later integrated and systematized inner-heat practice (his "Book of Three Inspirations"/Naropa commentary), making tummo the indispensable foundation for all the other dharmas. Parallel completion-stage systems exist in Sakya (Lamdre/Hevajra), Nyingma and the indigenous Bon tradition (which has its own tsa-lung and trul khor), and the Kalachakra Tantra has its own distinct Six Yogas. The practices are thus old, multi-lineage, and still living. Note: the framing of these as a tidy "Six Yogas" set is itself a Tibetan systematization; the underlying Indian sources are more diffuse.

coreConcepts.aliases: rtsa rlung (Wylie); Skt. nadi-vayu; 'channels and winds'
coreConcepts.meaning: Umbrella term for subtle-body practices manipulating the channels (tsa) and winds (lung); also the broad model of the vajra/subtle body itself.

coreConcepts.aliases: rtsa; Skt. nadi (also sira, srota, dhamani)
coreConcepts.meaning: Channels/conduits of the subtle body through which the winds flow; said to number 72,000 in total, with three principal ones. Not physical anatomy but an experiential energetic map.

coreConcepts.aliases: rlung; Skt. prana / vayu; 'wind, breath, vital energy'
coreConcepts.meaning: Subtle energy-winds linked to the air element that carry/move consciousness; mind 'rides' the wind like a rider on a horse. Ten are counted (five root + five branch). Distinguished as karmic (impure) winds vs. wisdom winds.

Luminous essence-drops of vital energy in the channels/chakras; chiefly a white drop (from the father, crown) and red drop (from the mother, navel), plus the 'indestructible drop' at the heart that carries the subtlest mind and departs at death.

coreConcepts.aliases: gtum mo, 'fierce/wrathful woman'; Skt. candali; 'inner/mystic heat, inner fire'
coreConcepts.meaning: Foundational completion-stage yoga that ignites psychic heat at the navel to drive winds into the central channel and melt the drops, producing bliss-emptiness.

coreConcepts.aliases: dbu ma (central channel); Skt. avadhuti (also sushumna)
coreConcepts.meaning: The central channel running through the body's core (roughly along/just in front of the spine, crown to lower body); normally 'empty'/blocked. The aim of all practice is to bring the winds into it. Often visualized blue (sometimes pale/white or red on the inside).
Right-side channel (in the common Naropa/Gelug schema, red, associated with blood/female principle/sun). Carries downward-flowing winds; runs to the right nostril.

Left-side channel (in the common schema, white, associated with semen/male principle/moon). Runs to the left nostril. NOTE: left/right color and male/female assignments are contested and vary by tantra and by practitioner gender.

coreConcepts.aliases: bum chen / bum pa can; bum pa ltar dgang ('filling like a vase'); Skt. kumbhaka
coreConcepts.meaning: Core breath-retention: upper winds pressed down and lower winds drawn up so they unite and are 'held in a vase' below the navel.
Dynamic yogic movements + breath sequences that open the channels and circulate the winds; preliminary and complement to tummo. Depicted in the Lukhang murals.

coreConcepts.aliases: Na ro chos drug; Six Dharmas of Naropa
coreConcepts.meaning: Tummo (inner heat), gyulu (illusory body), milam (dream), osel (clear light), bardo (intermediate state), phowa (transference of consciousness).
The subtlest level of mind, revealed when winds dissolve in the central channel; the 'clear light of death' that practice trains one to recognize and use to realize emptiness.

coreConcepts.aliases: phyag rgya chen po; bde stong; 'Great Seal'
coreConcepts.meaning: The realized union of great bliss (generated by the melting drops) and the direct cognition of emptiness — the fruit of completion-stage practice.

coreConcepts.aliases: dbang; Skt. abhiseka (initiation); dam tshig (samaya, tantric vow)
coreConcepts.meaning: Ritual transmission by a qualified guru, with binding vows, that authorizes the practice. Considered a prerequisite without which the practice is improper and potentially harmful.

coreConcepts.aliases: yidam; lha'i rnal 'byor; generation stage (bskyed rim) & completion stage (rdzogs rim)
coreConcepts.meaning: Visualizing oneself as an enlightened deity with 'divine pride' and 'clear appearance' (empty yet apparent, like a rainbow); tsa-lung/tummo are completion-stage practices done on this divine-body basis.

energyCenters.name: Crown chakra (Great Bliss wheel)
energyCenters.meaning: Seat of the white drop (white bodhicitta, paternal essence); when navel heat rises and melts it, bliss-nectar drips down. Associated with body and the waking state.
energyCenters.associations: Mahasukha-cakra; ~32 petals/channel-spokes; variegated/multicolored; petals face downward; houses inverted white syllable HAM; linked to body and waking; white drop.

energyCenters.name: Throat chakra (Enjoyment wheel)
energyCenters.meaning: Center of speech/energy and of dream-state experience; a key node where winds and drops are gathered.
energyCenters.associations: Sambhoga-cakra; ~16 petals; commonly red; petals face upward; red syllable OM; linked to speech and dreaming; (fire element in the five-element-by-chakra scheme of some sources).

energyCenters.name: Heart chakra (Dharma wheel)
energyCenters.meaning: Most important center: seat of the indestructible white-and-red drop and the subtlest mind/clear light, which abides through life and departs at death; focal point for clear-light yoga.
energyCenters.associations: Dharma-cakra; ~8 petals (some sources 6); commonly white; petals face downward; blue syllable HUM; linked to mind and deep sleep; (space element in some schemas); indestructible drop.

energyCenters.name: Navel chakra (Emanation/Manifestation wheel)
energyCenters.location: Roughly four finger-widths below the navel
energyCenters.meaning: The furnace of tummo: site of the fiery short-A (a-she / A-stroke) syllable and the red drop; where the side channels insert into the central channel and inner heat is ignited.
energyCenters.associations: Nirmana-cakra; ~64 petals; red; triangular, petals face upward; red short-A syllable (a-thung/'A-stroke', 'nature of inner fire'); linked to manifestation; red drop; (earth element in some schemas).

energyCenters.name: Secret chakra (bliss-sustaining wheel)
energyCenters.meaning: Lower center governing the bliss that is generated and sustained; emphasized especially in Kalachakra five-chakra schemes.
energyCenters.associations: Bliss-preserving wheel; associated with wind/action; (counted in five-chakra Kalachakra systems; the four-chakra Naropa system stops at the navel).

research.energyFlowModel:
The model rests on the inseparability of wind and mind: wherever the subtle winds go, consciousness goes. Ordinarily the winds course through the two side channels (roma/rasana on the right and kyangma/lalana on the left), which wrap around and pinch (knot) the central channel at the chakras — especially navel, heart, throat, and crown — keeping the central channel 'empty' and blocked. Because mind rides these dualistic karmic winds, ordinary perception is dualistic.

Practice reverses this. (1) Trul khor movements and the 'ninefold expelling of stale breath' (alternate-nostril clearing) and cleansing visualizations open and purify the channels. (2) Vase breathing unites the upper (descending) and lower (ascending) winds at the navel: the practitioner inhales, swallows to push the upper winds down, and gently contracts the pelvic floor to draw the lower (downward-voiding apana) winds up, holding them locked together below the navel "like water in a vase." (3) This pressure plus visualization ignites the fiery short-A at the navel. (4) The flame flares up the central channel, and on reaching the crown it melts the white drop/HAM; molten "drops of honey/melted butter" of white bodhicitta descend back down through throat, heart, navel, generating the "four joys/blisses" (joy, supreme joy, special/extraordinary joy, innate joy) as they pass each center. (5) Crucially, the heat and breath-work loosen the knots so the winds enter, abide, and dissolve in the central channel.

When the winds dissolve, the gross levels of mind cease in a sequence of "dissolution signs" (mirage, smoke, fireflies/sparks, lamp-flame, then white 'appearance', red 'increase', black 'near-attainment', and finally clear light) — the same stages experienced involuntarily at death. At death specifically, the white drop descends from the crown and the red drop ascends from the navel toward the indestructible drop at the heart; the meeting reveals the clear light, and the indestructible drop (carrying the subtlest mind/wind) then leaves the body. The yogi's aim is to consciously reproduce this dissolution in meditation so the clear-light mind itself realizes emptiness — bliss and emptiness fused. An external sign of progress is breath flowing equally through both nostrils (indicating winds have centered).
research.feltSenseAndTransitions:
Practitioners are told to expect, first, the literal felt warmth of inner heat: a glowing, then blazing, heat kindling at a point about four finger-widths below the navel during retained vase breaths, which can spread to fill and warm the whole body (the basis of Milarepa's reputed ability to stay warm near-naked in snow; modern studies of advanced practitioners recorded measurable rises in core and especially peripheral skin temperature). The vase-breath itself is felt as a firm but comfortable fullness/pressure held low in the belly — emphatically not a forced, straining clench (Tsongkhapa stresses doing it "gently, without forcefulness").

As the heat rises and the crown drop melts, the described sensation is a downward-streaming, cooling, nectar-like trickle ("like honey or melted butter") that produces successive waves of bliss as it passes the throat, heart, and navel — the four joys, each subtler and more pervasive than the last, culminating in a non-conceptual, all-pervading "innate" bliss. The transition that matters most is from gross dispersed sensation toward an increasingly subtle, settled, luminous quality of mind as the winds gather and dissolve centrally: the dissolution signs (mirage-shimmer, smoke, spark-like points, a steady lamp-flame glow, then whitish, reddish, and dark appearances, then clarity) are experienced inwardly as the senses and concepts quieting into spacious luminosity. The whole point is that this bliss must be wedded to awareness of emptiness rather than indulged as mere pleasure; the felt bliss is treated as a vehicle for, and "similitude" of, awakening.

practices.name: Tummo (inner heat)
practices.aliases: gtum mo; Skt. candali; 'fierce woman / inner fire / mystic heat'
practices.tradition: Vajrayana Highest Yoga Tantra; first of the Six Yogas of Naropa (Kagyu, later Gelug); related forms in Sakya, Nyingma, Bon, and Kalachakra
practices.description: The foundational completion-stage practice: generate psychic heat at the navel to force the winds into the central channel, melt the drops, and produce bliss-emptiness.
practices.howPracticed: Seated in vajra (full-lotus) posture, spine straight, often with vajra-fist mudra. Visualize the three channels (central blue, right red, left white) and the four chakras with their petal-counts, plus seed-syllables: inverted white HAM at crown, red OM at throat, blue HUM at heart, fiery red short-A at navel, all 'tiny as a sesame/mustard seed.' Perform vase breathing (kumbhaka): inhale through the nose, swallow to send upper winds down, contract the pelvic floor to draw lower winds up, hold them united below the navel for as long as comfortable, then exhale gently. The held breath/pressure ignites the short-A; the flame flares up to the crown, melts HAM, and white nectar descends generating the four joys. Combine throughout with meditation on emptiness.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Draw the winds into, and dissolve them in, the central channel to reveal the subtlest clear-light mind and unite great bliss with the realization of emptiness (Mahamudra); also serves as the engine for the other five yogas.
practices.feltExperience: A kindling warmth below the navel building to whole-body heat; a comfortable fullness in the lower belly during retention; then a cooling, honey-like downward stream producing escalating waves of subtle bliss; progressive inner quieting toward luminous clarity.
practices.safetyNotes: Requires empowerment, samaya, and a qualified guru; needs prior stability in channel-visualization and emptiness. Practice gently, never forcing retention; on an empty stomach; breathe through the nose. Forceful or unguided practice is said to risk physical and psychological harm, including subtle-energy imbalance (cf. 'rlung'/lung disorder, a recognized wind-imbalance condition in Tibetan medicine).

practices.name: Vase breathing
practices.aliases: bum chen / bum pa can; bum pa ltar dgang; Skt. kumbhaka (breath retention)
practices.tradition: Vajrayana; the core breath of tummo and tsa-lung
practices.description: A breath-retention technique that unites the upper and lower winds in a 'vase' below the navel.
practices.howPracticed: Inhale fully through the nose; swallow silently to press the upper (descending) winds down; gently tense the pelvic floor to lift the lower (apana) winds up; hold the two together just below the navel, rounding the lower belly like a pot; retain as long as is comfortable (capacity traditionally measured by repeated knee-slaps/finger-snaps), then release slowly through the nose.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Compress and unify the winds at the navel to ignite inner heat and begin drawing winds into the central channel.
practices.feltExperience: Firm, rounded fullness and gentle pressure low in the abdomen; warmth building at the navel; calm, not strained.
practices.safetyNotes: Build retention gradually; keep it gentle; coordinate with a teacher. Over-forcing breath retention is specifically warned against.

practices.name: Trul khor (yantra yoga)
practices.aliases: 'phrul 'khor; tsa lung trul khor; Skt. (rendered) yantra yoga
practices.tradition: Vajrayana and Bon; preliminary/companion to tummo
practices.description: A sequence of dynamic physical movements coordinated with breath (vase-breath held during many movements) to open the channels and circulate the winds.
practices.howPracticed: Combines asana-like postures, vigorous coordinated movements (e.g., hip-swiveling 'like a wheel', 'hooking', 'shooting the bound-vajra mudra', forceful exhalations), and breath retention while maintaining deity self-visualization. Includes preliminary breath-clearing such as the 'ninefold expelling of stale winds' via alternate-nostril breathing through the visualized side channels.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Remove channel blockages so the winds can flow freely and be gathered into the central channel; prepare the body for tummo.
practices.feltExperience: Heat and openness in the channels; loosening of energetic 'knots'; readiness/lightness.
practices.safetyNotes: Learned in person from a qualified teacher; performed before meals; nasal breathing maintained.

practices.name: Vajra recitation / wind-mind unification
practices.aliases: rdo rje bzlas pa; Skt. vajra-japa (OM AH HUM with the breath)
practices.tradition: Vajrayana completion stage
practices.description: Silent recitation that fuses the breath/winds with the three syllables OM-AH-HUM on inhalation, retention, and exhalation to purify and gather the winds.
practices.howPracticed: Synchronize OM with inhalation, AH with retention at the heart/navel, HUM with exhalation, dissolving conceptual mind into the wind-mind.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Purify karmic winds into wisdom winds and steady the mind for entry into the central channel.
practices.feltExperience: Settling of mental chatter; breath becoming subtle and even.
practices.safetyNotes: Empowerment and guidance required.

practices.name: The other Five Yogas (context)
practices.aliases: gyulu (illusory body), milam (dream yoga), osel (clear light), bardo, phowa (transference)
practices.tradition: Six Yogas of Naropa / Kalachakra Six Yogas
practices.description: The completion-stage practices that build on tummo: recognizing all appearance as illusory/empty, lucid dream practice, recognizing/uniting with clear light, navigating the intermediate state, and ejecting consciousness at death to a pure realm.
practices.howPracticed: Each uses the same channels-winds-drops machinery; e.g., phowa visualizes consciousness as a syllable shot up the central channel out the crown; clear-light yoga rests in the luminosity revealed when winds dissolve.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: To carry the realization of bliss-emptiness/clear light through waking, dreaming, and dying, attaining Buddhahood swiftly.
practices.feltExperience: Varies; e.g., dream lucidity, sense of the body as rainbow-like/illusory, luminous clarity.
practices.safetyNotes: Advanced; sequential; require the prior mastery of tummo and full lineage transmission.

visualMotifs: The three channels as three vertical tubes: a central channel (uma/avadhuti) flanked by a right and a left side channel that run from the nostrils down and curve into the central channel below the navel — the side channels twisting around and knotting the central one at the chakras.

Standard color-coding (Naropa/Gelug schema): central channel blue (sometimes white outside/red inside), right channel red, left channel white — though left/right colors are contested across tantras and reverse by practitioner gender.

Chakras drawn as horizontal wheels (cakra) or upward/downward-facing parasols/lotuses with specific spoke/petal counts: crown 32 (variegated, downward), throat 16 (red, upward), heart 8 (white, downward), navel 64 (red, triangular, upward).

Tibetan seed-syllables (Lantsa/Ranjana or Tibetan script) placed in the channels: inverted white HAM at the crown, red OM at the throat, blue HUM at the heart, and the small flame-like red short-A (a-stroke) at the navel.
White and red drops (tigle/bindu) as luminous spheres — white at the crown, red at the navel, the red-and-white indestructible drop at the heart; nectar depicted dripping downward when melted.
Flame imagery at the navel for tummo (inner fire), with the flame rising up the central channel; nectar streaming down — fire-below/nectar-above polarity.

Anatomical-style subtle-body diagrams and yantra/'magical wheel' figures showing yogis in trul khor postures — most famously the late-17th-century Lukhang temple murals (the Dalai Lama's 'Secret Temple' behind the Potala), which illustrate tsa-lung, tummo and 23+ yogic movements.

Mandala and deity-yoga imagery: the practitioner visualized as a luminous, 'empty yet apparent' (rainbow-/mirage-like) yidam deity, the divine body being the basis on which the channels and winds are imagined.

Thangka conventions: figures rendered transparent or schematic to show the interior channel-wheel architecture rather than physical anatomy; sun/moon discs and red/white symbolism for the female (blood/sun) and male (semen/moon) polarities.
research.attributionNotes: These are sacred, initiatory Highest Yoga Tantra practices, not generic breathwork. Several sensitivities must be respected: (1) Empowerment and lineage. Traditionally one may not practice tummo/tsa-lung without receiving the specific empowerment (abhiseka/wang), taking samaya vows, and training under a qualified guru who holds an unbroken lineage. Presenting full operative instructions as a self-help technique misrepresents the tradition; the app should clearly frame this as belief/tradition and direct serious interest to authentic teachers. (2) Secrecy and 'self-secrecy.' The tradition deliberately keeps details restricted — partly to protect unprepared practitioners from harm, partly because the teachings are considered 'self-secret' (incomprehensible or even dangerous without context/initiation). Some instructions are intentionally omitted or coded; reputable practitioner sources withhold the most advanced drop/sexual-yoga details. Respect that reticence. (3) Safety/health framing. Improper or forceful practice is said to risk genuine physical and psychological harm, including 'rlung' (wind) imbalance; the app must not present tummo as a validated medical or thermoregulation method. (4) Lineage accuracy and naming. Distinguish the lineages (Kagyu origin via Tilopa-Naropa-Marpa-Milarepa-Gampopa; Gelug via Tsongkhapa; plus Sakya, Nyingma, and the non-Buddhist Bon tradition) and note that the Kalachakra Six Yogas are a separate system. Get transliterations right (Wylie vs. phonetics; Tibetan tsa/lung/tigle vs. Sanskrit nadi/prana/bindu) and flag that channel colors and male/female assignments differ across tantras — state these as contested rather than fixed. (5) Avoid conflation. Do not collapse Tibetan tsa-lung into Hindu yoga's ida/pingala/sushumna or into modern '7-chakra' New Age systems; they overlap historically but the Buddhist enumeration (typically 4-5 chakras, distinct functions, emptiness-oriented goal) is its own framework. (6) Sexual symbolism. Red/white, mother/father, and consort imagery are integral and should be handled respectfully and non-sensationally. (7) Cultural ownership. These belong to living Tibetan and Himalayan Buddhist (and Bon) communities under conditions of cultural and political pressure; treat them as a heritage to honor and attribute, not a commodity to extract.

research.topic: Buddhist Meditation on the Breath: Ānāpānasati (Mindfulness of Breathing) across Theravāda, Zen, and Tibetan Traditions
research.summary:
Mindfulness of breathing — Ānāpānasati (Pali; Sanskrit ānāpānasmṛti) — is one of the oldest and most widely practiced Buddhist meditations, attributed to the historical Buddha (Siddhārtha Gautama, c. 5th century BCE) and said in the canon to be the method by which he himself awakened. Its locus classicus is the Ānāpānasati Sutta, the 118th discourse of the Majjhima Nikāya in the Pali Canon, which lays out sixteen steps grouped into four tetrads. These tetrads map directly onto the four satipaṭṭhāna (foundations/establishings of mindfulness): body, feeling, mind, and dhammas (mental objects/phenomena). The breath serves as a single object that, fully developed, fulfills all four foundations of mindfulness and in turn the seven factors of awakening (bojjhaṅga), leading to true knowledge and liberation.

The breath is the primary, near-universal meditation anchor in Buddhism because it is always available, free of ritual props, naturally settles the mind, and bridges body and mind. Two complementary modes are cultivated: samatha (calm/tranquility, concentration on one point, leading to jhāna absorption) and vipassanā (insight, clear seeing of impermanence/anicca, suffering/dukkha, not-self/anatta). The breath supports both — early steps build collectedness, later steps turn the concentrated mind toward investigation. Sources differ on sequence: Buddhaghosa's 5th-century Visuddhimagga teaches developing jhāna first and then insight, while several suttas describe the two yoked together; this is genuinely contested among traditions and teachers.

Concretely, the practitioner sits with legs crossed and spine erect and rests attention where breath is most distinctly felt — for the classical Theravāda/Visuddhimagga method, the "touching point" at the nostril rim or upper lip; for many modern Burmese and Zen schools, the lower abdomen (hara/tanden) or the whole body. Beginners often count breaths (gaṇanā; Japanese susokukan) up to ten and back. As concentration deepens, the gross breath grows subtle and may seem to disappear, replaced by a luminous mental sign (nimitta) — described as cotton wool, mist, a star, a gem, a wheel, or a bright disc like the midday sun — marking access concentration (upacāra-samādhi) and the threshold of jhāna.

The same root practice fans out across traditions: Theravāda anapanasati and the modern Burmese Vipassanā movement (Ledi Sayadaw → U Ba Khin → S.N. Goenka; Mahasi Sayadaw's abdomen-rising/falling method); Chinese Chan and Japanese Zen susokukan/zuisokukan (counting/following the breath, energy gathered in the tanden); and Tibetan Vajrayāna, where breath becomes the vehicle for working with subtle winds (rlung/prāṇa) in the channel system, including nine-round purification breathing, vase breathing (kumbhaka/bumchen), and tummo inner-heat yoga.
research.historyAndOrigin: Ānāpānasati is attributed to Siddhārtha Gautama, the historical Buddha (c. 5th century BCE, northeast India / Gangetic plain), and the tradition holds it was the meditation he was absorbed in on the night of his awakening and that he practiced it throughout his teaching life. Its canonical foundation is the Ānāpānasati Sutta (Majjhima Nikāya 118) of the Theravāda Pali Canon; Thanissaro Bhikkhu calls it the most detailed meditation instruction in the Canon. Parallel material appears in the Saṃyutta Nikāya (Ānāpāna-saṃyutta) and, in northern transmission, in the Sarvāstivāda Ekottara Āgama and other Āgamas. It crossed into Chinese Buddhism early as 安般 / shù xī guān (數息觀, "contemplation of counting the breath"); An Shigao translated breath-meditation texts in the 2nd century CE, and the practice was systematized by Zhiyi (Tiantai) and carried into Chan and Japanese Zen as susokukan. In Tibetan Vajrayāna the breath was integrated into tantric completion-stage yogas (e.g., the Six Yogas of Nāropa). The most influential systematic Theravāda manual is Buddhaghosa's Visuddhimagga (c. 5th century CE, Sri Lanka), which codifies the touching-point method, counting, the nimitta, and the jhāna progression. The modern revival of breath-based insight meditation stems from Burmese teachers beginning with Ledi Sayadaw (1846–1923) and continuing through Mahasi Sayadaw, Sayagyi U Ba Khin (1899–1971), and the lay teacher S.N. Goenka (1924–2013), whose courses spread the practice worldwide; Thai Forest teachers (e.g., Ajahn Lee, Ajahn Buddhadāsa) and Western Insight (Vipassana) teachers (Kornfield, Goldstein, Salzberg via the Insight Meditation Society) also transmitted it. Thich Nhat Hanh (Plum Village, Zen) re-translated the sutta as "The 16 Exercises of Mindful Breathing" (book: Breathe, You Are Alive).

coreConcepts.aliases: Sanskrit: ānāpānasmṛti; Chinese: 安那般那 / 數息觀 (shùxī guān); Japanese: anna-hannen; 'mindfulness of breathing'
coreConcepts.meaning: Compound of āna (in-breath/inhalation), apāna (out-breath/exhalation), and sati (mindfulness, recollection). The practice of resting mindful awareness on the natural breath.
Four domains of mindful contemplation — body (kāya), feeling-tone (vedanā), mind/mind-states (citta), and mental objects/phenomena (dhammā). The four anapanasati tetrads map onto these.
Concentration practice that stills and unifies the mind on one object, culminating in jhāna. Breath-counting is a classic samatha technique.

coreConcepts.aliases: Sanskrit: vipaśyanā; 'insight', 'clear seeing'; Tibetan: lhaktong (lhag mthong)
coreConcepts.meaning: Insight practice: direct seeing of the three marks of existence — impermanence (anicca), unsatisfactoriness (dukkha), and not-self (anatta). The later tetrads of anapanasati turn the breath toward insight.

coreConcepts.aliases: Sanskrit: dhyāna; Chinese: chán (禪); Japanese: zen; 'meditative absorption'
coreConcepts.meaning: States of deep concentration. First jhāna has five factors: vitakka (applied thought), vicāra (sustained thought), pīti (rapture), sukha (bliss), ekaggatā (one-pointedness). Successive jhānas drop coarser factors; the fourth rests in equanimity (upekkhā) and one-pointedness.

The mental sign that arises as concentration deepens and the breath becomes subtle: progresses from unstable learning sign to a steady, luminous counterpart sign that signals access concentration and the doorway to jhāna.
Access concentration: hindrances suppressed, counterpart nimitta stable, on the threshold of jhāna. Absorption concentration: full immersion in jhāna.
Classical four-stage Visuddhimagga progression of anapanasati: count breaths, then follow them, then keep contact at the touching point, then the mind fixes and the nimitta arises.
Sensory desire, ill-will, sloth-and-torpor, restlessness-and-worry, and doubt — the obstacles that concentration on the breath temporarily suppresses, allowing jhāna.

coreConcepts.aliases: Japanese 數息観 (counting the breath) / 隨息観 (following the breath)
coreConcepts.meaning: Zen breath practices: silently counting exhalations one to ten and returning to one (susokukan), or simply following the breath without counting (zuisokukan); energy (ki) is gathered in the tanden.
In Vajrayāna, the subtle inner winds that flow in the channels (rtsa/nāḍī) and carry the mind. 'The wind is like a horse and the mind the rider' — controlling the breath/wind steadies the mind.

practices.name: The Sixteen Steps / Four Tetrads (Ānāpānasati Sutta, MN 118)
practices.aliases: The 16 contemplations; Thich Nhat Hanh's '16 Exercises of Mindful Breathing'
practices.tradition: Theravāda (canonical); adapted across Buddhist schools
practices.description: The complete progressive scheme. FIRST TETRAD — Body (kāya): (1) breathing in/out long, knowing it is long; (2) breathing in/out short, knowing it is short; (3) 'I will breathe in/out experiencing the whole body'; (4) 'I will breathe in/out calming the bodily formation (kāya-saṅkhāra).' SECOND TETRAD — Feeling (vedanā): (5) experiencing rapture (pīti); (6) experiencing bliss/pleasure (sukha); (7) experiencing the mental formation (citta-saṅkhāra); (8) calming the mental formation. THIRD TETRAD — Mind (citta): (9) experiencing the mind; (10) gladdening the mind; (11) concentrating the mind (samādahaṃ cittaṃ); (12) liberating/releasing the mind. FOURTH TETRAD — Dhammas (mental objects): (13) contemplating impermanence (anicca); (14) contemplating dispassion/fading (virāga); (15) contemplating cessation (nirodha); (16) contemplating relinquishment/letting go (paṭinissagga).
practices.howPracticed: Sit cross-legged, body erect, establish mindfulness 'in front' (parimukhaṃ); breathe naturally and progress through the steps as the mind stabilizes. Early steps emphasize calm/concentration; later steps shift to insight.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: To develop mindfulness so it fulfills all four foundations of mindfulness, then the seven factors of awakening (bojjhaṅga), culminating in true knowledge (vijjā) and liberation (vimutti).
practices.feltExperience: Progression from coarse physical breath sensation, through calming of body and arising of rapture (pīti) and bliss (sukha), to a unified, gladdened, then released mind, and finally a felt sense of fading, letting go, and equanimity.
practices.safetyNotes: Steps 5–8 can release strong rapture/energy; teachers advise not chasing or clinging to pīti. Insight stages (tetrad 4) can surface difficult material — traditionally done with a teacher.

practices.name: Counting and Following the Breath (Visuddhimagga method)
practices.aliases: gaṇanā (counting), anubandhana (following); 'touching-point' / anapana-spot method
practices.tradition: Theravāda (Buddhaghosa, Visuddhimagga)
practices.description: Beginner-to-intermediate samatha technique anchoring attention at a fixed point.
practices.howPracticed: Fix mindfulness at the 'touching point' — the rim of the nostrils or the upper lip, within a small triangle between nose-tip and lip, where the air contact is felt most distinctly (the gatekeeper simile: watch the breath only at the 'gate,' not following it inside the body). Count breath cycles 'one, one … two, two …' up to ten, then restart at one; count faster as breath quickens; never count below five or above ten. When the mind is steady, drop counting and simply 'follow'/connect with each breath (beginning–middle–end). As breath subtilizes, keep contact (phusanā) and let the mind fix (ṭhapanā).
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Build access and then absorption concentration (upacāra → appanā samādhi); suppress the five hindrances; reach jhāna.
practices.feltExperience: The breath becomes progressively finer and quieter; the body feels cool and comfortable; the gross breath may seem to vanish. A nimitta (mental sign) appears — first an unsteady 'learning sign' (uggaha-nimitta), then a steady, bright 'counterpart sign' (paṭibhāga-nimitta) likened to cotton wool, a star, a gem, a string of pearls, mist, a wheel, or a bright disc/midday sun.
practices.safetyNotes: Do not strain to control the breath or force the nimitta; if the breath 'disappears' do not panic — keep attention at the touching point. Excessive striving can cause headache/tension; relax effort.

practices.name: Mahasi 'Rising and Falling' (abdomen) Method
practices.aliases: Mahasi Sayadaw vipassanā; noting practice
practices.tradition: Burmese Theravāda (Vipassanā movement)
practices.description: Insight-oriented method using the abdomen rather than the nostrils as the primary breath object.
practices.howPracticed: Rest attention on the rising and falling movement of the abdomen with each breath, mentally 'noting' ('rising,' 'falling,' and other arising sensations/thoughts). Emphasis is on bare observation of changing phenomena, not on fixed concentration.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Direct insight into impermanence and not-self; progress through the 'insight knowledges' (ñāṇa) toward stream-entry.
practices.feltExperience: Vivid, granular awareness of sensations arising and passing; can include the 'arising and passing away' stage with energy, lights, and rapture, and challenging 'dukkha ñāṇas' (fear, disgust, dissolution).
practices.safetyNotes: Insight stages (notably the 'dark night'/dukkha knowledges) can be psychologically intense; traditionally undertaken in retreat under a qualified teacher.

practices.name: Susokukan / Zuisokukan (Zen breath counting/following)
practices.aliases: Japanese 數息観 / 隨息観; the six aspects: su (count), zui (follow), shi (stop), kan (observe), gen (return), jō (purify)
practices.tradition: Chan / Zen (Rinzai and Sōtō); from Chinese shùxī guān, ultimately Tiantai/Zhiyi
practices.description: The most basic zazen breath practice; the Japanese form of anapanasati.
practices.howPracticed: Sit in zazen (full/half lotus, Burmese, or seiza), spine erect, hands in the cosmic mudra. Breathe naturally, settling awareness in the lower abdomen (hara / tanden, a point roughly two inches below the navel). Count silently — usually one number per exhalation — from one to ten, then return to one; when the count is lost, simply begin again at one. With stability, drop counting and just follow the breath (zuisokukan), then eventually shikantaza ('just sitting').
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Gather ki (vital energy) in the tanden, unify body-breath-mind, settle into samādhi, and ultimately realize one's true nature (kenshō/satori).
practices.feltExperience: A sense of energy collecting and warmth in the lower belly; mind clarifies and quiets; long, slow exhalations; eventually breath and counting merge into effortless flow.
practices.safetyNotes: Over-forcing the breath into the tanden or holding tension can cause strain; teachers emphasize natural, unforced exhalation.

practices.name: Nine-Round (Ninefold) Purification Breathing
practices.aliases: Tibetan: rlung sbyong; nine-fold purification of the winds; a form of alternate-nostril breathing
practices.tradition: Tibetan Vajrayāna (preliminary to tsa-lung / completion-stage yogas)
practices.description: A preparatory breath-purification done before subtle-body practice.
practices.howPracticed: Sit in the seven-point Vairocana posture. In sets of three, breathe through alternating nostrils (closing one with a finger) while visualizing stale/afflictive winds being expelled and fresh prāṇa drawn in: typically inhaling through the right and exhaling left, then left/right, then both — nine breaths total — clearing the three channels of obstructing winds and afflictions (the three poisons).
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Purify the channels, calm and balance the winds, settle the mind, and prepare for visualization and completion-stage practice.
practices.feltExperience: A sense of clearing, lightness, and balance; calming of agitation; the body visualized as transparent and luminous.
practices.safetyNotes: Subtle-wind work is traditionally taught only with empowerment and direct guidance from a qualified lama; forcing retention can cause rlung imbalance (agitation, insomnia, energetic disturbance — 'lung disease').

practices.name: Vase Breathing (Kumbhaka)
practices.aliases: Tibetan: bumchen / bumpachen; Sanskrit: kumbhaka; 'pot/vase breathing'
practices.tradition: Tibetan Vajrayāna (completion stage, e.g., tummo/Six Yogas)
practices.description: Gentle breath-retention that gathers and 'seals' the winds in the lower belly.
practices.howPracticed: After exhaling stale air, inhale, draw the lower (downward-clearing) wind up and press the upper wind down, uniting them at a point about four finger-widths below the navel so the abdomen takes the rounded shape of a vase/pot; hold gently, then release. Combined with visualization of the central channel and inner heat.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Draw the winds into the central channel (avadhūtī), ignite inner heat (tummo), stabilize subtle energy, and generate blissful warmth and clarity.
practices.feltExperience: Warmth/heat building below the navel and rising through the central channel; bliss (bde ba); steadiness and vividness of mind.
practices.safetyNotes: Strongly cautioned against without a qualified teacher and empowerment; improper retention can cause serious rlung disturbances. Not a beginner practice.

practices.name: Tummo (Inner Heat Yoga)
practices.aliases: Tibetan: gtum mo; Sanskrit: caṇḍālī; 'fierce woman'/inner fire
practices.tradition: Tibetan Vajrayāna (first of the Six Yogas of Nāropa)
practices.description: Advanced completion-stage practice using breath, visualization, and posture to ignite subtle heat.
practices.howPracticed: Combines vase breathing, physical movements (trul khor / 'tsa lung'), and visualization of a hot AH-stroke/flame at the navel chakra and a white drop (HAM) at the crown; the wind fans the inner fire, melting the drops which descend through the channels producing bliss.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Melt and circulate the subtle drops (thigle/bindu), generate the union of bliss and emptiness, and realize the clear-light nature of mind.
practices.feltExperience: Physical heat (famously demonstrated by drying wet sheets), waves of bliss, and refined states of consciousness.
practices.safetyNotes: Among the most advanced and physically demanding practices; requires empowerment, transmission, and close supervision. Misuse risks energetic and psychological harm.

energyCenters.name: Touching point / Ānāpāna spot (Theravāda)
energyCenters.location: The small triangular area between the tip of the nose and the upper lip — the nostril rim or upper lip where in- and out-breath contact is felt most distinctly.
energyCenters.associations: Not an 'energy center' in the tantric sense but the classical Theravāda fixation point for the breath nimitta; the place where the luminous counterpart sign (likened to a star, gem, mist, or midday sun) eventually appears.
energyCenters.meaning: The 'gate' at which the breath is watched; stabilizing attention here leads to the nimitta, access concentration, and jhāna.

energyCenters.name: Hara / Tanden (Zen)
energyCenters.location: Lower abdomen, a point roughly two inches (about three finger-widths) below the navel and inward.
energyCenters.associations: Center of ki (vital energy); the focus of grounding and gathering energy in zazen. Cultivated through long, settled exhalations.
energyCenters.meaning: The somatic anchor of Zen breathing: energy and awareness are 'gathered in the tanden' to stabilize body, breath, and mind.

energyCenters.name: Central channel — Avadhūtī / rTsa Uma
energyCenters.location: Runs vertically up the center of the body (roughly in front of the spine) from below the navel/perineum to the crown of the head.
energyCenters.associations: Color commonly visualized blue (sometimes dark blue/transparent); associated with the wind humor and the nervous system in Tibetan medicine; the channel into which the winds are drawn in completion-stage yoga. Sanskrit: avadhūtī (also suṣumnā in Indian systems).
energyCenters.meaning: The master channel; when winds and mind enter, settle, and dissolve here, blissful clear-light awareness arises.

energyCenters.name: Right channel — Rasanā / rTsa Roma
energyCenters.location: Runs parallel to the central channel on the right side.
energyCenters.associations: Visualized red; linked with solar/warming energy, the bile humor and blood circulation, and (in some teachings) the wind that carries afflictions/aversion. Sanskrit: rasanā (also piṅgalā in Indian systems).
energyCenters.meaning: Carries the 'solar' prāṇa; one of the two side channels purified by alternate-nostril (nine-round) breathing.

energyCenters.name: Left channel — Lalanā / rTsa Kyangma
energyCenters.location: Runs parallel to the central channel on the left side.
energyCenters.associations: Visualized white; linked with lunar/cooling and nourishing energy, the phlegm humor and lymphatic function. Sanskrit: lalanā (also iḍā in Indian systems).
energyCenters.meaning: Carries the 'lunar' prāṇa; together with the right channel it must be emptied so winds can enter the central channel.

energyCenters.name: Chakras / Wheels — Khorlo (rtsa 'khor lo)
energyCenters.location: Energy wheels strung along the central channel — commonly the crown, throat, heart, navel, and secret/below-navel centers (number varies by system: often five in Tibetan tantra, sometimes more).
energyCenters.associations: Each is a hub where channels branch (channel-petals), where specific winds and drops (thigle) gather; associated in higher yoga with particular syllables, deities, blisses, and elements. The navel chakra is the seat of inner heat (tummo); the heart chakra holds the indestructible drop and very subtle wind-mind.
energyCenters.meaning: Knots/junctions in the channel network; loosening them lets the winds enter and abide in the central channel, producing the four blisses and clear light.

research.energyFlowModel: Two distinct models operate depending on tradition. (1) Theravāda/early Buddhist (non-tantric): there is no esoteric channel system; instead the model concerns the qualitative refinement of the breath and mind. As mindfulness stabilizes at the touching point, the breath progressively calms and becomes subtle (the bodily-formation is 'tranquilized'); the five hindrances are suppressed; rapture (pīti) and bliss (sukha) pervade the body; the gross breath may seem to disappear and is replaced by the luminous nimitta. The mind then 'settles' (ṭhapanā) and absorbs into jhāna. In the insight mode, attention observes the breath and body as a flux of arising-and-passing sensations, revealing impermanence. (2) Tibetan Vajrayāna (tantric subtle body / 'vajra body'): the body is visualized as transparent and composed of channels (rtsa / nāḍī), winds (rlung / prāṇa), and drops (thigle / bindu). Some 72,000 channels branch from three main ones (central, right, left). The mind 'rides' the winds 'like a rider on a horse' — so steadying and directing the breath/wind steadies and directs the mind. Through nine-round purification, vase breathing, and tummo, the practitioner clears the side channels, then draws the normally dispersed winds into the central channel and dissolves them at the chakras (especially navel and heart). Inner heat melts the drops, which descend and ascend through the central channel producing successive blisses, culminating in the experience of the very subtle wind-mind and clear light. Blockages (knotted chakras, winds trapped in the side channels, afflictive winds) are precisely what the practices aim to open and resolve. Imbalanced or forced wind-work can cause 'lung disorder' (rlung disease) — agitation, insomnia, energetic disturbance.
research.feltSenseAndTransitions: Across breath meditation, the felt arc moves from coarse to subtle. Early on, the breath is a tangible push of air at the nostrils or a clear rise-and-fall of the abdomen; the mind wanders and counting (one to ten) catches the wandering. As attention steadies, the breath naturally lengthens, slows, and softens; the body often feels cool, light, settled, and comfortable. In samatha development, the gross breath grows so fine it can seem to vanish — practitioners are told not to chase it but to keep awareness at the contact point. Rapture (pīti) may arise as tingling, waves, goosebumps, lightness, or energy currents, followed by a gentler, pervading bliss (sukha) and a quiet, unified, one-pointed stillness (ekaggatā). A luminous mental sign (nimitta) may appear — diffuse and unsteady at first (learning sign), then a stable bright orb or disc (counterpart sign) likened to cotton wool, a star, a pearl, mist, a wheel, or the midday sun — and merging with it is the felt transition into jhāna. In Zen, the felt center is the warm, grounded gathering of energy in the tanden and long settled exhalations. In Tibetan wind-work, the transitions are explicitly somatic: clearing and lightness from purification breathing; warmth/heat building below the navel that rises through the central channel in vase breathing and tummo; and waves of bliss as the drops melt and move. In insight (vipassanā), the felt sense becomes granular — sensations are experienced flickering, arising and dissolving moment to moment — which can include exhilarating clarity as well as challenging, destabilizing 'insight knowledge' stages (fear, dissolution, disenchantment) before equanimity.
visualMotifs: The luminous nimitta (counterpart sign): a bright orb, disc, or point of light at the nostril/upper-lip — described as cotton wool, a star, a string of pearls, a gem, mist, a wheel, or the clear bright midday sun.
The enso (Zen ink circle): a single brushstroke ring symbolizing the unified, empty, complete mind cultivated in zazen breathing.
Seated meditation iconography: the Buddha and patriarchs in cross-legged lotus posture, spine erect, hands in dhyana mudra — the canonical posture for breath meditation.

The Tibetan 'vajra body' diagram: a transparent human figure showing the three vertical channels (blue central, red right, white left) and the chakra-wheels strung along the central channel, with channel-petals radiating at each wheel.

Thangka and tantric charts mapping channels (rtsa), winds (rlung), and drops (thigle), often with seed-syllables and deities at the chakras (e.g., the AH-stroke flame at the navel, white HAM drop at the crown for tummo).
Color-coded subtle energy: blue/dark-blue for the central channel, red (solar/warmth) for the right, white (lunar/cooling, nourishing) for the left.
The wind-horse (lungta) prayer-flag motif: a horse carrying a jewel, symbolizing the vital wind (lung/prāṇa) and the 'mind riding the wind.'
Tummo inner-fire imagery: a small flame or heat at the navel center rising through the central channel, melting a white drop at the crown.
Vase/pot shape of the lower belly in kumbhaka 'vase breathing' visualization.
Breath depicted as natural, unforced movement (rising/falling abdomen) in modern Burmese and Plum Village teaching materials, emphasizing ordinariness over esoteric imagery.
research.attributionNotes: This is a living religious tradition, not merely a wellness technique — accuracy and respectful attribution matter. Key cautions: (1) Lineage transparency. Modern "mindfulness" and secular breath techniques descend directly from Buddhist anapanasati, transmitted through identifiable teachers — especially the Burmese Vipassanā lineage (Ledi Sayadaw → Saya Thetgyi → U Ba Khin → S.N. Goenka; and Mahasi Sayadaw) and the Thai Forest tradition. Scholars have documented a "cultural appropriation" critique: that mindfulness was first marketed as "the heart of Buddhism," then stripped of Buddhist framing and sold as a secular, goal-oriented, therapeutic technique. Presenting breath practice should acknowledge these Buddhist roots and name lineages rather than implying a generic or invented origin. (2) Do not conflate traditions. Theravāda anapanasati has NO chakra/channel/kundalini system — importing tantric or Hindu yogic subtle-body anatomy into Theravāda is a misrepresentation. Conversely, Tibetan tsa-lung/tummo are specifically Vajrayāna. The Indian (Hindu) nāḍī/chakra system (iḍā/piṅgalā/suṣumnā) is related but distinct from the Tibetan (rasanā/lalanā/avadhūtī) system; keep the terminologies and lineages separate. (3) Sacredness and restricted transmission. Tibetan completion-stage practices — vase breathing, tummo, the Six Yogas, deity visualization — are traditionally esoteric, requiring empowerment (wang), oral transmission (lung), and a qualified teacher. They are not meant to be performed casually from text alone; both for sacredness and for real safety (risk of rlung/wind imbalance). Apps should present these descriptively/educationally, not as do-it-yourself instructions. (4) Safety framing. Insight stages can be psychologically intense ("dark night"/dukkha-ñāṇa); forced breath retention can cause physical and energetic harm. (5) Spelling/transliteration. Be careful with diacritics and the Pali/Sanskrit distinction (ānāpānasati vs ānāpānasmṛti; jhāna vs dhyāna; sati vs smṛti; satipaṭṭhāna vs smṛtyupasthāna) and Tibetan Wylie vs phonetics (rlung = "lung", gtum mo = "tummo", rtsa = "tsa"). (6) Living teachers' framings (e.g., Thich Nhat Hanh's "16 Exercises of Mindful Breathing," Goenka's "Vipassana as taught by S.N. Goenka") are copyrighted/branded interpretations and should be attributed to their source rather than presented as anonymous tradition. Where sources disagree (e.g., samatha-first vs. samatha-vipassana-together; nostril vs. abdomen focus), this should be stated as contested rather than settled.

research.topic: Japanese Hara, Ki, and Zazen Breathing (with Seika Tanden, Kokyu, and Aikido's One-Point)
research.summary:
In Japanese contemplative and martial culture, the hara (腹, "belly/abdomen") is the lower abdomen treated as the body's vital center — the seat of life, balance, and ki (vital energy/breath). Within the hara lies the tanden (丹田), more precisely the seika tanden (臍下丹田, "tanden below the navel"), a focal point roughly 3–5 cm (about two finger-widths to two inches) below the navel and felt to extend three-dimensionally toward the lower back. Tanden is the Japanese reading of the Chinese dāntián ("cinnabar/elixir field"), a concept originating in Daoist internal alchemy (neidan); the term and its meditative use were transmitted to Japan with Buddhism and Chinese cultural influence from around the 6th century onward. A common distinction holds that hara names the whole lower-abdominal region while tanden/seika tanden names a more precise point within it, though the two words are frequently used interchangeably.

Ki (気, the Japanese rendering of Chinese qì) is the life-force or vital breath believed to animate all things and to be gathered, stored, and circulated through breathing and posture centered on the hara. In Zen, regulated abdominal breathing (fukushiki kokyu) centered on the tanden is foundational to zazen (seated meditation). Sōtō Zen, following Dōgen's Fukanzazengi, emphasizes natural, uncontrived breathing that "reaches the tanden and leaves from there"; Rinzai Zen, systematized by Hakuin Ekaku (1686–1769), uses breath-counting (susokukan) and energetic cultivation of the tanden, and Hakuin's Yasenkanna ("Idle Talk on a Night Boat") preserves the naikan introspective method and "soft-butter" visualization he credited with curing his "Zen sickness" (zenbyō). In aikido and other budō, the same center appears as the "one point" (seika no itten); Koichi Tohei's Ki-Aikido (Shin Shin Tōitsu Aikidō) built an entire system of "ki breathing" and mind-body unification around keeping one-point, relaxing completely, keeping weight underside, and extending ki.

Across these traditions the hara is not primarily a medical or anatomical claim but a felt, cultivated center: practitioners are told to let the breath sink low and settle, to "move from the hara," and to experience grounded stability, calm, and a sense of energetic fullness or warmth below the navel. The 20th-century German diplomat-scholar Karlfried Graf Dürckheim popularized "Hara" in the West as a posture and way of being centered in the belly rather than the chest.

This capture presents these as living beliefs and disciplines, with attention to where sources agree, where they diverge (e.g., Sōtō "non-seeking" breath vs. Rinzai active tanden-cultivation), and to respectful attribution of lineage.
research.historyAndOrigin:
The conceptual root is the Chinese dāntián (丹田, "cinnabar/elixir field"), first described in Daoist self-cultivation and internal-alchemy (neidan) literature. The lower dantian is detailed in the Laozi zhongjing (Central Scripture of Laozi, c. 3rd century CE) and the term appears in the Huangtingjing (Yellow Court Classic, c. Wei dynasty, 220–265 CE) as the field where "essence" (jing) and "spirit" (shen) are stored and refined. The associated notion of qì (vital energy/breath) traces to early Chinese medical and philosophical texts such as the Huangdi Neijing (Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon).

These ideas reached Japan through cultural and Buddhist transmission (commonly dated from around the 6th century CE onward), where dāntián became tanden (丹田) and qì became ki (気). The hara concept was absorbed into Zen Buddhism, Japanese medicine, and the martial arts (budō). Japanese tanden is essentially a direct adoption of the Chinese term and concept rather than a fundamentally different system, though it developed its own emphases (e.g., seika tanden, seika no itten).

Key Japanese lineage figures: Dōgen Zenji (1200–1253), founder of Sōtō Zen, whose Fukanzazengi gives the canonical zazen instructions on posture and breathing; Keizan Jōkin (1264–1325), whose Zazen Yōjinki advises placing attention at the tanden and counting breaths when the mind wanders; and Hakuin Ekaku (1686–1769), reviver of Rinzai Zen, who systematized koan practice and breath/energy cultivation of the tanden and recorded the naikan and "soft-butter" methods in Yasenkanna. In the 20th century, Koichi Tohei (1920–2011) codified ki and one-point principles for aikido in his Ki Society (Shin Shin Tōitsu Aikidō), and Karlfried Graf Dürckheim (1896–1988), who spent years in pre-war Japan, introduced "Hara" to Western audiences in his 1956 book Hara: The Vital Centre of Man.

coreConcepts.meaning: The lower abdomen treated as the vital center of the human being — center of gravity, balance, life-force, and emotional/spiritual grounding. To 'have hara' or 'move from the hara' is to be centered, calm, and grounded. Broader region than the single tanden point.

coreConcepts.aliases: 丹田; Chinese dāntián / dan tien; Wade-Giles tan t'ian; Korean danjeon; pinyin dāntián; "cinnabar field" / "elixir field" / "energy field"
coreConcepts.meaning: A focal energy center in the body. In Japanese usage usually refers to the lower point within the hara where ki is gathered, stored, and refined. The kanji evoke the Daoist alchemical 'cinnabar/elixir field.'

coreConcepts.aliases: 臍下丹田 (seika = "below the navel"); often shortened to tanden
coreConcepts.meaning: The 'tanden below the navel' — the precise lower center located roughly two finger-widths to about two inches (3–5 cm) below the navel, extending three-dimensionally back toward the lower back/koshi. The classic locus of hara breathing and ki cultivation.

coreConcepts.aliases: 臍下の一点; "the one point below the navel"; "one-point" / "the One Point"
coreConcepts.meaning: The martial-arts / Ki-Aikido name for the same lower center, treated as both physical and mental center of balance. Central to Koichi Tohei's Ki principles ('keep one point').

coreConcepts.aliases: 気/氣; Chinese qì (chi); Korean gi
coreConcepts.meaning: Vital energy, life-force, or vital breath believed to flow through and animate all things and the body; gathered and circulated via hara-centered breathing. Closely tied to breath (the same character appears in kokyu).

coreConcepts.aliases: 呼吸; "breath / breathing"; also kokyū-hō (breath method); kokyū-ryoku (breath-power)
coreConcepts.meaning: Breath/breathing as a cultivated discipline. In zazen and budō, 'kokyu' denotes regulated breathing centered on the tanden; in aikido 'kokyu-ryoku' is 'breath-power,' the unified power expressed through coordinated breath and ki.

Deep abdominal (belly) breathing — the foundational breathing mode for zazen and ki cultivation, in which the lower abdomen expands and the breath is felt to settle into the tanden rather than the chest.

coreConcepts.aliases: 数息観; "breath-counting meditation"; also susokkan; cf. zuisokukan 随息観 ("following the breath")
coreConcepts.meaning: Rinzai Zen breath-counting practice: counting exhalations (often to ten and repeating) while gathering ki in the tanden. Said to have 'six wonderful gates' (counting, following, stopping, contemplating, returning, purifying).

In Hakuin's Yasenkanna, an introspective meditation using abdominal breathing and concentration on the hara to gather ki in the lower body and form the 'true elixir' (shintan). (Distinct from the modern Yoshimoto Naikan reflective therapy.)
Sōtō Zen's objectless seated meditation in which breathing is left natural and non-seeking rather than counted or manipulated.

coreConcepts.aliases: 欠気一息; the opening deep breath of zazen
coreConcepts.meaning: At the start of zazen, a deliberate deep exhalation and inhalation (mouth slightly open to exhale) to settle the body before letting breathing become natural through the nose.

A Daoist-derived map of three centers: lower (jing refined to qi, in the lower belly), middle (qi refined to shen, at the heart), upper (shen refined to emptiness, between the brows). Japanese practice overwhelmingly emphasizes the lower (seika) tanden.

energyCenters.name: Lower tanden / seika tanden (hara)
energyCenters.location: Lower abdomen, roughly 2 finger-widths to ~2 inches (3–5 cm) below the navel, deep inside toward the spine; felt to extend three-dimensionally back to the lower back (koshi).
energyCenters.meaning: The primary energy center of Japanese practice: seat of ki, physical and mental center of gravity/balance, and the place where breath is settled and ki gathered and refined. In Daoist terms, where jing (essence) is refined into qi.
energyCenters.associations: Element: traditionally water/earth in Chinese five-phase schemes (lower abdomen); color: cinnabar/red (from 'cinnabar field,' dan = vermilion) is the inherited alchemical color, though Japanese Zen rarely color-codes it; symbol/geometry: a point, ball, or sphere ('a great rolling ball'), and the One Point; sound/seed-syllable: not assigned a fixed bīja in Zen practice (Japanese hara work is generally non-mantric, unlike Indian chakra systems); deity: none specific in Zen, though Daoist sources personify the centers. Felt-quality: warmth, weight, fullness, rootedness.

energyCenters.name: Middle dantian ("crimson palace")
energyCenters.location: Center of the chest, at the level of the heart.
energyCenters.meaning: In the inherited Daoist three-field model, where qi is refined into shen (spirit). Rarely emphasized in Japanese Zen or budō, which center on the lower tanden.
energyCenters.associations: Heart/chest region; color crimson; refinement of qi to spirit. Largely a Chinese neidan concept carried in the background of the tanden vocabulary.

energyCenters.name: Upper dantian ("third eye")
energyCenters.meaning: In Daoist alchemy, where shen (spirit) is transmuted toward wu wei / emptiness. Present in the inherited terminology but not a focus of standard hara/zazen practice.
energyCenters.associations: Brow point; emptiness; spirit. Chinese-derived; peripheral to Japanese hara-centered practice.

research.energyFlowModel:
The dominant Japanese model is one of settling and gathering rather than elaborate channel-circulation. In hara breathing, the breath is felt to 'sink' from the chest down into the lower abdomen and to settle/store in the seika tanden, as if the throat and tanden were connected by 'an open pipe.' Ki is understood to accumulate and consolidate there (strengthening the 'Sea of Qi'), producing rootedness, calm, and available power. In Rinzai susokukan, breath-counting is explicitly 'the primary means by which we gather the ki in the tanden,' progressively densifying energy at the center; Hakuin's naikan deliberately drives the 'heart' (mind/energy) and all bodily congestion downward into the lower body to form the 'true elixir' (shintan), curing the upward-floating, head-heavy imbalance of 'Zen sickness.'

A circulatory model is inherited from the Daoist/neidan background that the vocabulary comes from: a three-field refinement (jing→qi→shen→emptiness, lower→middle→upper), and in qigong the 'microcosmic orbit' circulating qi up the spine and down the front. Aikido's Tohei adds a directional breathing image: on inhalation the breath is felt to enter at the tip of the nose, travel up the bridge of the nose, between the eyes, down the spine, and fill the body from toes to crown, then calm 'infinitely' at the one point; on exhalation it is sent out 'infinitely to the end of the Universe.' Common to all: energy should sink and settle low; blockage or imbalance is felt as energy rising or 'floating' into the chest/head (tension, anxiety, the 'chest-out, belly-in' Western posture Dürckheim criticized). The Sōtō counter-emphasis is that the breath should not be forced or steered at all — it 'reaches the tanden and leaves from there' naturally ('non-seeking breath').
research.feltSenseAndTransitions:
Practitioners are told the tanden is 'elusive at first' — one begins by simply imagining 'something down there, a couple of centimeters below the navel, extending three-dimensionally through to the low back,' and it is often easier to first feel it in the lower back (koshi) than in the front of the belly. The awakening of the center 'begins often as a little flutter, or a little heat in the general area below the navel,' developing over time into a steady sense of warmth, weight, density, and fullness — sometimes imaged as 'a great rolling ball.'

On the breath itself: the inhalation should fill and gently expand the lower belly; the exhalation should feel as though it 'comes not from the chest but directly from the lower abdomen,' long, slow, and quiet. Rinzai instruction is to begin with an exhalation (starting with an inhalation 'tends to stiffen the body'), exhaling completely. In Tohei's ki breathing, exhalation is gradual and barely audible, 'Ki continues infinitely like a fading note,' lengthening from ~10 seconds for beginners to 20–30 seconds with practice; inhalation is from the tip of the nose 'until the body is saturated with breath,' after which one 'calms the mind infinitely at the One Point.' The overall felt transition is from scattered, chest-high, head-heavy tension toward a low, grounded, settled stability — calm mind, relaxed body, weight 'underside,' and a sense of being centered and rooted. At depth, traditions describe absorption (samadhi) and, in Tohei's framing, a felt unity 'One with the Universe.' The undesirable transition to watch for is energy 'rising' or floating upward (agitation, lightheadedness), which the practices aim to reverse by drawing attention and breath back down to the hara.

practices.name: Zazen with abdominal (tanden) breathing — Sōtō style
practices.aliases: shikantaza; fukushiki kokyu; per Dōgen's Fukanzazengi
practices.tradition: Sōtō Zen Buddhism
practices.description: Seated meditation in which breathing is left natural and uncontrived, settling into the lower abdomen/tanden.
practices.howPracticed: Sit upright (full/half lotus or seiza) on a zafu, spine straight, ears in line with shoulders, nose aligned with navel. Hands in the cosmic mudra (hokkai-jō-in): right hand palm-up under the left, left palm-up on the right, thumb tips lightly touching near the navel. Eyes half-open, cast down ~45°. Mouth closed, tongue against the palate behind the upper teeth. Begin with kanki-issoku: a deliberate deep exhale and inhale (mouth slightly open to exhale), then breathe quietly through the nose and 'do not try to control your breathing — let it come and go so naturally that you forget you are breathing.' The breath 'reaches the tanden and leaves from there.'
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: To drop discriminating thought and abide in 'just sitting'; calm, settled, non-seeking awareness; embodiment of buddha-nature in posture and breath.
practices.feltExperience: Stable, grounded, low-centered; breath felt low in the belly; a quiet, settled, non-grasping awareness rather than any engineered energetic sensation.
practices.safetyNotes: Approach knees/hips gently; avoid forcing the legs into lotus. Because Sōtō stresses non-manipulation, deliberate breath-forcing is discouraged. General caution: do not strain the breath.

practices.name: Susokukan — breath-counting meditation (Rinzai)
practices.aliases: susokkan 数息観; cf. zuisokukan 随息観 (following the breath)
practices.tradition: Rinzai Zen Buddhism (systematized by Hakuin Ekaku)
practices.description: Counting the breath while gathering ki in the tanden; the basic Rinzai entry practice and a means to develop the tanden and enter samadhi.
practices.howPracticed: In zazen posture, center respiration in the tanden and begin with an exhalation. For the first several breaths exhale completely through the mouth; thereafter breathe through the nose. Count: commonly each exhalation counts one, up to ten, then start again at one (some count a full inhale+exhale cycle as one). If the count is lost, return to one. The exhale should feel as if coming directly from the lower abdomen, 'as though there were an open pipe directly connecting the tanden and the mouth.' Traditionally unfolds through 'six wonderful gates': su (counting), zui (following), shi (stopping/one-pointedness), and three further stages (contemplating, returning, purifying).
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: To gather and concentrate ki in the tanden, settle and cleanse the mind, develop the tanden, and approach samadhi — a foundation for koan work.
practices.feltExperience: At first the tanden is hard to feel — imagine a point a few cm below the navel reaching to the low back; it may first register as a flutter or warmth, sometimes more in the lower back than the belly, developing into steady warmth and fullness; mind becomes one-pointed and calm.
practices.safetyNotes: Do not strain to lengthen or hold the breath; over-forcing energy upward or over-concentration was historically linked to 'Zen sickness' (see Hakuin). Best learned under a qualified teacher.

practices.name: Naikan introspective method (Hakuin)
practices.aliases: 内観法; from Yasenkanna ("Idle Talk on a Night Boat")
practices.tradition: Rinzai Zen Buddhism
practices.description: An introspective concentration using abdominal breathing and focus on the hara to drive ki and 'heart' down into the lower body, forming the 'true elixir' (shintan).
practices.howPracticed: Lying down or seated, breathe abdominally and concentrate awareness on the seika tanden / lower belly, repeatedly bringing the mind and vital energy 'down into your lower body,' filling the lower abdomen and loins with ki until it feels firm and full (like a 'leather ball that has never been kicked'). Often paired with affirmative contemplations on the lower body as the seat of one's original life.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Hakuin taught it to cure 'Zen sickness' (zenbyō) — the agitated, head-heavy, energy-floating-upward breakdown from over-intense practice — and to build robust health and concentrated ki as a basis for Zen.
practices.feltExperience: A downward sinking and settling of energy from head/chest into the belly and lower body; warmth, fullness, and firmness in the lower abdomen; relief of upward agitation.
practices.safetyNotes: Hakuin presented it precisely as a remedy for over-exertion; it is a corrective for forcing practice. Learn from a teacher; do not self-diagnose serious conditions with it.

practices.name: Soft-butter method (nansō no hō)
practices.aliases: 軟酥の法; "soft-ointment" / "soft butter" visualization; from Yasenkanna
practices.tradition: Rinzai Zen Buddhism (taught by Hakuin, attributed to the hermit Hakuyū)
practices.description: A healing visualization that melts tension and 'sinks' energy from the crown down through the body into the lower abdomen.
practices.howPracticed: Imagine a lump of fragrant, soft butter (or ointment), about the size of a duck egg, resting on the crown of the head. With its exquisite color and scent it slowly melts, its warmth flowing down to moisten the head, then oozing to the shoulders, elbows, chest, lungs, diaphragm, liver, stomach and bowels, down the spine through hips, pelvis and buttocks. All congestion, aches, and pains 'follow the heart as it sinks downward into the lower body,' pooling warmth in the lower abdomen and legs.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Deep relaxation, release of tension/illness, and settling of ki into the lower body; part of Hakuin's cure for Zen sickness.
practices.feltExperience: A spreading warm, melting, descending sensation from the top of the head downward; relaxation of each region as the warmth passes; warmth and fullness gathering in the belly and lower body.
practices.safetyNotes: Gentle and low-risk as a relaxation visualization; not a substitute for medical care for real illness.

practices.name: Ki breathing (ki no kokyu-hō) — Tohei / Ki-Aikido
practices.aliases: 気の呼吸; ki breathing method; within Shin Shin Tōitsu Aikidō (Ki-Aikido)
practices.tradition: Aikido / budō (Koichi Tohei's Ki Society)
practices.description: A mind-body unification breathing practice centered on keeping the One Point and relaxing completely; aims to relax, not to train lung capacity.
practices.howPracticed: Sit calmly (often seiza), keep one-point at the lower abdomen, relax completely, keep weight underside. Exhale gradually and with control through the mouth, with a distinct but barely audible sound; let the breath taper so 'Ki continues infinitely like a fading note' (~10 sec for beginners, lengthening to 20–30 sec). Then inhale from the tip of the nose 'until the body is saturated,' imagining the breath traveling up the bridge of the nose, between the eyes, down the spine, filling the body from toes to crown; finish by calming the mind 'infinitely at the One Point.' Imagery: exhale to the end of the universe; inhale to the one point and continue infinitely there. Best practiced alone in a quiet place.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Unify mind and body, deepen calm and relaxation, cultivate and extend ki, develop kokyu-ryoku (breath-power) for aikido technique.
practices.feltExperience: Lengthening, effortless breath; deep relaxation; a stable, settled one-point; in Tohei's framing, a felt sense of oneness with the universe.
practices.safetyNotes: Breathe naturally; the explicit instruction is not to strain for lung capacity but to relax — over-effort defeats the purpose.

practices.name: Keeping One Point (seika no itten) in motion
practices.aliases: keep one-point; one of Tohei's four basic principles
practices.tradition: Aikido / budō
practices.description: A standing/moving discipline of keeping attention and balance at the lower abdomen during movement and technique, paired with relaxing completely, keeping weight underside, and extending ki.
practices.howPracticed: Maintain awareness at the one-point (a few cm below the navel) while standing, walking, and executing techniques; let the breath stay low; tested through partner exercises (e.g., 'unbendable arm,' 'unraisable body') that demonstrate stability when centered and relaxed.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Rooted stability, relaxed power, and the ability to harmonize with and redirect an attacker's energy ('moving from the hara').
practices.feltExperience: Grounded, heavy-bottomed stability; relaxed but unshakeable; power that flows from the center rather than from muscular tension.
practices.safetyNotes: Standard martial-training cautions; learn under a qualified instructor.

visualMotifs: Ensō (円相): the hand-drawn Zen circle, often in one breath/one stroke, expressing no-mind, emptiness, and the cycle of inhalation/exhalation (in on the first half, out on the second); an open ensō suggests breath/spirit flowing in and out.
Sumi-e ink and brush on washi paper: black ink (sumi), single fluid uninhibited strokes done 'in one breath' as a direct trace of ki and the artist's state of mind.
The tanden depicted as a point, sphere, or 'great rolling ball' low in the belly — a glowing or warm center of gravity.
Cinnabar/vermilion red — the inherited color of the 'cinnabar field' (dan 丹) from Daoist alchemy, occasionally used to mark the lower center.
Three-field diagrams (lower/middle/upper dantian) inherited from Chinese neidan, with the lower field in the abdomen, middle at the heart ('crimson palace'), upper between the brows.
Daoist 'inner landscape' body diagrams (Neijing tu style) showing the abdomen as a field/furnace where energy is refined — background imagery for the tanden vocabulary.
Posture iconography: the upright seated meditator with the cosmic mudra (hokkai-jō-in) at the navel, settled low — the visual emblem of a centered hara.
Calligraphic characters themselves — 気/氣 (ki), 丹田 (tanden), 円相 (ensō) — written expressively as ki-laden brushwork.
research.attributionNotes: These practices belong to living Japanese Buddhist (Sōtō and Rinzai Zen), martial-arts (budō/aikido), and broader East Asian traditions, themselves rooted in Chinese Daoist and medical thought. Present them as beliefs and disciplines within their lineages, not as medical or scientific facts about anatomy or energy; the app's framing should make clear that ki, hara, and tanden are experiential/traditional concepts. Attribute accurately: the lower-dantian/cinnabar-field concept is Chinese Daoist (neidan) in origin; tanden/ki are the Japanese readings and adaptations — do not present the hara as an indigenous Japanese 'invention' divorced from its Chinese source, nor conflate it directly with the Indian chakra system (the hara is non-mantric and has no fixed bīja syllables; mapping it onto a sacral or root chakra is a modern Western syncretism that should be flagged, not asserted). Honor specific lineage attributions: Fukanzazengi to Dōgen; Zazen Yōjinki to Keizan; susokukan, naikan, and the soft-butter method to Hakuin Ekaku (via Yasenkanna, with the legendary hermit Hakuyū); the One Point / ki principles and 'ki breathing' to Koichi Tohei and the Ki Society; and the Western popularization of 'Hara' to Karlfried Graf Dürckheim. Use correct transliterations and macrons where possible (Dōgen, Yasenkanna, kokyū, seika). Areas that are contested or vary by school should be marked as such: Sōtō Zen's 'non-seeking,' uncontrived breath versus Rinzai's active tanden-cultivation and breath-counting; the hara-vs-tanden 'whole region vs. single point' distinction (often blurred); and the exact location below the navel (sources give anywhere from ~2 finger-widths to ~2 inches / 3–5 cm). Some Zen teachers (e.g., Takuan Sōhō) explicitly warn that fixating the mind below the navel is 'a low level of understanding,' so do not present tanden-fixation as universally endorsed. Avoid medical claims (e.g., that hara breathing cures disease) — Hakuin's healing claims should be reported as tradition, not endorsed. Treat the material respectfully as sacred/serious practice rather than wellness decoration.
