research.topic: Sufi and Islamic Breath Practices: Nafas, Dhikr, the Lataif, the Breath of the Merciful, and Sufi Breathing
research.summary:
In Islamic and Sufi (tasawwuf) thought, breath is not merely respiration but a sacred link between the human being and the divine. The vocabulary itself carries this: nafas (breath) is cognate with nafs (self/ego-soul), and ruh (spirit) derives from a root tied to breath and wind (r-w-h). The Qur'an grounds this when God "breathes" (nafakha) of His spirit into Adam (Qur'an 15:29; 38:72), and declares the spirit a matter "from the command of my Lord" whose nature is largely withheld from human knowledge (17:85). Across traditions, conscious breathing becomes a vehicle for dhikr (remembrance of God): the in-and-out of the breath is heard as the silent recitation of the divine Name "Hu" (He/the Absolute), and the practitioner is taught never to draw a breath in heedlessness (ghafla).

Two complementary streams dominate the topic. First, the cosmological vision of Ibn 'Arabi (1165-1240), in which the entire universe is articulated within the Nafas al-Rahman, the "Breath of the All-Merciful": as we form words upon our breath, God deploys existence (inbisat al-wujud) by breathing out the divine Names, creating, sustaining and renewing the cosmos at every instant. Second, the practical disciplines of the orders (tariqas): the Naqshbandi principle Hosh dar dam ("awareness in the breath"), the Kubrawi/Simnani and Naqshbandi-Mujaddidi systems of the lataif (subtle centers) activated through breath-borne dhikr, breath-retention practices (habs-i-dam), and the elemental "purification breaths" taught in the modern Inayat Khan (Inayatiyya) lineage.

Energetically, breath is understood to circulate the divine Name through the subtle body. In the negation-and-affirmation dhikr (nafy wa ithbat), the formula "La ilaha illa Allah" is driven on the breath from the navel, up and across the chest, and "struck" into the physical heart (qalb), awakening a graded series of subtle centers in the chest. Each latifa is associated with a color (a "photism" seen in vision), a body location, and often a prophet. The states of qabd (contraction) and bast (expansion) — themselves two of God's Names, al-Qabid and al-Basit — give a felt model of spiritual inhalation and exhalation.

These are living, initiatic traditions. Authentic transmission almost universally requires a qualified shaykh and an authorized silsila (chain); the lataif and breath-retention practices in particular are taught one-to-one and are considered unsafe or ineffective if extracted from their devotional and ethical context. Sources differ on details (color assignments, number of lataif, whether breath is held), and these differences are noted as genuinely contested between lineages.
research.historyAndOrigin:
Roots are Qur'anic and prophetic: the divine in-breathing of the spirit into Adam (Qur'an 15:28-29, 38:71-72) and the Qur'anic statement that "the Spirit (al-ruh) is from the command of my Lord" (17:85). Early Sufis such as Junayd of Baghdad (d. 910) and al-Hallaj (d. 922) developed notions of inner spiritual centers and the breath of remembrance.

Cosmological strand: Ibn 'Arabi (b. Murcia 1165, d. Damascus 1240), the "Shaykh al-Akbar," in al-Futuhat al-Makkiyya and Fusus al-Hikam, articulated the Nafas al-Rahman (Breath of the All-Merciful) as the medium of creation. This was later commented on by the Akbari school (e.g., al-Qunawi, Jami).

Subtle-centers (lataif) strand: an early notion of the heart's faculties is traced to al-Hakim al-Tirmidhi (9th c.). The systematic color-and-vision teaching was developed in the Kubrawiyya order founded by Najm al-Din Kubra (d. 1221), elaborated by his successor Najm al-Din Razi (d. 1256, Mirsad al-'ibad), and most fully systematized by 'Ala al-Dawla Simnani (1261-1336), who mapped seven subtle centers (lataif) each to a color (photism) and a prophet, including the famous "black light" (nur-e-siyah) of the Essence. The five-center chest-based system used today was standardized within the Naqshbandi order, especially by Ahmad Sirhindi (1564-1624, "Mujaddid alf-i thani"), giving the Naqshbandi-Mujaddidi lineage. The breath principles of the Naqshbandi path (the eight, later eleven, principles) descend from the Khwajagan masters, notably 'Abd al-Khaliq al-Ghujdawani (d. ~1179) and Baha al-Din Naqshband (1318-1389).

Whirling strand: the Mevlevi order, founded after Jalal al-Din Rumi (1207-1273) in Konya, developed the Sema ceremony, whose form was finalized in the 15th-16th centuries.

Modern transmission to the West: Hazrat Inayat Khan (1882-1927), a trained Indian (Chishti) musician and Sufi, brought a universalist Sufi message to Europe/America from 1910; his elemental "purification breaths" are practiced today in the Inayatiyya / Sufi Order / Ruhaniat / Dervish Healing Order lineages, continued by Pir Vilayat Inayat Khan, Pir Zia Inayat Khan, and Murshid Hidayat Inayat-Khan. Idries Shah (1924-1996) popularized a simplified lataif model in the 20th century.

coreConcepts.meaning: Breath / a single breath. Cognate with nafs. In Sufi practice the unit of spiritual attention; 'awareness in the breath' means not letting a single breath pass in heedlessness.

coreConcepts.aliases: nafs (self, ego-soul, lower self); nafs al-ammara (the commanding self), lawwama, mutma'inna
coreConcepts.meaning: The self or ego-soul, etymologically rooted in 'breath.' Seen as needing purification (tazkiyat al-nafs); the lower nafs is what dhikr and breath-discipline aim to refine and transform.

coreConcepts.aliases: ruh (spirit); pl. arwah; root r-w-h tied to wind/breath (rih = wind)
coreConcepts.meaning: The divine spirit breathed by God into the human being; immaterial, animating, 'from the command of God.' Distinguished from nafs: the ruh drives and gives life, the nafs is the seat of ego and appetite.

coreConcepts.aliases: Breath of the All-Merciful / Breath of the Compassionate; nafas al-rahmani; nafas ar-Rahman
coreConcepts.meaning: Ibn 'Arabi's cosmological doctrine: existence is 'breathed out' by God. As speech is formed on human breath, God articulates the universe by uttering the divine Names within His Breath (inbisat al-wujud = the deployment of existence). Existence itself equals mercy (rahma).

coreConcepts.aliases: zikr; dhikr Allah (remembrance of God); dhikr khafi/qalbi (silent/heart dhikr); dhikr jali (vocal)
coreConcepts.meaning: Remembrance/invocation of God by repeating a Name or formula, classically synchronized with the breath. Silent heart-dhikr is the Naqshbandi specialty; vocal dhikr is used in many other orders.

The divine Name 'He'/the Absolute Essence beyond all attributes. The 'h' sound of every exhalation is heard as Hu; commonly paired as inhale 'Allah', exhale 'Hu'. In Mevlevi Sema the collective 'Hu' represents the divine breath insufflated into the human being.

coreConcepts.aliases: the tahlil; kalima; nafy wa ithbat (negation and affirmation)
coreConcepts.meaning: 'There is no god but God.' The principal dhikr formula moved through the body on the breath: 'la ilaha' negates all but God, 'illa Allah' affirms God, driven into the heart.

coreConcepts.aliases: lata'if; sing. latifa; lataif-e-sitta (the six subtleties); lata'if-i khamsa (five)
coreConcepts.meaning: Subtle spiritual centers/organs of perception, mostly located in the chest, awakened in sequence; each linked to a color, location, and (in some systems) a prophet.

coreConcepts.aliases: hush dar dam; 'awareness/consciousness in the breath'
coreConcepts.meaning: First principle of the Naqshbandi path (from the Khwajagan): every inhalation and exhalation, and the pause between, must be done in conscious presence of God; the foundation of the work is in the breath.

Restraining/holding the breath while repeating the dhikr internally. Practiced in some Kubrawi and Indian Sufi (e.g., Qadiri-Shattari) settings; notably NOT obligated by Baha al-Din Naqshband, who required heart-awareness (wuquf-i qalbi) instead.

coreConcepts.aliases: qabd (contraction), bast (expansion); divine Names al-Qabid, al-Basit
coreConcepts.meaning: Paired spiritual states of contraction/withholding and expansion/opening (Qur'an 2:245), experienced as a felt inhalation-exhalation of the soul; two of the 99 Names of God.

Muraqaba = sustained meditative attention on a latifa; tawajjuh = the shaykh's directed attention transmitting the activation of a center to the disciple. Primary methods, alongside dhikr, for awakening the lataif.

coreConcepts.aliases: al-insan al-kamil; the Complete/Perfect Human Being; kamil insan
coreConcepts.meaning: The goal of the path: the fully realized human who mirrors all divine Names; the lataif work and Sema turning are framed as steps toward this realization.

practices.name: Negation-and-Affirmation Dhikr (nafy wa ithbat) on the breath
practices.aliases: dhikr of La ilaha illa Allah; the four-beat dhikr (Kubrawi/Simnani method)
practices.tradition: Kubrawi and Naqshbandi; described by 'Ala al-Dawla Simnani (14th c.) and 'Ali Hamadani
practices.description: The formula 'La ilaha illa Allah' is divided into four beats and moved through the upper body coordinated with breath, to 'strike' the affirmation of God into the physical heart and dissolve attachment to all-but-God.
practices.howPracticed: Classic Kubrawi pattern (Simnani): with full strength exhale 'la' upward from above the navel; inhale 'ilaha' to the right side of the chest; exhale 'illa' from right across to the left; inhale 'Allah' driving it into the physical heart on the left. The whole formula is said in a single connected breath (some adepts hold the breath, habs-i-dam, instead). A common added image: 'la ilaha' negates, then the head jerks down toward the heart on 'illa Allah,' imprinting the Name. Repeated in counts prescribed by the shaykh (often odd numbers), progressing toward synchronization with the heartbeat.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: To purify the nafs, 'polish' and awaken the heart (qalb), negate idols of the ego, and establish ceaseless remembrance until the dhikr continues by itself, even in sleep.
practices.feltExperience: A felt 'striking' or warmth in the heart; the breath and heartbeat gradually merging with the Name; movement from contraction to a settled, luminous interior presence. Practitioners report the dhikr becoming self-sustaining.
practices.safetyNotes: Traditionally taught only after initiation (bay'a) and under a living, authorized shaykh; the count, breath-hold, and pacing are individually prescribed. Done incorrectly or without guidance it is considered spiritually unsafe and prone to ego-inflation.

practices.name: Silent Heart Dhikr (dhikr khafi / dhikr al-qalb)
practices.aliases: dhikr-i qalbi; hidden remembrance
practices.tradition: Naqshbandi (and Naqshbandi-Mujaddidi)
practices.description: Wholly internal, breath-borne repetition of the Name 'Allah' or the tahlil within the heart, without sound or movement, emphasizing presence over visionary experience.
practices.howPracticed: The practitioner sits, focuses on the qalb (left chest), and with each breath silently brings the Name to the heart according to the count given by the shaykh, maintaining wuquf-i qalbi (awareness of the heart). Inhale and exhale are kept conscious (Hosh dar dam); the Name is 'returned' to God (baz gasht) and intrusive thoughts are watched without engagement (nigah dasht).
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Continuous remembrance, annihilation of ego (fana), and a heart that 'recites' on its own; the Naqshbandi path deliberately strips away spectacular states in favor of sober presence.
practices.feltExperience: A quiet, sober interiority; awareness centered in the heartbeat/chest; presence rather than visions; the sense that the heart itself is doing the dhikr.
practices.safetyNotes: Requires authorization and a heart-link (rabita/tawajjuh) to the shaykh. Naqshbandis explicitly do not require breath-holding here, only conscious breath and heart-awareness.

practices.name: Hosh dar dam (Awareness in the Breath)
practices.aliases: hush dar dam; conscious breathing
practices.tradition: Naqshbandi Khwajagan (from 'Abd al-Khaliq al-Ghujdawani; emphasized by Baha al-Din Naqshband)
practices.description: A continuous discipline (not a fixed sitting) of bringing full divine presence to every inhalation, exhalation, and the interval between, so that no breath passes in heedlessness.
practices.howPracticed: Throughout daily life, attend to each breath, keeping it 'occupied with the Divine Presence'; guard the breath in inhalation, exhalation, and its pauses. Baha al-Din: 'The foundation of our work is in the breath; the more conscious one is of breathing, the stronger is one's inner life.'
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: To keep the heart alive and present; Ghujdawani warned that breath taken 'without God's Presence in it leads to heedlessness and death of the heart.'
practices.feltExperience: A settling into the present moment; the breath felt as the living thread connecting body to spirit; release of past-and-future preoccupation.
practices.safetyNotes: Generally safe as a presence practice; the deeper retention/circulation work attached to it is reserved for guided students.

practices.name: Elemental Purification Breaths
practices.aliases: purification breaths; element breaths; tahara through the five elements
practices.tradition: Inayat Khan lineage (Inayatiyya / Sufi Order / Ruhaniat / Dervish Healing Order); element model rooted in classical fivefold cosmology
practices.description: A morning practice of five sets of five breaths, each set keyed to an element (earth, water, fire, air, ether), with a specific nose/mouth pattern and visualized color, to purify and balance the elemental constitution of the body.
practices.howPracticed: Stand upright, feet slightly apart, palms forward, ideally at an open window after washing and morning prayers. Earth: in nose / out nose x5, visualize gold (some sources: yellow). Water: in nose / out mouth x5, visualize green. Fire: in mouth / out nose x5, visualize red. Air: in mouth / out mouth x5, visualize blue. Ether: in nose / out nose, very refined x5, visualize grey or transparency. Optionally add 'Ya Shafee' (the Healer) on the inhale and 'Ya Kafee' (the Remedy) on the exhale.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: To attract the elements the body needs and expel what is undesirable in body and mind; Inayat Khan framed the breath as carrying 'Prana,' the life-essence ('in fact God Himself'), to revivify, heal and purify the subtle centers. Tasawwuf is itself glossed as 'purification.'
practices.feltExperience: Each element is felt as a distinct quality — earth: grounded stability; water: flowing, cleansing; fire: warming, energizing; air: light, expansive freedom; ether: refined, spacious peace. The practice should become a natural daily rhythm 'like winding a clock.'
practices.safetyNotes: Comparatively gentle and self-practicable, but taught as part of a guided curriculum; color and breath-direction assignments vary slightly between sub-lineages (see attribution notes).

practices.name: Mevlevi Sema (Whirling)
practices.aliases: sema; whirling dervishes; the Turn
practices.tradition: Mevlevi order (after Rumi, Konya)
practices.description: A ceremonial turning meditation in which the breath, the Name 'Hu,' and the body's rotation enact the soul's journey toward union; not primarily a breath-control technique but an embodied breath/remembrance ritual.
practices.howPracticed: Initiated by the breath of the ney (reed flute), whose sound symbolizes the divine breath insufflated into humanity. The dervish turns with the right palm up (receiving divine grace/breath) and the left palm down (transmitting it to the earth), circling the heart. The ceremony's stages move through love, vision and certainty toward union, closing with a collective 'Hu.'
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: To become the 'perfect human' (kamil insan); the turning symbolizes creation, resurrection and divine love; breath and remembrance unify the turner with the cosmic order.
practices.feltExperience: Centeredness amid motion; the body as a conduit between heaven and earth; loss of self-preoccupation into rhythmic remembrance.
practices.safetyNotes: Highly disciplined, learned over long training within the order; sacred and not to be reduced to performance/entertainment.

practices.name: Breath of the Compassionate (contemplative/movement meditation)
practices.aliases: nafas al-Rahman practice
practices.tradition: Akbari (Ibn 'Arabi) cosmology; modern contemplative adaptations
practices.description: A contemplative or movement practice in which one's own inhalation/exhalation is felt as a participation in the divine creative breath, often paired with sacred geometry (the eight-pointed star) and mantra.
practices.howPracticed: Coordinate breath with awareness of contraction (qabd) on the in-breath and expansion (bast) on the out-breath, contemplating the universe as continuously created, dissolved and renewed within God's Breath; some forms add gentle movement, vocal mantra, and visualization of the eight-pointed star uniting the four elements.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: To realize existence as mercy and constant renewal (tajdid al-khalq), cultivating compassion and presence.
practices.feltExperience: Breath felt as a polar rhythm of giving-and-receiving; a sense of being breathed by a larger reality.
practices.safetyNotes: Modern adaptations vary; the classical doctrine is contemplative/metaphysical rather than a prescribed exercise.

energyCenters.name: Qalb (Heart)
energyCenters.location: Left side of the chest, about two finger-widths below the left nipple
energyCenters.meaning: The 'heart' as organ of spiritual perception and the seat of remembrance; the first center awakened; in Sufi psychology the 'organ of evolution' that refines the nafs. Associated with negation/affirmation dhikr being 'struck' here.
energyCenters.associations: Naqshbandi-Mujaddidi color: yellow (some traditions red). Kubrawi/Simnani: red, prophet Abraham, heart/circulation. Naqshbandi assigns qalb to prophet Adam. Element/quality: submission, the 'taste' of practice.

energyCenters.name: Ruh (Spirit)
energyCenters.location: Right side of the chest, mirror-position to the heart
energyCenters.meaning: The subtle center of vitality, strength and the capacity to act as God's vicegerent; addresses anger.
energyCenters.associations: Naqshbandi-Mujaddidi color: red (some traditions yellow); prophets Noah/Abraham. Kubrawi/Simnani: ruhiya = yellow, prophet David, joy/warmth/expansion. (Note the cross-lineage color swap, which is contested.)

energyCenters.name: Sirr (Secret)
energyCenters.location: Upper-left chest, above the breast (Naqshbandi); 'innermost heart chamber' (Simnani)
energyCenters.meaning: The 'secret' center of unmediated, superconscious knowledge and intimate divine communication.
energyCenters.associations: Color: white (both systems agree). Naqshbandi prophet: Moses. Symbolism: purity, truth, direct revelation.

energyCenters.name: Khafi (Hidden)
energyCenters.location: Upper-right chest, above the breast (Naqshbandi); forehead in some accounts
energyCenters.meaning: The 'hidden' center of inspiration and intuition (ilham); reception of spiritual influx.
energyCenters.associations: Naqshbandi color: black (some say blue); prophet Jesus. Kubrawi/Simnani khafiya: shiny/luminous black, prophet Jesus, dissolution/ascent beyond form.

energyCenters.name: Akhfa / Ikhfa (Most Hidden)
energyCenters.location: Center of the chest (sternum), between heart and spirit
energyCenters.meaning: The 'most hidden' center of the deepest divine knowledge and ultimate spiritual realization.
energyCenters.associations: Color: green (sometimes described as emerald or violet); prophet Muhammad. Represents direct divine gnosis and the apex of the chest-lataif.

energyCenters.name: Nafs (Self)
energyCenters.location: Middle of the forehead (between the eyebrows)
energyCenters.meaning: The latifa of the self/ego; belongs to the 'World of Creation' ('alam-i khalq) rather than the 'World of Command'; the seat to be purified.
energyCenters.associations: Color: described as colorless/clear; once purified, associated with stillness. Linked to the breath itself (nafs/nafas).

energyCenters.name: Qalab / Sultan al-Adhkar (Body / Whole-being center)
energyCenters.location: The physical frame as a whole; in Naqshbandi practice a culminating point spreading dhikr through every cell
energyCenters.meaning: In Simnani's seven-center scheme, qalabiya is the bodily mold (skin/frame), the ground of embodied presence; in Naqshbandi practice the final stage saturates the whole body with dhikr.
energyCenters.associations: Simnani: matte black (beginning/absence, distinct from the luminous black of khafi), prophet Adam, embodiment.

energyCenters.name: Haqq / Haqqiya (the Real) — Simnani's seventh
energyCenters.location: 'The Reality itself'; the innermost essence, not a fixed bodily point
energyCenters.meaning: In Simnani's seven-fold Kubrawi system, the center where the soul recognizes itself as a unique, unrepeatable theophany of God.
energyCenters.associations: Color: emerald/luminous green (highest stage), prophet Muhammad. Approached only after the 'black light' (nur-e-siyah) of the Essence.

research.energyFlowModel:
Breath is understood as the carrier-current that moves the divine Name through the subtle body and awakens the lataif in sequence. In the negation-and-affirmation dhikr the formula is driven on the breath along a path: rising 'La ilaha' from above the navel, sweeping up and to the right side of the chest, then crossing to the left and being 'struck' into the physical heart with 'illa Allah.' Repeated breathing along this circuit is said to 'polish' and ignite the qalb, after which awareness is progressively raised through the centers of the chest — qalb (left), ruh (right), then sirr, khafi and akhfa toward the sternum and finally the head/whole body — under a shaykh's guidance, one latifa at a time.

There is no single fixed channel-map like the nadis of yoga, but the working model is: (1) breath as the conduit of presence and life-force (Inayat Khan calls the inhaled subtle current 'Prana,' the essence of breath); (2) directed attention (muraqaba) plus the shaykh's transmitted attention (tawajjuh) that 'opens' a center; (3) blockage understood as heedlessness (ghafla) and the unpurified nafs, which a breath taken without divine presence reinforces ('the death of the heart'); (4) opening/flow understood as the heart 'coming alive' and the dhikr becoming self-sustaining, eventually synchronizing with the heartbeat. 

At the cosmic scale, Ibn 'Arabi's Nafas al-Rahman provides the macro-model: existence itself is an out-breath of divine mercy, continuously exhaled, dissolved and renewed at every instant (tajdid al-khalq) — so the practitioner's breath mirrors a universal rhythm of expansion and contraction. The felt states of bast (expansion/opening) and qabd (contraction/withholding) map this onto interior experience, the soul alternately opening and closing as the divine 'gives and withholds.' The hands in Mevlevi Sema render the flow visibly: right palm up to receive the descending divine breath/grace, left palm down to pour it into the world — the human being as a turning conduit between heaven and earth.
research.feltSenseAndTransitions:
Practitioners are told to feel the breath as a living thread of presence, not mechanical air: in Hosh dar dam, each inhalation, exhalation, and the pause between should be felt as 'occupied with the Divine.' In heart-dhikr the felt locus is the qalb (left chest), where the Name is felt to be 'struck' or to glow with warmth, and where one eventually senses the heartbeat itself reciting. As practice deepens, the transition described is from scattered, restless attention to a sober, settled interior centered in the chest; the Naqshbandi path deliberately favors this quiet sobriety over dramatic sensation.

The qabd/bast model names the felt transitions directly: contraction (qabd) is felt as a gripping, narrowing, heaviness or spiritual dryness — the sense that the connection to grace has been 'severed' for a time; expansion (bast) is felt as opening, lightness, relief and being 'freed from blockage.' Sufis teach that contraction is always followed by expansion, so the felt rhythm of closing-and-opening is itself part of the path rather than failure.

In the lataif work, awakening a center is reported as a localized sensation (warmth, pulsation, or a perceived color/light, 'photism') at that point in the chest, then a felt 'opening' that lets attention rise to the next center. In the elemental purification breaths, each element is felt as a distinct somatic mood: earth = grounded steadiness, water = flowing and cleansing, fire = warming and energizing, air = light and freeing, ether = refined, spacious peace. In Sema the felt experience is centeredness within motion — the still axis of the turning body — and a sense of being a conduit between sky and earth.
visualMotifs: Sacred calligraphy of the divine Names, especially 'Allah' and 'Hu' (هو), used as a focus for breath-dhikr; the letter 'ha' of these Names is identified with the sound of every exhalation.

Color photisms of the lataif: graded luminous colors seen in inner vision — matte black (lowest/beginning), blue, red, white, yellow, luminous/'shiny' black, and emerald green (highest) in the Kubrawi/Simnani scheme; yellow, red, white, black, green in the Naqshbandi-Mujaddidi chest map.

Simnani's 'black light' (nur-e-siyah / nur-e-Dhat, the Light of the Essence): blackness arising not from absence but from overwhelming intensity, 'like trying to look at the sun' — a key visionary motif of the approach to the divine Essence.

The eight-pointed star (khatim/rub el hizb), formed of two overlapping squares, used to depict the 'Breath of the Compassionate' uniting the four elements (fire, air, water, earth); its geometric expansion-and-contraction read as a 'visual inhalation and exhalation.'

Concentric and radiant geometry of Islamic art (girih patterns, rosettes) read as the deployment of the divine Names — endless articulation of unity into multiplicity, mirroring the Breath that articulates the cosmos.
Diagrams of the human torso marking the locations of the lataif as colored points on the chest, forehead and over the heart, used in teaching manuals.

Mevlevi imagery: the conical sikke hat (the ego's tombstone), the white skirt (the shroud) flaring into a circle in the Turn, one hand raised and one lowered — the body as a living symbol of breath descending from heaven to earth.
The reed flute (ney): its breath-driven lament is the iconic emblem (Rumi's Masnavi opens with it) of the soul's longing breath, severed from its source and crying to return.
research.attributionNotes: These are living, initiatic religious traditions within Islam, not free-floating 'wellness' techniques. Several cautions matter for respectful presentation: (1) Lineage and authorization: the lataif, breath-retention (habs-i-dam), and negation-affirmation dhikr are transmitted through an initiatic chain (silsila) and ordinarily require initiation (bay'a) with a living, authorized shaykh; tawajjuh (the shaykh's transmitted attention) is considered essential to 'open' the centers in the correct order. Presenting these as do-it-yourself routines misrepresents the tradition and is widely warned against as spiritually unsafe and prone to ego-inflation. (2) Sacredness: the divine Names (Allah, Hu, the 99 Names) are sacred; dhikr is worship, not a relaxation tool. Mevlevi Sema is a religious ceremony, not a 'dance' or tourist spectacle — reducing it to performance is a known sensitivity. (3) Islamic framing: many Muslim authorities tie these practices to Sunnah and the Qur'an; some other Muslim voices regard certain breath/posture forms or specific dhikr counts as bid'a (innovation). The relationship of Sufism to broader Islam is itself contested, and practices should be attributed to specific orders (Naqshbandi, Kubrawi, Chishti, Mevlevi, Qadiri-Shattari) rather than to 'Islam' generically. (4) Not yoga/chakras: the lataif resemble chakras superficially but come from a distinct Qur'anic-prophetic and Neoplatonic-influenced cosmology; conflating them with Hindu/Buddhist chakra systems is a common and resented appropriation error. (5) Contested details: lineages genuinely disagree on the number of lataif (five vs six vs seven vs ten), their color assignments (e.g., qalb yellow vs red; ruh red vs yellow), their prophet-correspondences, body locations, and whether the breath is held or kept flowing — these should be presented as lineage-specific and contested, not as one fixed system. (6) The modern universalist Inayat Khan lineage is itself a distinct, more accessible stream and should be named as such rather than presented as 'classical Sufism.' (7) The ruh/spirit is, in Qur'anic terms, deliberately mysterious ('you have not been given knowledge, save a little,' 17:85); claims about 'what the breath/spirit really is' should be framed as belief and tradition, not established fact.

research.topic: Indigenous, Shamanic, and Pacific Breath & Energy/Life-Force Traditions (Hawaiian hā/mana & Huna; Native American breath-spirit; shamanic trance breathing; African & Afro-diaspora life-force concepts)
research.summary:
This entry covers a cluster of distinct traditions that share a deep theme — breath as the vehicle or sign of an invisible life-force — but that are NOT a single system and must not be flattened into one "indigenous energy" model. Four families are treated separately and with care for attribution. (1) Pacific / Hawaiian: hā ("breath/breath of life") and mana ("spiritual power/efficacy"), expressed in the honi greeting (sharing breath nose-to-nose) and in chiefly/genealogical power. Mana is a genuine, deeply studied Oceanic concept (Codrington 1891; debated by Keesing, Mauss, Lévi-Strauss). Crucially, "Huna" is a 20th-century New Age invention by Max Freedom Long (from 1936), built on New Thought and Theosophy, and is rejected by Native Hawaiian scholars as "not, and never was Hawaiian" — it must be flagged as a modern reconstruction/appropriation, not traditional kahuna religion. (2) Native American (here, Lakota as a documented example): niyá, "breath/life," one of a triune soul (niyá, nağí, šičú), animated by the sky-power Tákú Škaŋškáŋ, within the all-pervading sacred wakȟáŋ / Wakȟáŋ Tȟáŋka. Breath/steam in the inípi (sweat lodge) is purificatory. These are living, closed ceremonial traditions; specific rites should not be reproduced as "techniques."

(3) Shamanic trance and modern breathwork: classical shamanism (per Eliade, Harner) reaches "ecstasy" / non-ordinary reality primarily through repetitive drumming (~4–7 beats/sec), not a fixed breath ratio; modern Holotropic Breathwork (Stanislav & Christina Grof, Esalen, ~1974) uses sustained accelerated breathing (hyperventilation) plus evocative music to induce altered states — an explicitly modern, LSD-era synthesis, not a traditional indigenous practice. (4) African & Afro-diaspora: Yoruba àṣẹ/ashe (the power that makes things happen, carried in the breath/spoken word, seated in the orí/head) and èmí (the divine breath/soul breathed in by Olódùmarè); the Bantu philosophical category NTU and "vital force" (popularized via Tempels, Kagame, Jahn — note these are scholarly/colonial-era framings, contested); Nommo (Dogon "word/life-force"). Afro-diaspora religions (Lukumí/Santería, Candomblé) carry àṣẹ forward as living, initiatory traditions.

The throughline for the app: present each tradition in its own terms; mark mana, niyá, àṣẹ, èmí as traditional concepts; mark Huna and Holotropic Breathwork as modern reconstructions/syntheses; and treat closed ceremonial practices (sweat lodge, orisha initiation) as sacred and not as decontextualized "breathing exercises."
research.historyAndOrigin:
PACIFIC / HAWAIIAN: Mana is a pan-Oceanic concept attested across Polynesia and Melanesia. The reconstructed Proto-Oceanic *mana likely first denoted powerful natural forces (thunder, storm winds) and shifted, as speakers expanded eastward, toward unseen supernatural power/efficacy. The first detailed English description is missionary R. H. Codrington's "The Melanesians" (1891), based on the New Hebrides/Solomons. Hā (breath) and mana are central to traditional Hawaiian and broader Polynesian life; the honi/hongi greeting (sharing breath) is pre-contact. HUNA, by contrast, is NOT traditional: it was created by Max Freedom Long (1890–1971), a haole schoolteacher in Hawaiʻi from 1917, who left in 1931 and from 1936 onward built a system he claimed was "encoded" in the Hawaiian language; he founded Huna Research (1945) and published "The Secret Science Behind Miracles" (1948) and later works. Scholars trace Huna to New Thought, Christian Science, and Theosophy.

NATIVE AMERICAN (LAKOTA example): Oral ceremonial tradition of the Lakota/Dakota (Očhéthi Šakówiŋ), documented partly through James R. Walker's early-20th-century collaborations with Lakota holy men (e.g., George Sword) and later Lakota authors. Concepts of niyá, nağí, šičú, wakȟáŋ, and Wakȟáŋ Tȟáŋka are core; the inípi (sweat lodge) is one of the Seven Sacred Rites associated with the Wȟ́aŋblí / White Buffalo Calf Woman tradition.

SHAMANISM & BREATHWORK: "Shaman" derives from the Tungusic/Evenki šamán (Siberia). Mircea Eliade's "Shamanism: Archaic Techniques of Ecstasy" (1951) framed it comparatively (and is itself critiqued for over-generalizing). Michael Harner's "The Way of the Shaman" (1980) launched "core shamanism" / neoshamanism, abstracting cross-cultural techniques for Western practitioners. Holotropic Breathwork was developed by Stanislav Grof (b. 1931, Czechoslovakia) and Christina Grof at the Esalen Institute (~1974) as a legal successor to LSD-assisted therapy.

AFRICAN & AFRO-DIASPORA: Yoruba religion (Ifá/Òrìṣà) originates in present-day Nigeria/Benin among the Yoruba; àṣẹ and èmí are core cosmological terms. Carried by the transatlantic slave trade into Lukumí/Santería (Cuba), Candomblé (Brazil), and related traditions. The pan-Bantu philosophical framing — NTU and "vital force" — was popularized by Placide Tempels' "Bantu Philosophy" (1945, a Belgian missionary), Alexis Kagame (Rwandan priest-philosopher, four categories: Muntu/Kintu/Hantu/Kuntu), and Janheinz Jahn's "Muntu" (1958/1961), who also imported "Nommo" from Dogon (Marcel Griaule's ethnography). These framings are influential but contested as colonial-era systematizations.

coreConcepts.meaning: Breath; the breath of life. In Hawaiian thought breath is emblematic of life itself. Appears in aloha (folk-parsed alo 'presence/face' + hā 'breath' = 'presence of breath/sharing breath') and in honi (breath exchange).

coreConcepts.aliases: mana (pan-Polynesian/Melanesian: Hawaiian, Māori, Tahitian, Samoan, Tongan, Fijian)
coreConcepts.meaning: Supernatural power, efficacy, authority, prestige that 'attaches to persons and things.' Communicable, gainable and losable through action, genealogy, ritual. Codrington: 'a force altogether distinct from physical power.' Contested whether originally a noun (substance) or a stative/verb ('to be effective/true') — Keesing argued the latter for Melanesia.

Tapu/kapu = sacred, restricted, charged with mana and danger; noa = common, free, unrestricted. The complementary regulatory pair governing how mana is contained and released. Not interchangeable with mana but tightly bound to it.

MODERN RECONSTRUCTION (not traditional). A New Age system by Max Freedom Long redefining Hawaiian words for a New Thought self-help framework. Native Hawaiian kahuna/scholar Charles Kenn: 'it is not, and never was Hawaiian.' Must be labeled as a 20th-century invention, not ancestral kahuna teaching.
Breath; the breath of life / life-breath, given at birth by the sky power Tákú Škaŋškáŋ; animates the body and departs at death (likened to smoke or shadow). One of the three souls.
The spirit/ghost that carries individual personality and persists after death; part of the triune soul.
An indwelling guardian/spirit-potency bestowed at birth; can travel during sleep and returns to its nonhuman source at death; further šičúpi can be acquired via visions/dreams.
The mysterious, sacred, nonhuman power/energy pervading the universe; 'holy/sacred/power.' Breath and the three souls are wakȟáŋ aspects of a person, hence immortal.
The totality/sum of all that is wakȟáŋ; often rendered 'Great Spirit' but better 'Great Mystery' — a unifying sacred reality, not a single personal deity in the Christian sense.
The sky/motion power that grants niyá (breath/life) and the first šičú at birth; principle of movement and life.

Yoruba: the power-to-make-things-happen; the divine force from Olódùmarè enabling existence and change. Resides in deities, ancestors, people, nature, AND in voiced words (prayers, praise, curses) — hence breath/utterance is potent. Used in diaspora/AAVE as an affirmation akin to 'amen.'

Yoruba: breath / the breath-soul. The vital spiritual essence breathed into the clay body by Olódùmarè; seat of life and consciousness; departs at death. Distinct from àṣẹ (power) and orí (destiny-head).
Yoruba: the 'inner head' — personal destiny, consciousness, and the principal seat/locus of àṣẹ in a person; chosen before birth. In art the head is enlarged to mark its spiritual primacy.

In the Tempels–Kagame–Jahn framing, the universal force/being in which all beings participate; everything is conceived as force, not static substance. CONTESTED as a colonial-era scholarly systematization rather than a single indigenous doctrine.

Dogon concept of the generative 'word'/life-force; popularized by Jahn as African 'life force expressed through the power of the word.' Originally Dogon (via Griaule); should not be presented as pan-African.

coreConcepts.aliases: Greek holos 'whole' + trepein 'to move toward' = 'moving toward wholeness'
coreConcepts.meaning: Coined by Stanislav Grof for states/processes that orient the psyche toward wholeness; names the modern Holotropic Breathwork method (not a traditional term).

energyCenters.name: orí (inner head)
energyCenters.location: The head / crown; the 'inner head' (orí-inú) within the person
energyCenters.meaning: Primary seat and locus of àṣẹ and of personal destiny in Yoruba thought; the most spiritually significant part of the body, honored and 'fed' in ritual (e.g., ìború/feeding the head).
energyCenters.associations: Element/symbol: the head, enlarged in Yoruba sculpture to signal spiritual primacy; linked to one's chosen destiny and to the òrìṣà overseeing the head; associated with cool/calm (ìwà, character).

energyCenters.name: piko (Hawaiian 'centers')
energyCenters.location: Three bodily piko: crown of the head (piko poʻo), navel (piko waena), genitals/loins (piko maʻi)
energyCenters.meaning: In Hawaiian thought the piko are connective 'centers'/cords linking a person vertically to ancestors (aumākua, via the crown), to one's own generation/self (navel), and to descendants (loins) — a genealogical rather than chakra-like energetic map. NOTE: do not conflate with Indian chakras.
energyCenters.associations: Crown piko = ancestral/ancestor connection; navel piko = self/present; genital piko = descendants/continuity. Bound up with mana and genealogy (moʻokūʻauhau).

energyCenters.name: (No fixed chakra system)
energyCenters.location: N/A — these traditions generally lack a chakra/dantian-style ladder of centers
energyCenters.meaning: Lakota, mainstream Hawaiian, classical shamanic, and Yoruba traditions do NOT teach a universal chain of energy centers like the Indic/Chinese systems. The app should resist importing a chakra template. Where 'centers' exist (orí, piko), they are local and differently conceived.
energyCenters.associations: Caution flag: presenting these traditions with a chakra overlay is a common appropriative distortion to avoid.

research.energyFlowModel: These traditions do NOT generally share a meridian/nāḍī-style internal circulation model; their 'flow' models are relational and cosmological rather than anatomical. Key models: (a) Hawaiian/Polynesian — mana flows along genealogy (moʻokūʻauhau / whakapapa) from gods and ancestors (aumākua) into chiefs, people, places (e.g., Haleakalā), and objects; it is communicable and transferable, can be accumulated or depleted by one's actions, and is regulated by kapu/tapu (containment) versus noa (release). Breath (hā) is the medium of person-to-person transmission in honi: two people press noses/foreheads and inhale together, literally exchanging breath/life and acknowledging shared mana; the piko 'cords' connect a person up to ancestors and down to descendants. (b) Lakota — niyá (breath) is granted at birth by Tákú Škaŋškáŋ (the sky power of motion) and animates the body; at death niyá departs (smoke/shadow), nağí persists as the personal spirit, and šičú returns to its nonhuman/stellar source. Sacred power (wakȟáŋ) pervades all things; in the inípi (sweat lodge), water poured on heated stones produces steam (the 'breath' of the stone-people), and breathing this sacred steam purifies. Movement is between ordinary life and the wakȟáŋ realm, mediated by ritual, song, and the pipe (čhaŋnúŋpa), whose smoke carries prayer upward. (c) Shamanic/breathwork — classical shamanism models the spirit (not breath per se) leaving on a 'journey' to upper/lower worlds, the shaman 'flying' in ecstasy; the driver is sonic (drum ~4–7 beats/sec) more than respiratory. Modern Holotropic Breathwork is the exception that centers breath: sustained fast/deep breathing (hyperventilation) lowers CO2 and is theorized to open access to perinatal/transpersonal layers, 'moving toward wholeness'; sensation is said to build, peak (catharsis/'activation'), and settle/integrate. (d) Yoruba/Afro-diaspora — àṣẹ flows down from Olódùmarè through the òrìṣà and ancestors into people, words, and things; it concentrates in the orí (head) and is released through the breath/spoken word (prayer, praise, incantation/ofò), drumming, and offering. There is no internal channel map; the 'circulation' is between the spirit world (òrun) and the visible world (ayé), mediated by sacrifice (ẹbọ), divination (Ifá), and possession/'mounting' of devotees by òrìṣà.
research.feltSenseAndTransitions: HAWAIIAN honi: practitioners describe a quiet, intimate, reverent sensation — leaning in, gentle pressure of noses/foreheads, a shared slow inhalation; the felt sense is of connection, mutual recognition, and the literal mingling of breath/life, not of energy 'rising.' LAKOTA inípi: intense moist heat, the felt 'living breath' of the steam, sweat, darkness, and the rhythm of song and prayer; participants speak of cleansing, humility, surrender, and renewal ('rebirth' on emerging) — a felt transition from impurity/heaviness to clarity. (These ceremonial feelings are reported within the tradition and should be framed respectfully, not as a recipe.) SHAMANIC journeying: with steady drumming, practitioners report dissociation/disembodiment, a sense of flight or descent, vivid imagery and meetings with spirits/power animals, sometimes ego-dissolution — phenomenologically likened in studies to mild psychedelic states. HOLOTROPIC BREATHWORK is the most explicitly 'breath-felt': the documented arc is (1) onset — tingling, tetany (carpopedal spasm/clawed hands), dizziness, lightheadedness from hyperventilation/hypocapnia; (2) intensification — strong emotions, muscular tension, trembling, vivid imagery, sometimes reliving memories ('biographical/perinatal material'); (3) catharsis/peak — release, weeping, sound, sometimes 'oceanic' or transpersonal states; (4) settling/integration — calm, openness, fatigue. YORUBA/AFRO-DIASPORA: in devotional contexts the felt arc moves from rhythmic build (drumming, song, dance) toward altered awareness and, for initiates, possession/'mounting' by an òrìṣà, experienced as the deity's presence rather than self; àṣẹ in speech is felt as the weight/efficacy of words. Across all: a common shape is build → peak/opening → settle/integrate, but the causes (breath vs drum vs steam vs possession) and meanings differ and should not be merged.

practices.name: Honi (breath-sharing greeting)
practices.aliases: hongi (Māori); honi ihu ('nose kiss')
practices.tradition: Native Hawaiian / Polynesian (also Māori Aotearoa)
practices.description: Traditional greeting in which two people gently press noses (and often foreheads) together and inhale at the same moment, exchanging hā (the breath of life) and acknowledging shared mana.
practices.howPracticed: Stand close, incline the head, press nose-to-nose / forehead-to-forehead with eyes closed, and breathe in together (some Māori iwi do it twice, for inhale and exhale). No mantra; the act itself is the exchange.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: To establish connection and unity, to recognize the other's life and dignity, and (Māori) for a manuhiri/visitor to 'blend with the people of the land' (become tangata whenua for the moment).
practices.feltExperience: Intimate, calm, reverent; a felt mingling of breath and presence, not arousal or energy 'rising.'
practices.safetyNotes: A sacred social protocol — engage only when invited and following local custom; do not perform as a casual party trick. Folk etymology links 'haole' (foreigner) to hā-ʻole ('without breath') i.e. one who doesn't share breath, but scholars note 'haole' predates 1778 European contact, so this is a post-contact folk explanation, not established fact.

practices.name: Inípi (sweat lodge ceremony)
practices.aliases: ini kaġapi; 'stone-people lodge'; one of the Seven Sacred Rites
practices.tradition: Lakota / Dakota (Očhéthi Šakówiŋ) — and analogues in other Plains nations
practices.description: Purification rite in a low dome of bent saplings covered to be dark; heated stones (the 'stone-people') are brought in and water is poured on them to make steam, breathed as sacred 'living breath.'
practices.howPracticed: Led by a qualified ceremonial leader; participants enter humbly, often in rounds, with prayer, song, and the čhaŋnúŋpa (sacred pipe); water on hot stones produces purifying steam. Specific words/songs are tradition-internal and not for public reproduction.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Spiritual and physical purification, prayer, humility, and renewal — often preparatory to other rites (e.g., vision quest, Sun Dance).
practices.feltExperience: Intense moist heat, darkness, sweat, surrender; described as cleansing and rebirth on emerging.
practices.safetyNotes: SACRED and CLOSED: should be conducted only by recognized Lakota/Native ceremonial leaders. Commercial/'New Age' sweat lodges have caused serious injury and deaths (e.g., the 2009 Sedona sweat-lodge deaths under a non-Native operator) and are widely condemned by Native nations as dangerous and appropriative. Do NOT present as a DIY breathing exercise. Real physiological risk of hyperthermia.

practices.name: Shamanic drum journey
practices.aliases: core shamanism journeying (Harner); 'ecstatic trance' (Eliade)
practices.tradition: Neoshamanism / 'core shamanism' (Michael Harner, 1980), abstracted from many indigenous Siberian/Amazonian/circumpolar sources
practices.description: Entering non-ordinary reality to meet spirits, power animals, and teachers, primarily via monotonous drumming rather than a breath technique.
practices.howPracticed: Lie down, relax, set an intention; a drum (or recording) is played in a steady rhythm ~4–7 beats per second (~5 bps typical) for a set time; the practitioner 'travels' to lower/upper worlds and returns when the drum signals (callback rhythm).
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Healing, guidance, retrieval (e.g., 'soul retrieval'), insight.
practices.feltExperience: Light-to-deep trance, vivid imagery, sense of flight/descent, dissociation, sometimes ego-dissolution.
practices.safetyNotes: 'Core shamanism' is a modern Western synthesis — distinguish it from specific living indigenous shamanisms (which are culturally bound and not free for the taking). Avoid claiming a generic 'shaman' identity or selling specific tribal rites. Not advised in acute psychosis/severe dissociation without support.

practices.name: Holotropic Breathwork
practices.aliases: HB; successor to LSD psychotherapy (Grof)
practices.tradition: Modern transpersonal psychology — Stanislav & Christina Grof, Esalen Institute, ~1974 (NOT a traditional indigenous practice)
practices.description: A modern method using sustained accelerated breathing plus evocative music (and often bodywork) to induce non-ordinary states for self-exploration and healing.
practices.howPracticed: In a safe space with a trained facilitator and a 'sitter,' the 'breather' lies down with eyes closed and breathes faster and deeper than normal for an extended period (often 1.5–3 hours), guided by intense music; afterward, integration via drawing mandalas and group sharing. No fixed count/ratio — the instruction is simply faster and deeper.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Access to biographical, 'perinatal,' and 'transpersonal' material; emotional catharsis and 'moving toward wholeness.'
practices.feltExperience: Tingling, tetany/carpopedal spasm (clawed hands), dizziness from hyperventilation, then strong emotion, imagery, possible reliving of memories, catharsis, then calm integration.
practices.safetyNotes: Real contraindications due to hyperventilation/hypocapnia and cardiovascular/intracranial stress: NOT advised in pregnancy, cardiovascular disease/hypertension, history of aneurysm/stroke, glaucoma, retinal detachment, epilepsy/seizure history, recent surgery/fractures, severe psychiatric conditions (psychosis, bipolar). Should be done only with trained facilitators. Present as a modern method, not ancient tradition.

practices.name: Ofò / incantation and prayer (power of the spoken breath)
practices.aliases: ofò àṣẹ (Yoruba incantation); àdúrà (prayer); oríkì (praise-poetry)
practices.tradition: Yoruba (Ifá/Òrìṣà) and Afro-diaspora (Lukumí/Santería, Candomblé)
practices.description: Ritual use of voiced breath — incantation, prayer, praise, blessing — believed to carry àṣẹ and thus to effect change in the world.
practices.howPracticed: Spoken or sung with intention, often with offerings (ẹbọ), divination (Ifá/dilogún), drumming and dance; words may be sealed with 'Àṣẹ' (≈ 'so be it/amen'). Breath as utterance is the active agent.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: To activate blessing, healing, protection, or transformation; to align with òrìṣà and ancestors.
practices.feltExperience: Sense of potency/weight in the words; in fuller devotional rites, rhythmic build toward altered awareness and, for initiates, possession ('being mounted') by an òrìṣà.
practices.safetyNotes: Initiatory, lineage-based traditions: deeper practices (initiation, possession, specific incantations, òrìṣà 'feeding') are reserved for initiates under a babaláwo/iyalorixá/babalorixá. Do not stage possession or sell initiations. Casual diaspora use of 'ashe' as an affirmation is widespread and generally accepted, but the ritual core is not public-domain.

visualMotifs: Hawaiian/Polynesian: breath/mana is rarely drawn as 'energy lines'; visual culture centers on genealogy and the natural world — kapa (barkcloth) patterns, the honi (two faces nose-to-nose), ocean/wind/wave imagery, the kalo (taro) and the piko (navel/'center') as a connective motif, feathered cloaks/standards (ʻahuʻula, kāhili) signaling chiefly mana. Māori: koru (unfurling fern spiral) for new life/breath, wharenui carving, the hongi.

Lakota/Plains: the medicine wheel/sacred hoop and four directions/four colors (commonly black=west, red=north or south, yellow=east, white—colors and assignments vary by nation and keeper, so do not present as fixed); the čhaŋnúŋpa (sacred pipe) with rising smoke as visible prayer/breath; eagle and eagle-feather imagery (breath/spirit ascending); the inípi dome; rising steam. Caution: these are sacred symbols, not decorative.

Shamanic/neoshamanic: world-tree / axis-mundi connecting upper-middle-lower worlds, the drum and its painted skin, spirit/power-animal imagery, journey maps; Siberian/Tungusic costume and drum iconography. Modern breathwork uses abstract 'wholeness'/spiral and, in Holotropic practice, post-session mandala drawings.

Yoruba/Afro-diaspora: àṣẹ visualized through the orí (head) — sculptures with deliberately enlarged heads — beaded crowns (adé) with veils, opón Ifá (divination tray) and ìróké tapper, cowrie shells, the colors and emblems of each òrìṣà (e.g., white for Obatalá, blue/white for Yemọja, red/white for Ṣàngó's thunder-axe oṣẹ, yellow/gold for Ọṣun); sacred drumming (batá, bembé) as the rhythmic vehicle of àṣẹ; in Candomblé, axé as the consecrated power of the terreiro.
African 'vital force' (NTU) imagery is largely a 20th-century scholarly abstraction and has no single classical iconography; avoid inventing one.
research.attributionNotes: CRITICAL SENSITIVITY GUIDANCE. (1) Huna is the single biggest trap: it is NOT traditional Hawaiian religion. It is a 1930s–1940s New Age invention by Max Freedom Long rooted in New Thought/Theosophy. Native Hawaiian authority Charles Kenn (a recognized kahuna) said 'it is not, and never was Hawaiian'; Bishop Museum's Pali Jae Lee said 'Huna is not Hawaiian'; scholar Lisa Kahaleole Hall called it bearing 'absolutely no resemblance to any Hawaiian worldview.' If the app mentions Huna at all, it must be explicitly labeled a modern reconstruction/appropriation and clearly separated from traditional hā, mana, kahuna, aumākua, and the Three-Selves redefinition (unihipili/uhane/aumakua) Long imposed (which inverts the real Hawaiian meanings: 'spirit of a dead person,' 'spirit,' 'family/personal gods'). Use authentic Hawaiian framings (hā, mana, honi, piko, moʻokūʻauhau, aumākua) and credit Native Hawaiian sources, ideally with ʻōlelo Hawaiʻi diacritics (ʻokina and kahakō). (2) Native American ceremonies (inípi/sweat lodge, pipe, vision quest, Sun Dance) are sacred, often closed, and tied to specific nations; the Lakota material here is illustrative, not a universal 'Native American' system — there is no pan-Indian belief. Do not provide how-to instructions for ceremonies, do not sell or stage them, and explicitly warn against non-Native commercial sweat lodges (which have killed people). Honor terms like Wakȟáŋ Tȟáŋka as 'Great Mystery' rather than reducing it to a Western 'God,' and use Lakota orthography where possible. (3) 'Shaman/shamanism' is a Western umbrella term; 'core shamanism' (Harner) and Holotropic Breathwork (Grof) are modern syntheses — never present them as 'ancient indigenous breathing.' Distinguish living indigenous practitioners from neoshamanic adaptations, and avoid letting non-indigenous people claim the title 'shaman' over specific tribal lineages (e.g., ayahuasca/Amazonian, Siberian). (4) Yoruba/Afro-diaspora (Ifá, Lukumí/Santería, Candomblé) are initiatory, hierarchical, living religions; àṣẹ as a spoken affirmation is broadly shared, but deeper practice (initiation, possession, incantations, òrìṣà offerings) belongs to initiates under proper elders. Credit Yoruba/diaspora terms and their custodians; do not stage possession or commodify initiation. (5) The Bantu 'vital force/NTU' framework (Tempels, Kagame, Jahn, Nommo) is partly a colonial-era/scholarly construction; present it as an interpretive philosophy with named authors, not as a verbatim indigenous doctrine, and note it is contested. (6) GENERAL: do not flatten these into one 'universal life-energy' or overlay a chakra/meridian template — that itself is a recognized form of appropriative distortion. Where sources disagree (e.g., mana as noun vs. stative-verb; haole etymology; the universality of 'mana'/'vital force'), say it is contested. Frame everything as living belief and tradition, with respect, accurate attribution, and an explicit non-medical, non-scientific stance.
