research.topic: Visual depiction of breath and energy across contemplative traditions and modern breathing apps
research.summary:
This is a survey of how breath and subtle "energy" (prāṇa, qi, lung) are made VISIBLE — as colors, shapes, diagrams, channels, sacred geometry, and light — across Indic, Tibetan, and Chinese traditions, and how that visual grammar reappears in modern meditation/breathwork apps. Two broad families of imagery recur. First, MAPS OF THE INTERIOR: the body drawn as a system of channels and centers — the Indic chakra-and-nāḍī system (lotus "wheels" strung on a central channel), the Tibetan tsa-lung three-channel diagram, and the Daoist Neijing Tu "inner landscape" with its three dantian and Microcosmic Orbit loop. Second, COSMOGRAMS / FIELDS OF RADIANCE: mandalas, yantras (Sri Yantra), sacred-geometry figures (Flower of Life), and the halo/aureole/mandorla — diagrams or auras in which energy is shown not as a path but as a centered, radiating whole.

Across these systems a shared visual vocabulary emerges for ENERGY STATE and DIRECTION: rising/ascending energy is drawn vertically up a central axis (kundalini up the suṣumnā, qi up the Du Mai/spine), upward-pointing triangles and flames connote active/expansive/masculine/fire, downward-pointing triangles and crescents connote receptive/settling/feminine/water, the lotus opening (petals) signals an awakened/active center, concentric expansion from a bindu/center signals both source and culmination, and gold/white light signals the most refined or transcendent state. Color is used to differentiate centers and elements (earth/water/fire/air/ether), though the now-ubiquitous rainbow (ROYGBIV) chakra palette is a modern Western systematization, not classical Tantra.

Modern apps translate this inheritance into a minimal, time-based "pacer" grammar. The dominant motif is the EXPANDING/CONTRACTING CIRCLE (Apple Watch Breathe's six-circle "flower," Calm, Headspace): the shape grows on inhale, holds at full scale, shrinks on exhale, and rests at minimum — a direct visual analog of the lungs and of the older "expansion from a center" idea. Supporting devices include warm gradient color transitions (cool/contracted to warm/expanded, or palettes mapped to emotional states), soft particle/fluid fields, body-glow overlays, and geometric pacers like the box-breathing square (tracing four equal sides for inhale-hold-exhale-hold). The design intent is explicit and consistent: the animation acts as a non-verbal pacemaker the nervous system entrains to, using slow easing, rounded forms, and muted/warm color to signal safety and calm.

The app maps practice presents these as living beliefs and aesthetic traditions, not medical claims, and care is needed with attribution: chakras/yantras (Hindu Tantra and Shri Vidya), tsa-lung/thangka channels (Vajrayana Buddhism), and the Microcosmic Orbit/Neijing Tu (Daoist neidan) are sacred, lineage-transmitted systems frequently flattened or commercialized in wellness contexts.
research.historyAndOrigin: CHAKRA/NĀḌĪ SYSTEM (India): Subtle-body channel-and-center maps developed in tantric and haṭha-yoga literature roughly 8th–16th c. CE; the canonical six-plus-one chakra scheme is codified in texts such as the Ṣaṭ-cakra-nirūpaṇa (1577, by Pūrṇānanda), popularized in the West by Arthur Avalon (John Woodroffe), The Serpent Power (1919). TIBETAN TSA-LUNG (Vajrayana Buddhism): Three-channel/wind/drop (tsa-lung-thigle) physiology elaborated in tantric systems such as the Six Yogas of Nāropa (transmitted from Indian mahāsiddhas, 10th–11th c., into Tibet via Marpa/Milarepa, Kagyu and other schools); depicted in thangka and medical paintings (e.g., the 17th-c. Blue Beryl medical tangkas commissioned under Desi Sangye Gyatso). DAOIST NEIDAN (China): Internal-alchemy circulation of qi; the Microcosmic Orbit (Xiao Zhou Tian / "Small Heavenly Circuit") and the Neijing Tu ("Chart of the Inner Landscape," carved on a stele at White Cloud Temple/Baiyun Guan, Beijing, 1886; motifs date to Tang–Song, 7th–13th c.), associated with the Quanzhen school. HALO/NIMBUS: Pre-Christian roots in Zoroastrian khvarenah/hvarena (divine glory) and Greco-Roman solar radiance; adopted in Buddhist, Hindu, and Christian art (gold-ground nimbi standard in Byzantine/medieval Europe). MANDALA & YANTRA: Hindu and Buddhist Tantra; the Sri Yantra is central to the Shri Vidya school of Shakta worship. FLOWER OF LIFE: ancient overlapping-circle motif found across cultures; its "sacred geometry" interpretation is largely modern/New Age (popularized late 20th c., e.g., Drunvalo Melchizedek).

coreConcepts.meaning: Vital life-breath/energy in Indic thought; the substance moved by breathing and pranayama. Subdivided into five vāyus (prāṇa, apāna, samāna, udāna, vyāna).
coreConcepts.aliases: prana; cf. vāyu (wind); related: chi/qi, lung

coreConcepts.meaning: Subtle channel through which prāṇa flows; texts cite 72,000, with three principal: iḍā, piṅgalā, suṣumnā.
Left (lunar, cooling, feminine), right (solar, heating, masculine), and central channels. Kundalini rises through the central suṣumnā when iḍā and piṅgalā are balanced.
'Wheel'; a subtle energy center visualized as a lotus (padma) with a set number of petals, a central geometric shape (yantra) for its element, a seed-syllable, and presiding deities.
'Seed syllable' resonant with a chakra/deity (LAM, VAM, RAM, YAM, HAM, OM), chanted to activate the center.
Coiled latent energy (kundala = coiled) at the base of the spine, conceived as a serpent; rises through suṣumnā to the crown in awakening.
Vital energy/breath in Chinese thought, circulated through meridians; refined in neidan from jing to qi to shen.
The 'Three Treasures' (san bao): essence, vital energy, and spirit — the substances refined and circulated in Daoist internal alchemy.
'Cinnabar/elixir field'; three energetic reservoirs — lower (lower abdomen), middle (chest/heart), upper (head/between brows) — central to qi cultivation.
coreConcepts.aliases: tan tien; dan tian; cinnabar field

coreConcepts.meaning: Governing Vessel (up the spine/back midline) and Conception Vessel (down the front midline); the two channels joined into the Microcosmic Orbit loop.
coreConcepts.aliases: Governor/Governing Vessel; Conception/Conducting Vessel; Du/Ren meridians

coreConcepts.meaning: Neidan practice circulating qi up the Du Mai and down the Ren Mai in a continuous loop synced to breath.
coreConcepts.aliases: Xiao Zhou Tian; Small Heavenly Circuit/Cycle; Lesser Heavenly Circuit

coreConcepts.meaning: Tibetan channels-winds-drops: the three nāḍī (central uma/avadhūti, right roma/rasanā, left kyangma/lalanā), the inner winds (lung/prāṇa), and the subtle drops (thigle/bindu).
Point/drop; in yantras the central source-point of creation and the goal of meditation; in Tibetan systems the subtle 'drops' moved by the winds.
Geometric sacred diagrams: a yantra is a focused geometric instrument (e.g., Sri Yantra) and a mandala a cosmogram/'circle' used as meditation and ritual map of cosmos and mind.
Light imagery of the holy: nimbus (head halo), aureole/glory (full-body radiance), mandorla (almond/vesica-shaped body halo).

energyCenters.name: Muladhara (Root)
energyCenters.associations: Color (classical: deep red/yellow square; modern: red); element Earth (Pṛthivī); geometry: 4-petaled lotus with downward triangle inside a yellow square; seed-syllable LAM; deity Brahmā/Ganesha; animal elephant
energyCenters.meaning: Foundation/support; seat of dormant kundalini

energyCenters.name: Svadhisthana (Sacral)
energyCenters.associations: Modern color orange; element Water (Ap); 6-petaled lotus with white crescent moon; seed VAM; deity Viṣṇu; animal makara/crocodile
energyCenters.meaning: Flow, fluidity, generativity

energyCenters.name: Manipura (Solar Plexus)
energyCenters.associations: Modern color yellow; element Fire (Agni/Tejas); 10-petaled lotus with downward-pointing (fire) triangle; seed RAM; deity Rudra (fiery Shiva); animal ram
energyCenters.meaning: 'City of jewels'; heat, power, transformation

energyCenters.name: Anahata (Heart)
energyCenters.associations: Modern color green; element Air (Vāyu); 12-petaled lotus with hexagram (two interlocking triangles, air); seed YAM; animal antelope/deer
energyCenters.meaning: 'Unstruck' sound; balance, compassion, where ida/pingala themes meet

energyCenters.name: Vishuddha (Throat)
energyCenters.associations: Modern color blue/turquoise; element Ether/Space (Ākāśa); 16-petaled lotus with circle; seed HAM; animal white elephant
energyCenters.meaning: Purification; expression, sound

energyCenters.name: Ajna (Third Eye)
energyCenters.associations: Modern color indigo/violet; 2-petaled lotus (the two petals = ida and pingala terminating); seed OM/AUM; presiding over the two main side channels
energyCenters.meaning: 'Command' center; insight, where the three channels converge

energyCenters.name: Sahasrara (Crown)
energyCenters.associations: Color white/violet/multicolored radiance; thousand-petaled lotus (20 layers of ~50 petals); often NO seed-syllable (silence beyond sound), sometimes Om; union of Shiva-Shakti
energyCenters.meaning: Liberation; pure consciousness, transcendence of the body-map

energyCenters.name: Lower Dantian (Daoist)
energyCenters.associations: The 'Golden Stove/furnace'; reservoir/forge of qi and jing; root of the Microcosmic Orbit
energyCenters.meaning: Primary cultivation/storage center for vital energy

energyCenters.name: Middle Dantian (Daoist)
energyCenters.associations: Associated with qi and the heart-spirit; a refining station on the orbit
energyCenters.meaning: Transformation of qi

energyCenters.name: Upper Dantian (Daoist)
energyCenters.location: Head, between/behind the brows (Niwan), to crown (Baihui)
energyCenters.associations: Seat of shen (spirit); 'Mud Pellet' (Niwan) palace
energyCenters.meaning: Refinement of spirit; highest alchemical stage

energyCenters.name: Tibetan channel-wheels (chakras)
energyCenters.location: Along the central channel (commonly crown, throat, heart, navel, secret place)
energyCenters.associations: Channels branch out 'like the ribs of an umbrella'; right (roma/rasanā) and left (kyangma/lalanā) channels coil around the central uma/avadhūti forming 'knots' at the wheels
energyCenters.meaning: Sites where winds and drops gather; untying the knots is the aim of completion-stage practice

research.energyFlowModel: INDIC (chakra/nāḍī): Breath/prāṇa is moved by pranayama through the nāḍīs. Ordinary energy alternates through iḍā (left, lunar, cooling) and piṅgalā (right, solar, heating); the central suṣumnā is normally closed, and balancing the two opens it so kundalini RISES from Muladhara up the spine to Sahasrara — ascent equals awakening. Petals show how many nāḍīs converge; an opened lotus = active center, knots (granthi) = blocks. TIBETAN (tsa-lung): inner winds carry drops through the central channel and two coiling side channels; practice dissolves side winds into the central channel, untying knots at the wheels. DAOIST (Microcosmic Orbit): qi circulates as a LOOP — up the Du Mai (spine) on inhale, over the crown, down the Ren Mai (front) on exhale, settling in the lower dantian; substances refine jing→qi→shen. Both rising (yang) and settling (yin) phases are essential.
research.feltSenseAndTransitions: Practitioners are told to FEEL energy as warmth, tingling, pressure, fullness, or light moving along a line or gathering at a point. In the Microcosmic Orbit, attention follows a warm current rising up the spine and pouring down the front, with the lower dantian filling and warming like a stove and the practice settling energy DOWN to feel grounded. In kundalini/chakra work the felt sense is heat/current rising along the spine with each center 'opening' (expansion/release at navel, heart, throat, brow), though forcing it can feel like heat or agitation. In Tibetan tsa-lung, vase-breath retention is felt as warmth/fullness at the navel and a loosening of knots. The universal TRANSITION grammar tracks the breath cycle: INHALE = expansion/filling/rising/brightening; HOLD (kumbhaka) = fullness, stillness, gathering at a center; EXHALE = release/descent/softening/dimming; REST (pause after exhale) = settledness, emptiness, calm. Modern apps deliberately mirror this arc — circle expands to cue the inhale's fullness, holds at maximum for retention, contracts for the exhale's release, rests at minimum for the pause — so the body entrains to the visual without counting.

practices.name: Microcosmic Orbit
practices.aliases: Xiao Zhou Tian; Small/Lesser Heavenly Circuit/Cycle
practices.tradition: Daoist internal alchemy (neidan) / qigong
practices.description: Guided circulation of qi in a closed loop up the Governing Vessel (Du Mai) and down the Conception Vessel (Ren Mai), centered on the lower dantian.
practices.howPracticed: Seated, relaxed, tongue touching the upper palate to 'bridge' the two vessels. Attention rests at the lower dantian, then guides energy down to the perineum/tailbone, up the spine through three gates to the crown, then down the front midline back to the dantian. A common coordination: inhale raises qi up the back, exhale sends it down the front; breath slow and abdominal.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Harmonize yin/yang, refine jing→qi→shen, build vitality and calm; foundation for higher neidan and longevity ('attain immortality').
practices.feltExperience: Warmth pooling and circulating; a sense of current following attention; grounding/settling as energy returns to and gathers in the lower dantian.
practices.safetyNotes: Traditionally taught to build energetic capacity first (qigong/tai chi) before forcing circulation, to avoid 'nervous system strain' or qi deviation (zou huo ru mo). Best learned from a teacher.

practices.name: Kundalini awakening via pranayama
practices.aliases: laya yoga; kundalini yoga; cakra-bhedana (piercing the chakras)
practices.tradition: Hindu Tantra / Haṭha Yoga
practices.description: Balancing iḍā and piṅgalā so the central suṣumnā opens, allowing kundalini to rise from Muladhara through each chakra to Sahasrara.
practices.howPracticed: Seated āsana with bandhas (locks), mudras, breath retention (kumbhaka), and pranayama such as alternate-nostril breathing (nāḍī śodhana) to balance the side channels; concentration and visualization on chakras, their colors, geometry, and bīja mantras.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Awakening, expanded consciousness, union (Shiva-Shakti) at the crown, liberation (mokṣa).
practices.feltExperience: Heat/current rising along the spine; sensations of opening/expansion at successive centers; light at the brow/crown.
practices.safetyNotes: Forcing kundalini is widely cautioned; texts and teachers warn of heat, agitation, instability ('kundalini syndrome' in modern accounts). Should be undertaken with a qualified guru.

practices.name: Tsa-lung / vase breath
practices.aliases: rtsa rlung; tsa lung trul khor; kumbhaka/'vase breathing' (bumchen)
practices.tradition: Tibetan Vajrayana (e.g., Six Yogas of Nāropa, Bön/Buddhist)
practices.description: Yogic exercises combining posture, breath retention, and visualization to direct inner winds into the central channel and loosen the channel-knots.
practices.howPracticed: Specific postures/movements with 'vase' breath retention at the navel, visualizing the three channels, the wheels, and inner-fire (tummo/caṇḍālī) igniting at the navel and rising.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Generate inner heat (tummo), dissolve side-channel winds into the central channel to realize clear-light/bliss-emptiness.
practices.feltExperience: Warmth/heat at the navel spreading through the body; fullness during retention; subtle bliss as winds enter the central channel.
practices.safetyNotes: Restricted, initiatory practices requiring empowerment and a qualified lama; improper practice is said to cause lung (wind) imbalance/disorders.

practices.name: Box breathing (square breathing)
practices.aliases: 4-4-4-4; tactical/combat breathing
practices.tradition: Modern secular breathwork (used in apps)
practices.description: Equal-count cycle of inhale-hold-exhale-hold, visualized as tracing the four sides of a square.
practices.howPracticed: Inhale 4s (trace up/right), hold 4s (across), exhale 4s (down), hold 4s (back) — repeat. Apps animate a dot moving around a square or a square expanding/holding.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: In-the-moment stress regulation, focus, parasympathetic calm.
practices.feltExperience: Steadiness; the equal pause cues a sense of containment and control.
practices.safetyNotes: Generally low-risk; long holds may be uncomfortable for some — shorten counts as needed.

practices.name: 4-7-8 breathing
practices.aliases: 'relaxing breath'
practices.tradition: Modern breathwork (popularized by Andrew Weil; rooted in pranayama)
practices.description: Inhale 4, hold 7, long exhale 8 — an extended-exhale pattern.
practices.howPracticed: Inhale through nose 4s, hold 7s, exhale through pursed lips 8s; often 4 cycles.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Downregulation, sleep onset, relaxation (long exhale favors parasympathetic tone).
practices.feltExperience: Settling, drowsiness, slowing — the long exhale feels like 'letting go.'
practices.safetyNotes: Ease into the 7-count hold; not a medical treatment.

visualMotifs: Lotus/padma wheels (chakras) with element-geometry cores and seed-syllables
Central vertical axis with coiled serpent rising (kundalini up suṣumnā)
Twin intertwining side channels (iḍā/piṅgalā) crossing at the chakras
Upward (active/fire/Shiva) vs downward (settling/water/Shakti) triangles, interlocked for union
Bindu with concentric outward radiation — source and goal in one point
Mandala cosmogram: ring of fire → lotus petals → square palace with four gates → central deity/bindu
Sri Yantra: 9 interlocking triangles forming 43 small triangles around a bindu, ringed by lotuses and an earth-square (3D = Mahā Meru)
Flower of Life overlapping-circle lattice and Metatron's Cube
Halo/nimbus/aureole/mandorla — gold disk, rayed sun-halo, cruciform/square nimbus, almond mandorla; in Asian art often FLAMES
Daoist Neijing Tu 'inner landscape': body as mountains/rivers/fields, three dantian as cinnabar fields, qi as flowing water
Meridian/acupuncture charts tracing qi as lines over the body
Microcosmic Orbit loop drawn as an arrowed circuit up the back and down the front
Gold/white radiance and the thousand-petal crown lotus for the transcendent state
MODERN APPS: expanding/contracting circle pacer (Apple Breathe six-circle flower, Calm, Headspace)
MODERN APPS: warm animated gradient transitions, soft particle/fluid fields, body-glow overlays, and box-breathing square pacers
research.attributionNotes: These are sacred, lineage-transmitted systems, not generic 'wellness' graphics, and the app should attribute them accurately and avoid presenting belief as fact. KEY CAUTIONS: (1) The rainbow ROYGBIV chakra-color scheme (red root → violet crown) is a MODERN, largely 20th-century Western systematization (associated with Theosophy/New Age and Leadbeater's Chakras, 1927); classical Tantra assigns colors differently (e.g., element/petal colors, not a clean spectrum) — present the rainbow palette as a modern convention, not 'ancient.' (2) Chakras, nāḍīs, kundalini, and yantras belong to Hindu Tantra/Shri Vidya; the Sri Yantra in particular is an object of initiatory worship — depicting it as mere décor or misdrawing its geometry is considered disrespectful; its construction is contested (multiple valid renderings exist) and exact triangle counts/forms vary by lineage. (3) Tsa-lung, tummo, and thangka channel illustrations are Vajrayana practices traditionally requiring empowerment and a qualified lama; they should not be presented as casual self-help. (4) The Microcosmic Orbit and Neijing Tu are Daoist neidan (Quanzhen-associated); the Neijing Tu is symbolic 'inner landscape' allegory, not anatomy. (5) The halo/nimbus spans Zoroastrian, Greco-Roman, Buddhist, Hindu, and Christian art — avoid implying a single origin; note its Asian flame-form differs from the Western gold disk. (6) Transliterations vary (Sanskrit IAST vs popular spellings; Wade-Giles vs Pinyin for Chinese — e.g., 'tan tien'/'dantian', 'chi'/'qi'); pick one system and note common aliases. (7) Several 'sacred geometry' claims (Flower of Life as an ancient unified system, geometry as literal life-template) are modern New Age interpretations and should be flagged as contested/non-historical rather than stated as established fact. (8) Frame the modern-app visual grammar as inspired-by / aesthetically descended-from these traditions, not as equivalent to them.

research.topic: Phenomenology of how breath and energy are felt in the body: interoception, somatic vocabulary, and the felt transitions of subtle sensation across contemplative traditions
research.summary:
This capture is about the felt, first-person side of breathing and "energy" practice: not the metaphysics of subtle bodies as such, but how breath and inner energy are actually SENSED, named, and tracked as they move and change. The scientific frame is interoception, the perception of internal bodily signals. A widely cited taxonomy (Mehling; Farb et al.) splits it into dimensions such as awareness, attention, sensitivity, accuracy, sensibility, and regulation, and notes that contemplative training tends to increase "sensory granularity," the ability to notice fine, subtle changes in sensation rather than only gross states. Breath is the canonical interoceptive object: practitioners are told to feel its rhythm, temperature, texture, and location (nostrils, chest, abdomen) without conceptualizing it. Alongside this sits Eugene Gendlin's "felt sense," the vague, pre-verbal bodily knowing that precedes words, and that one approaches by trying out descriptors until one "resonates" with the body.

Empirically, Lauri Nummenmaa and colleagues (PNAS 2014, "Bodily maps of emotions") showed that emotions map to topographically distinct, cross-culturally consistent patterns of felt activation/deactivation across the body silhouette (e.g., anger and fear in the head/chest/arms; happiness/love nearly whole-body; sadness as deactivated limbs; disgust in throat and gut). This is the strongest evidence base for the app's premise that inner states have a shareable "body map," and a natural template for visual body-overlays. A follow-up (Nummenmaa et al. 2018, "Maps of subjective feelings") extended this to over 100 feelings, with breathing-related and bodily-salience dimensions.

Across living traditions, the felt vocabulary is strikingly convergent even where the cosmologies differ: warmth/heat, tingling, vibration, pulsation, pressure, expansion, openness, flow, heaviness, lightness, and settling. Traditions narrate felt TRANSITIONS as journeys: heat kindling and pooling in the lower belly (Taoist dantian, Tibetan tummo navel fire), energy rising up the spinal channel (kundalini up sushumna; lung drawn into the central channel), the chest "opening" (heart/anahata), and a circuit "completing" (the Taoist Microcosmic Orbit looping up the back and down the front). Buddhist sources are unusually precise about the felt arc of concentration: the five grades of piti (rapture) escalate from hair-raising tingles to wave-like "showering" and whole-body "pervading" flooding, then settle into the quieter warmth of sukha as the breath becomes subtle and seems to nearly stop. These are first-person reports within belief systems, not validated physiology, and the app should present them as such.

For design: felt-sense translates cleanly into modalities. Visuals can use Nummenmaa-style body heatmaps, expanding/contracting forms, color-temperature gradients (cool inhale to warm exhale), and rising/circulating motion paths. Haptics can map breath amplitude to vibration intensity and give the body a "marker" to lock onto (as in breathing wearables), with rising buzz for "energy up the spine," a settling pulse for grounding, and gentle waves for piti-like flooding. Audio can use rising/falling pitch and pink-noise "flow," a low drone settling for the exhale, and bija-style seed tones per body region. Reflection prompts should invite naming raw qualities (Where do you feel the breath? Is it warm or cool, tight or open, heavy or light, still or moving?) in the non-judgmental, granularity-building style shared by interoception research, focusing, and body-scan practice.
research.historyAndOrigin: The felt-sense lineage is braided from several streams. Scientific interoception traces to early-20th-century physiology (Sherrington's "interoceptors") and was reframed for contemplative science by A.D. Craig's neuroanatomy of bodily feeling and by Wolf Mehling's "Multidimensional Assessment of Interoceptive Awareness" (MAIA, 2012) and Norman Farb et al.'s "Interoception, contemplative practice, and health" (Frontiers in Psychology, 2015). Eugene Gendlin developed the "felt sense" and Focusing at the University of Chicago in the late 1950s-60s out of his work with Carl Rogers; his philosophy PhD (1958) and book "Focusing" (1978) anchor the modern somatic-awareness vocabulary, later joined by Peter Levine's Somatic Experiencing and, more recently, Stephen Porges's Polyvagal Theory (1990s onward) framing felt safety/mobilization/shutdown. Empirical body-mapping is recent: Lauri Nummenmaa, Enrico Glerean, Riitta Hari, and Jari Hietanen, "Bodily maps of emotions," PNAS 111(2):646-651, published online 31 Dec 2013, with ~700 participants from Finland, Sweden, and Taiwan using the topographic emBODY tool; extended in "Maps of subjective feelings," PNAS 2018. The contemplative felt-maps are far older: Hindu/tantric chakra-nadi schemes crystallize in medieval texts (e.g., the Sat-chakra-nirupana, 1577, translated by John Woodroffe as "The Serpent Power," 1919), with earlier nadi references in the Upanishads and four-to-five-chakra schemes in 8th-century CE Buddhist tantras (e.g., Hevajra Tantra). Tibetan tsa-lung-tigle and tummo belong to Vajrayana completion-stage yoga (e.g., the Six Yogas of Naropa, c. 11th century). The Taoist Microcosmic Orbit (Xiao Zhou Tian, "Small Heavenly Circulation") comes from neidan (internal alchemy), with roots in medieval Daoist practice. Buddhist mindfulness of breathing (anapanasati) is canonical, set out in the Anapanasati Sutta (Majjhima Nikaya 118), with the piti/jhana phenomenology elaborated in the Visuddhimagga (Buddhaghosa, 5th century CE).

coreConcepts.aliases: interoceptive awareness; visceral sense; the "sixth sense"
coreConcepts.meaning: The perception of internal bodily signals (heartbeat, breath, gut, temperature, muscular tension). Multidimensional: awareness, attention tendency, sensitivity, accuracy, sensibility, and regulation (Mehling MAIA; Farb/Daubenmier). Distinct from exteroception (outer senses) and proprioception (limb position).

The ability to notice fine, specific details of internal experience (subtle changes, not just gross states). Said to increase with contemplative training; the developmental core of a rich somatic vocabulary.

coreConcepts.aliases: Gendlin's felt sense; bodily felt meaning
coreConcepts.meaning: A vague, holistic, pre-verbal bodily knowing of a situation that is 'more than words.' One forms it by trying out descriptors until a word/image 'resonates' and the body shifts (a 'felt shift'). Basis of Focusing.

A palpable change of state in the body during practice (loosening of tightness, spreading warmth, a sense of dropping or sinking). In nervous-system terms, a move from mobilization toward ventral-vagal 'tranquil openness' (Porges).

The recurring felt-quality lexicon used across interoception, somatics, and traditions: warmth/heat, coolness, tingling, prickling, buzzing, vibration, pulsation, throbbing, pressure, tightness/constriction, clenching, expansion, openness, spaciousness, flow/streaming, heaviness, lightness, fluttering, sharp vs. dull, still vs. moving.
The 'vital wind/energy' said to move through the body in each tradition's channel system; functionally the felt sense of animating breath-energy that practitioners track and direct.

Channels through which subtle energy is felt to move. Key Hindu nadis: ida (left, cooling, lunar, white), pingala (right, heating, solar, red), sushumna (central, spinal). Tibetan: central channel (uma/avadhuti) flanked by left and right; ~72,000 channels total.

coreConcepts.aliases: tan t'ien; 'cinnabar/elixir field'; lower dantian = 'Golden Stove'
coreConcepts.meaning: The lower-belly energy center (a few finger-widths below the navel) where Taoist practice cultivates felt heat and pressure before circulating it. Functional anchor for 'energy pooling in the belly.'
Luminous vital 'drops' located at key points (e.g., the 'indestructible drop' at the heart); melted by inner heat in tummo to produce the felt 'four joys/blisses' as they descend.

coreConcepts.aliases: kuṇḍalinī; 'serpent power'; (Buddhist) caṇḍālī/tummo as cognate inner fire
coreConcepts.meaning: Dormant energy 'coiled' at the base of the spine that, when roused, is felt to rise up sushumna, often as heat, tingling, vibration, or waves through the chakras.
Buddhist felt factors of concentration. Piti = energetic, often physical rapture (graded from hair-raising to whole-body flooding); sukha = the calmer, warmer ease it settles into.

coreConcepts.aliases: Xiao Zhou Tian; Small Heavenly Cycle/Circulation; Lesser Heavenly Circuit
coreConcepts.meaning: Taoist felt circuit: qi rises up the Governing Vessel (Du Mai, along the spine) on the inhale and descends the Conception Vessel (Ren Mai, down the front midline) on the exhale, looping back to the dantian; 'the orbit completing.'

energyCenters.name: Muladhara (Root)
energyCenters.associations: Color red; element Earth; bija LAM; 4 petals; yellow square geometry; deity Ganesha. Felt as grounding, heaviness, stability, base of rising heat.
energyCenters.meaning: Survival/grounding center; traditional seat of dormant kundalini. Felt sense: weight, rootedness, the 'floor' of the body.

energyCenters.name: Svadhisthana (Sacral)
energyCenters.location: Lower abdomen / root of the sexual organs
energyCenters.associations: Color orange; element Water; bija VAM; 6 petals; crescent-moon geometry; deity Brahma. Felt as fluidity, warmth, flow.
energyCenters.meaning: Fluidity, sensation, emotional/creative current; felt as flowing or pooling warmth low in the belly.

energyCenters.name: Manipura (Solar plexus / Navel)
energyCenters.associations: Color yellow; element Fire; bija RAM; 10 petals; downward red triangle; deity (Braddha) Rudra. Felt as heat, fire, churning, radiant warmth. Overlaps the Taoist lower dantian and the tummo navel-fire site.
energyCenters.meaning: Heat and power center; key site for kindling and pooling inner fire across yogic and Taoist maps.

energyCenters.name: Anahata (Heart)
energyCenters.associations: Color green; element Air; bija YAM; 12 petals; hexagram (two interlaced triangles); deity Ishana Rudra Shiva. Felt as expansion, openness, warmth, softening, sometimes ache.
energyCenters.meaning: The center most associated with the felt 'opening of the chest'; breath expansion and emotional warmth converge here.

energyCenters.name: Vishuddha (Throat)
energyCenters.associations: Color blue; element Space/Ether (akasha); bija HAM; 16 petals; deity Panchavaktra Shiva. Felt as constriction/release, voice, swallowing, lump-in-throat.
energyCenters.meaning: Expression center; felt as tightening or opening of the throat (note the gut/throat axis of disgust in body-map research).

energyCenters.name: Ajna (Third eye / Brow)
energyCenters.associations: Color indigo; 'element' light/darkness; bija OM; 2 (or 96) petals; downward triangle marking convergence of ida, pingala, sushumna. Felt as pressure, tingling, inner light.
energyCenters.meaning: Confluence point where the three principal nadis meet; commonly a felt locus of pressure or luminosity.

energyCenters.name: Sahasrara (Crown)
energyCenters.associations: Color white/violet; thousand-petaled lotus; bija OM or silence. Felt as openness, lightness, tingling, dissolution.
energyCenters.meaning: Destination of rising energy; felt as spaciousness, lightness, or boundary-dissolving at the top of the head.

energyCenters.name: Lower Dantian (Taoist)
energyCenters.location: ~3 finger-widths below the navel, deep in the lower belly
energyCenters.associations: Called the 'Golden Stove'; cultivated via deep abdominal breathing; no chakra-style color scheme. Felt as warmth, fullness, pressure, a glowing center.
energyCenters.meaning: Primary Taoist energy reservoir where qi is gathered and heated before circulating in the Microcosmic Orbit.

energyCenters.name: Du Mai & Ren Mai (Taoist orbit channels)
energyCenters.location: Du Mai (Governing Vessel) up the back/spine; Ren Mai (Conception Vessel) down the front midline
energyCenters.associations: Inhale = qi up the back; exhale = qi down the front. Felt as a moving warmth/tingle tracing a loop.
energyCenters.meaning: The two vessels forming the Microcosmic Orbit; the felt 'circuit' that practitioners report completing.

research.energyFlowModel: Each tradition narrates breath-energy as MOVING along channels with characteristic directions and felt transitions. Hindu tantra: prana enters and, with practice, is drawn out of the side channels (ida = left, cooling/lunar; pingala = right, heating/solar) into the central sushumna; roused kundalini is felt to rise from the root up the spine, piercing each chakra in turn, often as heat, vibration, or waves, culminating at the crown. Tibetan Vajrayana: the ~72,000 channels carry lung (winds); in tummo the practitioner stokes a felt navel fire, draws the winds into the central channel via breath-retention and visualization, which 'melts' the tigle (drops) so they descend, producing the felt 'four joys.' Taoism: qi is first gathered and heated in the lower dantian, then circulated in the Microcosmic Orbit, rising up the Governing Vessel along the spine on the inhale and descending the Conception Vessel down the front on the exhale, looping continuously. Buddhist anapanasati works without an energy-channel cosmology: attention rides the raw breath sensation (nostrils, chest, or abdomen), the breath progressively becomes finer and 'settles,' and concentration produces piti that escalates and then quiets into sukha. Common cross-tradition grammar of felt transition: KINDLING/POOLING (heat gathering low in the belly), RISING (energy/heat ascending the spine or midline), OPENING (expansion/softening, especially the chest), CIRCULATING/COMPLETING (a loop or orbit closing), FLOODING (wave-like or whole-body pervasion, as in showering/pervading piti or kundalini waves), and SETTLING/GROUNDING (sinking, heaviness, stillness, breath nearly stopping). Blocks or imbalance are felt as tightness, constriction, stuck pressure, or — when forced — as overheating, agitation, and 'deviation.' These are belief-internal phenomenologies, not validated mechanisms.
research.feltSenseAndTransitions: How breath/energy is FELT, and how the sensation transitions, is remarkably consistent across sources even when frameworks differ. Breath itself is felt in qualities: rhythm, temperature (cooler in, warmer out), texture (smooth/rough), depth, and location (a touch at the nostrils, rise-and-fall at the chest, swelling at the belly). In skilled practice the breath 'refines': it becomes quieter, subtler, and seems to nearly stop (reported in both anapanasati and tummo retention). The energetic felt-vocabulary clusters into: thermal (warmth, heat, blazing, burning, coolness, chills); vibratory (tingling, prickling, buzzing, electric, vibration, pulsation, throbbing); pressure/volume (pressure, fullness, tightness, constriction, clenching vs. expansion, openness, spaciousness); motion (flow, streaming, rising, sinking, waves, rushing, fluttering); and mass (heaviness, density vs. lightness, floating, dissolving). The empirically documented transitions: the Ananda Marga kundalini study found somatosensory reports dominated by 'vibrating' (33%), 'energy' (29%), 'tingling' (24%), 'pressure' (21%), localized to the spine (31%), brow/ajna (21%), and heart/anahata (21%), with 19% reporting the hallmark 'sensations rising in the spine,' sometimes traveling both up and down, breath-synchronized, and expanding outward from a center (one report: 'tinglings up and down the sushumna nadi, very pleasant'). Buddhist piti escalates through five graded felt stages — minor (hair-raising), momentary (electric flash, insect-tickle, jolt of the spine), showering (waves breaking again and again on the body), uplifting (lightness/levity), and pervading (whole-body inundation) — before settling into the warmer, calmer ease of sukha. Taoist practice begins with felt heat and pressure pooling in the lower dantian, then a moving warmth tracing the orbit. Tummo is felt as fierce navel heat, then a descending bliss as the drops melt. The felt-sense framework (Gendlin) and body-scan practice add the meta-skill: notice without changing, name the quality precisely (sharp vs. dull, tight vs. open), and watch for the spontaneous shift. Nervous-system framing (Porges) reads the settling transition as a move from sympathetic mobilization (tight, fast, buzzy) toward ventral-vagal 'tranquil openness' (warm, slow, spacious, grounded).
visualMotifs: Nummenmaa-style body silhouette heatmaps: warm colors for felt activation, cool/blue for deactivation, mapped onto a front-facing body outline (the clearest evidence-based template for an interoceptive body overlay).
Expanding and contracting forms synced to breath: a glowing orb or ring at chest/belly that swells on inhale, softens on exhale (expansion/openness vs. settling).

Color-temperature gradients: cool blue/cyan for the cooling inhale and ida (lunar/white), warm amber/red for the heating exhale and pingala (solar/red); a vertical cool-to-warm gradient for rising heat.
Rising vertical light or particles up the spinal midline for kundalini/lung-into-central-channel; pooling glow low in the belly for dantian/navel fire.
Circulating loop animation for the Microcosmic Orbit: a luminous point rising up the back and descending the front, closing a circuit.

Chakra geometry: stacked lotuses with petal counts (4,6,10,12,16,2,1000), bija seed-syllables at center, element-symbols (yellow square=earth, crescent=water, downward triangle=fire, hexagram=air), traditional colors.
Tibetan thangka-style channel diagrams: central channel flanked by left/right, with drops (tigle) at key points; subtle-body line art.
Daoist neidan 'inner landscape' (Neijing Tu) imagery depicting the body as a circulating energetic terrain.
Wave / ripple motifs for piti 'showering' and 'pervading' rapture and for kundalini waves; fine grain/static for tingling and vibration.
Settling motifs: a descending, sinking, or grounding gradient; roots; a slowing, dimming pulse for the exhale and for the move toward calm.

practices.name: Body scan (interoceptive awareness)
practices.aliases: sensate focusing; mindfulness body sweep
practices.description: Systematic, sequential attention through regions of the body, noticing raw sensation without trying to change it.
practices.tradition: Modern mindfulness / interoception research (MAIA; Farb et al.); roots in Buddhist kayanupassana.
practices.howPracticed: Move attention slowly through body parts; for each, note temperature, texture, weight, movement, tension/ease; name the quality precisely (sharp vs. dull, tight vs. open).
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Build sensory granularity and interoceptive accuracy; decouple sensation from conceptual thought; support emotion regulation.
practices.feltExperience: Tingling, warmth, heaviness, pulsation, tightness, openness; often a felt 'settling' or release as a region softens.
practices.safetyNotes: For trauma histories, intense interoceptive focus can be activating; titrate, allow grounding/orienting, and keep it optional. Non-medical framing.

practices.name: Anapanasati (mindfulness of breathing)
practices.aliases: ānāpānasati; mindfulness of in-and-out breathing
practices.description: Sustained attention on the natural breath as a field of sensation, set out in the Anapanasati Sutta (MN 118).
practices.tradition: Buddhism (Theravada and beyond).
practices.howPracticed: Rest attention where breath is felt most clearly — nostrils, chest, or abdomen — experiencing raw sensation free of imagery; some traditions widen to the whole body as a unified field. No forced ratio.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Calm and unify the mind; cultivate the seven factors of awakening; access piti and sukha, then deeper jhana.
practices.feltExperience: Breath felt as cool-in/warm-out, smooth, then progressively subtler until it nearly stops; piti as graded rapture, then sukha as warm calm ease.
practices.safetyNotes: Generally gentle; intensive retreat practice can surface strong affect or energetic phenomena. Present as contemplative tradition, not therapy.

practices.name: Microcosmic Orbit
practices.aliases: Xiao Zhou Tian; Small Heavenly Cycle/Circulation
practices.description: Taoist neidan practice circulating qi up the spine and down the front midline in a continuous loop.
practices.tradition: Daoist internal alchemy (neidan); qigong.
practices.howPracticed: Begin with deep abdominal breathing to gather felt heat/pressure in the lower dantian; on inhale guide qi up the Governing Vessel (spine), on exhale down the Conception Vessel (front), often touching tongue to palate to 'connect' the circuit.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Health, longevity, and spiritual refinement by smoothing and circulating qi.
practices.feltExperience: Warmth and fullness pooling in the belly, then a moving warmth/tingle tracing the loop; a felt sense of the circuit 'completing.'
practices.safetyNotes: Sources warn against forcing: excessive intention or wrong posture can cause 'qi deviation' (zou huo ru mo) felt as overheating, agitation, insomnia, anxiety. Emphasize relaxed, unforced practice and grounding.

practices.name: Tummo (inner heat) / Tsa-lung
practices.aliases: gtum mo; caṇḍālī; 'fierce inner fire'; tsa lung trul khor
practices.description: Vajrayana completion-stage yoga combining breath retention, posture/movement, and visualization to draw the winds (lung) into the central channel and generate felt heat and bliss.
practices.tradition: Tibetan Buddhism (Vajrayana/Dzogchen; e.g., Six Yogas of Naropa).
practices.howPracticed: Visualize the channels and a tiny fire at the navel; use 'vase breathing' (drawing/holding breath at the belly) and physical exercises (trul khor) to ignite and stoke the heat, drawing lung into the central channel so the heart 'drop' melts and descends.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Realize the union of bliss and emptiness; traditionally also produces literal warmth.
practices.feltExperience: Fierce navel heat/blazing, energy entering and rising in the central channel, then a descending bliss ('four joys') as the drops melt.
practices.safetyNotes: Advanced, initiatory practice traditionally requiring a qualified teacher/empowerment; not to be self-taught from an app. Strong energetic and thermal effects.

practices.name: Kundalini-rousing tantric meditation
practices.aliases: kuṇḍalinī yoga; laya yoga; (in Ananda Marga) Tantric meditation
practices.description: Practices intended to rouse dormant energy at the base of the spine to rise through the chakras up sushumna.
practices.tradition: Hindu tantra; modern lineages (e.g., Ananda Marga studied empirically).
practices.howPracticed: Combinations of breath, bandhas (locks), mantra/bija, and concentration on chakra points; energy is felt to rise along the spinal channel.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Awakening, expansion of consciousness, union of energy with awareness.
practices.feltExperience: Reported as vibrating, energy, tingling, pressure, and heat rising in the spine (sometimes up and down), concentrated at spine, brow, and heart, with waves and breath-synchronized pulsation; affect often blissful.
practices.safetyNotes: Can produce intense, sometimes destabilizing experiences ('kundalini syndrome' / spiritual emergency): heat, involuntary movements, mood swings. The studied population was prepared/trained, which mitigated adverse effects. Frame respectfully; advise grounding and qualified guidance.

practices.name: Focusing (felt sense)
practices.aliases: Gendlin's Focusing
practices.description: A structured way of attending to the vague, pre-verbal bodily 'felt sense' of a situation and letting it become clearer.
practices.tradition: Humanistic/experiential psychotherapy (Gendlin, 1960s-70s).
practices.howPracticed: Clear a space, let a felt sense form in the body (often chest/belly), find a 'handle' word/image, check it against the body, and wait for a felt shift/release.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Access embodied meaning and produce lasting change via the felt shift.
practices.feltExperience: A bodily 'something' that is fuzzy at first, then a palpable loosening, easing, or opening when the right word resonates.
practices.safetyNotes: Gentle and self-paced; pair with grounding for distressing material. Non-medical.

practices.name: Coherent / slow breathing with biofeedback
practices.aliases: resonance breathing; HRV biofeedback; paced breathing
practices.description: Slow, even breathing (commonly ~5-6 breaths/min) often guided by visual, audio, or haptic cues.
practices.tradition: Modern psychophysiology / wellness tech (overlaps with pranayama heritage).
practices.howPracticed: Breathe to a steady pace, sometimes with longer exhale; devices map breath amplitude to vibration intensity or LED/pitch cues so the body has a 'marker' to lock onto without watching a screen.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Shift toward calm (ventral-vagal) states; steady attention; settle the nervous system.
practices.feltExperience: A felt slowing and warming, chest/belly expansion on inhale, a sinking 'settling' on exhale, reduced buzz/tightness.
practices.safetyNotes: Very breath-hold-light and safe for most; avoid strong breath retention for those with cardiovascular or anxiety conditions. Keep non-medical framing.

research.attributionNotes: Treat the felt-sense maps as living, often sacred traditions, not interchangeable wellness content. (1) The popular rainbow-chakra-with-personality-traits scheme is NOT ancient Indian doctrine: the seven-color rainbow mapping was introduced by Charles W. Leadbeater (Theosophy) in 1927, and the now-ubiquitous rainbow+qualities+gemstone+astrology bundle only coalesced in the West around 1977. Traditional texts (e.g., Sat-chakra-nirupana) give specific, sometimes different colors, petal counts, bija syllables, and deities; present the New Age scheme as a modern Western overlay, distinct from tantric sources, and credit the Hindu/tantric origin. (2) Tummo, tsa-lung, and kundalini practices are initiatory: traditionally they require empowerment and a qualified teacher and can be genuinely destabilizing. An app should describe and honor them, not instruct users to self-induce them; flag spiritual-emergency risk and grounding. (3) Do not flatten distinct cosmologies into one 'energy' soup: prana (Hindu), qi (Daoist), and lung (Tibetan Buddhist) sit in different metaphysical systems; name each correctly with its own transliteration and lineage. (4) Buddhist anapanasati and piti/sukha are doctrinal, with canonical sources (MN 118; Visuddhimagga) — attribute precisely and avoid implying the jhana phenomenology is guaranteed or medical. (5) Interoception science, the Nummenmaa body-maps, Gendlin's Focusing, and Polyvagal Theory are modern Western frameworks — keep them clearly separated from the contemplative traditions rather than using science to 'validate' or appropriate sacred practice. (6) Honor that some practices and mantras are considered sacred/transmitted; where a tradition regards material as restricted or oral-only, acknowledge that rather than presenting full instructions. Throughout, use respectful, accurate transliteration (e.g., prāṇa, nāḍī, suṣumnā, gtum mo, qì) and present everything as belief/tradition, explicitly not as medical or scientific truth.

research.topic: Comparative scholarship on the subtle/energy body across cultures: chakras vs dantian vs lataif vs sefirot vs koshas
research.summary:
The "subtle body" is a comparative-religion umbrella term for a quasi-material level of human existence intermediate between gross physical body and pure mind/spirit, posited across many traditions. The single most important scholarly anchor is Geoffrey Samuel and Jay Johnston's edited volume "Religion and the Subtle Body in Asia and the West: Between Mind and Body" (Routledge, 2013) — the first sustained academic study of the concept across cultures, premodern and contemporary, elite and vernacular. Its central methodological finding is that these traditions offer "fundamentally diverse portraits of the subtle body"; the apparent family resemblance (centers + channels + subtle breath) conceals deep differences in cosmology, number, location, function, and goal. Samuel specifically warns that 19th-century Theosophical translation (Blavatsky onward) rendered Sanskrit terms like sukshma-sharira as "subtle body" and thereby made the vocabulary "problematic" for scholars, because the Western synthesis altered original meanings.

The five systems compared here are NOT the same kind of object. The Hindu/Tantric chakra system (standardized at seven in late-medieval texts such as the Sat-Cakra-Nirupana, 1577) and the Tibetan Buddhist tsa-lung system are genuinely cognate: both are located energy WHEELS strung along a central channel (sushumna / central channel) through which subtle breath (prana / lung) and a serpent/inner-fire energy (kundalini / candali-tummo) rise. The Daoist three dantian ("cinnabar fields") are also located centers but only THREE, vertically stacked (lower belly, heart, head), serving as alchemical crucibles that refine the Three Treasures (jing→qi→shen→emptiness) rather than petalled lotuses to be "opened." The Sufi lataif-e-sitta (five or six "subtleties") ARE located on the torso/head and colored, and so superficially resemble chakras, but they are Islamic psycho-spiritual organs tied to Quranic cosmology, specific prophets, and purification of the nafs — and several sources explicitly flag the chakra comparison as a possible cross-cultural confusion. The Kabbalistic sefirot (ten divine emanations on the Tree of Life) are primarily attributes of GOD mapped secondarily onto the body of the cosmic Adam Kadmon — they are theological/cosmological, not a breath-circulation map. The Vedantic pancha-kosha (five sheaths) are CONCENTRIC LAYERS (like onion skins), not points in space, and explicitly cut across the gross/subtle/causal bodies — a categorically different model from located centers.

The strongest cross-cultural commonality is the structural intuition that the human being has an intermediate, breath-animated layer organized around centers and/or channels through which "energy" rises, settles, circulates, or is refined — and that disciplined breath, posture, sound, and visualization can move it toward a salvific goal. But scholars repeatedly caution against false equivalence: the seductive modern "master chart" lining up chakra = sefirah = lataifa = dantian = kosha is largely a 20th-century Theosophical/New Age construction (rainbow colors added by C.W. Leadbeater in 1927; the full color-plus-quality list appears only c.1977 per scholar Kurt Leland). The traditions differ in number (3 vs 5 vs 6 vs 7 vs 10), in location, in ontology (psychological faculty vs divine emanation vs alchemical furnace vs sheath of selfhood), and above all in soteriological goal (moksha/liberation vs Buddhahood vs return to the Dao vs union with Allah / al-insan al-kamil vs devekut/cleaving to God). Equating them flattens these differences and often reflects colonial-era appropriation rather than the living traditions' own self-understanding.
research.historyAndOrigin: Subtle-body ideas arise independently in several civilizational matrices and then get cross-wired in the modern West. HINDU: earliest layers in the Upanishads — the pancha-kosha in the Taittiriya Upanishad (c. 6th–1st c. BCE); channel/center ideas develop through Tantra. The standardized seven-chakra system is LATE: six-cakra schemes in Shaiva tantras like the Netra Tantra (c.700–850 CE) and Kubjikamata-tantra (c.11th c.), with the definitive classical description in Purnananda's Sat-Cakra-Nirupana (1577). TIBETAN BUDDHIST: the tsa-lung (channels-winds-drops) vajra-body system enters via Indian Buddhist tantra from c.8th c. CE, with completion-stage practices like tummo (Six Yogas of Naropa, 10th–11th c.). DAOIST: dantian first described in the Laozi zhongjing (Central Scripture of Laozi, c.3rd c. CE); body-gods and internal alchemy (neidan) elaborate through the Tang–Song and the Shangqing tradition. SUFI: the heart (qalb) faculty traces to early figures like al-Hakim al-Tirmidhi (9th c.); the systematic lataif scheme is credited to the Kubrawi master Ala ud-Dawla Simnani (1261–1336) and standardized in the Naqshbandi-Mujaddidi order by Ahmad Sirhindi (1564–1624). KABBALAH: the ten sefirot grow from the proto-text Sefer Yetzirah (the term "sefirot" appears there but the named ten-on-a-Tree is a medieval elaboration), crystallizing in 12th–13th c. Provence/Spain (Bahir, Zohar c.1280s). MODERN CONVERGENCE: Sir John Woodroffe (pen name "Arthur Avalon") translated the Sat-Cakra-Nirupana as "The Serpent Power" (1919), bringing the seven chakras to the West; the Theosophical Society (Blavatsky, 1880s onward; Leadbeater's "The Chakras," 1927) added gland correspondences and rainbow colors and began equating chakras with sefirot and other systems — a syncretic "reinvention" scholars distinguish sharply from the source traditions.

coreConcepts.aliases: sukshma-sharira / linga-sharira (Skt); 'quasi-material' body; vajra body (Tib.); jism latif (Arabic, loosely)
coreConcepts.meaning: A level of human existence intermediate between gross physical body and pure mind/spirit, posited across traditions; comparative-religion umbrella term popularized in scholarship by Geoffrey Samuel & Jay Johnston (2013). NOT a single shared doctrine — each tradition defines it differently.

In Hindu/Tantric yoga, a wheel-like energy center strung along the central channel, depicted as a lotus with a fixed number of petals, a seed-syllable, element, and presiding deities. Standard system = 7.

coreConcepts.aliases: ida, pingala, sushumna (the three principal channels)
coreConcepts.meaning: Subtle channels (said to be 72,000) conveying prana. Ida = left/lunar/cooling, pingala = right/solar/heating, sushumna = central axis up which kundalini rises.

coreConcepts.aliases: the 'serpent power'; candali / tummo (Tib. inner fire)
coreConcepts.meaning: Dormant coiled energy at the base center (Muladhara) which, awakened, rises through sushumna piercing each chakra toward union of Shakti with Shiva at the crown.

coreConcepts.aliases: dan tian, tan t'ien, 'cinnabar field', 'elixir field'; lower=xia dantian, middle=zhong dantian, upper=shang dantian
coreConcepts.meaning: Three vertically-stacked energy reservoirs/crucibles in Daoist alchemy (lower belly, heart, head/brow) where the Three Treasures are stored and refined.
The substances refined in neidan: jing→qi (lower dantian), qi→shen (middle), shen→emptiness/xu (upper). Refinement sequence, not a center-by-center 'opening.'

Sufi subtle psycho-spiritual organs/faculties — commonly Qalb, Ruh, Sirr, Khafi, Akhfa, plus Nafs (and sometimes Haqi/Nafs at navel). Receptors of divine grace, purified to attain al-insan al-kamil (the Complete/Perfect Human).

coreConcepts.aliases: sephiroth; the Ten Emanations; the Tree of Life (Etz Chaim)
coreConcepts.meaning: Ten attributes/emanations through which the hidden God (Ein Sof) creates and sustains reality: Keter, Chokhmah, Binah, Chesed, Gevurah, Tiferet, Netzach, Hod, Yesod, Malkhut. Mapped onto the cosmic body of Adam Kadmon. Primarily a theology of God, only secondarily a body map.

coreConcepts.aliases: pancha-kosha (five sheaths); annamaya, pranamaya, manomaya, vijnanamaya, anandamaya
coreConcepts.meaning: Five concentric SHEATHS enveloping the Atman (Self), from Taittiriya Upanishad: food, breath, mind, intellect, bliss. Layers/coverings — NOT located centers; they map onto the three bodies (gross/subtle/causal).

The primordial anthropos onto whose body the sefirot are mapped (Keter=crown/head, Chokhmah/Binah=brain, Tiferet=chest/heart, Chesed/Gevurah=arms, Netzach/Hod=legs, Yesod=genitals, Malkhut=feet/totality).
Tibetan Buddhist subtle-body triad: 72,000 channels (3 principal), five vital winds, and essence drops, with chakras as 'channel-wheels' along the central channel.

energyCenters.name: Muladhara (Hindu chakra 1)
energyCenters.associations: 4 petals; element Earth; bija LAM; deity Ganesha (Shakti Dakini); yellow square; seat of dormant kundalini
energyCenters.meaning: Root/foundation center

energyCenters.name: Svadhisthana (Hindu chakra 2)
energyCenters.associations: 6 petals; element Water; bija VAM; deity Brahma (Shakti Rakini); white crescent moon
energyCenters.meaning: Sacral / 'one's own abode'

energyCenters.name: Manipura (Hindu chakra 3)
energyCenters.associations: 10 petals; element Fire; bija RAM; deity Braddha Rudra (Shakti Lakini); red downward triangle
energyCenters.meaning: 'Jewel city' — heat, power, digestion

energyCenters.name: Anahata (Hindu chakra 4)
energyCenters.associations: 12 petals; element Air; bija YAM; deity Ishana Rudra Shiva (Shakti Kakini); hexagram (two interlaced triangles)
energyCenters.meaning: 'Unstruck (sound)' — heart center

energyCenters.name: Vishuddha (Hindu chakra 5)
energyCenters.associations: 16 petals (16 Sanskrit vowels); element Space/Akasha; bija HAM; deity Panchavaktra Shiva (Shakti Shakini)
energyCenters.meaning: 'Purest' — purification, speech

energyCenters.name: Ajna (Hindu chakra 6)
energyCenters.associations: 2 petals; element Mind/Light; bija OM (AUM); the guru principle (Shakti Hakini)
energyCenters.meaning: 'Command' — third eye, insight

energyCenters.name: Sahasrara (Hindu chakra 7)
energyCenters.associations: 1000 petals; beyond elements; no bija; union of Shiva-Shakti
energyCenters.meaning: 'Thousand-petaled' — liberation/union

energyCenters.name: Lower Dantian (xia dantian)
energyCenters.location: Lower abdomen, ~1.5–3 inches below/behind the navel
energyCenters.associations: The 'golden stove'; stores jing (essence); refines jing→qi; primary reservoir of Original Qi; warmth/fullness when gathered
energyCenters.meaning: Alchemical furnace — foundation of vitality

energyCenters.name: Middle Dantian (zhong dantian)
energyCenters.associations: Refines qi→shen; seat of emotion and consciousness
energyCenters.meaning: Crucible of spirit

energyCenters.name: Upper Dantian (shang dantian)
energyCenters.associations: Refines shen→emptiness (xu/wu wei); spiritual illumination
energyCenters.meaning: Return to the Dao

energyCenters.name: Qalb (Sufi latifa)
energyCenters.location: Two fingers below the LEFT breast
energyCenters.associations: Color yellow (some: red); prophet Adam; faculty of spiritual heart / divine love and discernment
energyCenters.meaning: The heart-subtlety

energyCenters.name: Ruh (Sufi latifa)
energyCenters.location: Two fingers below the RIGHT breast
energyCenters.associations: Color red; prophets Noah & Abraham; refined spirit; works on anger
energyCenters.meaning: The spirit-subtlety

energyCenters.name: Sirr (Sufi latifa)
energyCenters.associations: Color white (some: green); prophet Moses; 'the secret' where God's mystery manifests; works on greed
energyCenters.meaning: The secret-subtlety

energyCenters.name: Khafi (Sufi latifa)
energyCenters.associations: Color black (some: blue); prophet Jesus; awakened through divine love; works on jealousy
energyCenters.meaning: The hidden-subtlety

energyCenters.name: Akhfa / Ikhfa (Sufi latifa)
energyCenters.location: Center of chest / top-center of head (varies)
energyCenters.associations: Color green (some: violet); prophet Muhammad; point of unity / direct beatific vision; works on pride
energyCenters.meaning: The most-hidden subtlety

energyCenters.name: Nafs (Sufi latifa)
energyCenters.associations: Color yellow; prophet Adam; the lower/commanding self to be purified — inclusion among the lataif is contested and possibly a borrowing
energyCenters.meaning: The ego-self subtlety

energyCenters.name: Keter (sefirah 1)
energyCenters.associations: 'Crown'; divine will; topmost of the middle pillar; beyond intellect
energyCenters.meaning: Highest, most transcendent emanation

energyCenters.name: Chokhmah & Binah (sefirot 2–3)
energyCenters.location: Right brain (Chokhmah) & left brain (Binah) of Adam Kadmon
energyCenters.associations: Wisdom (right pillar, mercy) & Understanding (left pillar, judgment); first intellectual polarity
energyCenters.meaning: Flash of insight (Chokhmah) and its development (Binah)

energyCenters.name: Tiferet (sefirah 6)
energyCenters.location: Chest / heart of Adam Kadmon (middle pillar)
energyCenters.associations: 'Beauty/Harmony'; balances Chesed (love, right arm) and Gevurah (judgment, left arm)
energyCenters.meaning: Harmonizing center of the Tree

energyCenters.name: Yesod (sefirah 9)
energyCenters.associations: 'Foundation'; channel transmitting all higher energies to Malkhut/world
energyCenters.meaning: Conduit to the material realm

energyCenters.name: Malkhut / Shekhinah (sefirah 10)
energyCenters.associations: 'Kingdom'; the indwelling Divine Presence; receptive vessel for all emanations
energyCenters.meaning: Manifest world / divine immanence

energyCenters.name: Pancha-kosha (NOT centers)
energyCenters.location: Concentric layers, not points — span the whole body-mind
energyCenters.associations: Annamaya (food/physical), Pranamaya (breath/vital), Manomaya (mind), Vijnanamaya (intellect), Anandamaya (bliss); map to gross/subtle/causal bodies
energyCenters.meaning: Five SHEATHS over the Atman — included here only to contrast with point-like centers

research.energyFlowModel: The traditions model movement differently, which is exactly where false equivalence breaks down. HINDU/TANTRIC: prana flows through 72,000 nadis; the three key channels are ida (left, lunar, cooling), pingala (right, solar, heating), and sushumna (central). Breath control (pranayama) and bandhas (locks) draw prana into sushumna; dormant kundalini at Muladhara then RISES, piercing each chakra in sequence toward Shiva-Shakti union at Sahasrara — a vertical, upward, ascensional model culminating in liberation. TIBETAN BUDDHIST: vital winds (lung) normally move in the side channels; tummo (inner-fire) practice forces the winds to ENTER, ABIDE, and DISSOLVE in the central channel, melting the drops (thigle) to generate the four blisses united with insight into emptiness — also vertical/central but framed as wind-and-drop dynamics, not a 'serpent.' DAOIST: not a single ascent but CIRCULATION and REFINEMENT. The Microcosmic Orbit (Small Heavenly Circulation) loops qi UP the back via the Governing Vessel (Du Mai, perineum→crown) and DOWN the front via the Conception Vessel (Ren Mai, palate→lower dantian), the tongue touching the palate to bridge the two; energy is gathered and 'cooked' in the lower dantian and progressively refined jing→qi→shen→emptiness. SUFI: less a flow than ACTIVATION/illumination — the shaykh transmits baraka (tawajjuh) and the seeker directs concentrated awareness (muraqaba) and dhikr to each latifa in a set order, each lighting up with its color, refining the nafs and opening the heart to divine presence. KABBALAH: the dominant image is DESCENT — divine light (shefa) emanates downward through the sefirot from Keter to Malkhut along the three pillars and the channels/paths; the practitioner's work (devotion, mitzvot, kavvanah) draws light down and 'raises' it back up, but this is cosmic/theological flow, not bodily breath circulation. KOSHAS: no flow at all — a static layered model in which the seeker discriminates inward through the sheaths to the Self.
research.feltSenseAndTransitions: Each tradition counsels distinctive felt-sense markers but also strong cautions not to force or expect them. HINDU/KUNDALINI: described as heat, tingling, or a rising sensation along the spine; light or pressure at the brow (Ajna); awakened kundalini is felt as intense energy ascending and 'piercing' centers — texts and modern teachers warn this can be overwhelming if forced ('kundalini syndrome'). TIBETAN TUMMO: practitioners report literal inner HEAT at the navel (Manipura-equivalent) spreading up the central channel, then waves of 'bliss' (the four joys) as drops melt — famously demonstrated in cold tolerance, but traditionally a closely-guarded completion-stage practice. DAOIST MICROCOSMIC ORBIT: the canonical felt cue is WARMTH or FULLNESS gathering in the lower dantian, then a sense of qi moving up the spine and down the front; teachers stress GENTLE awareness — 'the mind leads the qi, the breath fuels the qi' — and explicitly say not to force or expect a specific experience. SUFI LATAIF: muraqaba focuses awareness on each chest/head point; experiences are framed as subtle warmth, presence, or the inner 'opening' of the heart and visionary states (kashf), understood as gifts of grace rather than self-produced. KABBALAH: classical practice emphasizes intellectual/devotional contemplation (kavvanah) and visualization of letters/sefirot rather than somatic sensation, though ecstatic Kabbalah (Abulafia) used breath, chant, and head-movements to induce altered states. KOSHAS: the 'transition' is contemplative discernment — peeling attention inward from body-awareness (annamaya) through breath (pranamaya) and mind to the bliss-sheath, experienced as progressively subtler interiority rather than located sensation.

practices.name: Chakra meditation / Kundalini yoga
practices.aliases: laya yoga; shatchakra sadhana; kundalini awakening
practices.tradition: Hindu Tantra / Hatha & Kundalini yoga
practices.description: Concentration on each chakra in sequence using its seed-syllable, color/form, and deity, combined with breath control to rouse kundalini up sushumna.
practices.howPracticed: Seated posture (e.g., siddhasana/padmasana) with erect spine; pranayama (e.g., nadi shodhana alternate-nostril, ujjayi); bandhas (mula, uddiyana, jalandhara locks) to direct prana; visualization of the lotus, its petals, geometric yantra, bija mantra (LAM, VAM, RAM, YAM, HAM, OM), and presiding deity at each center.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Awaken and raise kundalini through the chakras to union of Shakti with Shiva at Sahasrara = moksha (liberation), with intermediate purification and siddhis.
practices.feltExperience: Heat/tingling/rising energy along the spine; light or pressure at the brow; bliss at the crown.
practices.safetyNotes: Traditionally requires an initiated guru; forced practice is said to risk 'kundalini' disturbance (anxiety, heat, psychological destabilization). App should present as belief, not a health prescription.

practices.name: Tummo (inner heat) / tsa-lung
practices.aliases: gtum-mo; candali; one of the Six Yogas of Naropa
practices.tradition: Tibetan Vajrayana Buddhism (and Bon)
practices.description: Completion-stage practice driving vital winds into the central channel to ignite inner heat and generate bliss-emptiness.
practices.howPracticed: Vase breathing (kumbhaka-like retention) drawing winds down and locking them at the navel; visualization of the central + side channels, a short-A syllable / inner flame at the navel, and the white drop at the crown; the melting drop descends through the chakras producing the four blisses.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Unite great bliss with direct realization of emptiness (the basis of the illusory/rainbow body); progress toward Buddhahood.
practices.feltExperience: Literal inner heat at the navel rising through the central channel; successive waves of bliss.
practices.safetyNotes: Highly esoteric; requires empowerment (wang), transmission, and a qualified lama; not to be self-taught from texts.

practices.name: Microcosmic Orbit / neidan
practices.aliases: Small Heavenly Circulation; xiao zhou tian; internal alchemy
practices.tradition: Daoism
practices.description: Circulating qi up the Governing Vessel and down the Conception Vessel while refining the Three Treasures in the three dantian.
practices.howPracticed: Seated, relaxed, tongue tip on the upper palate to bridge the vessels; slow deep abdominal (and later reverse) breathing centered on the lower dantian; mind gently 'leads' qi up the spine (Du Mai) to the crown and down the front (Ren Mai) back to the lower dantian; gather and 'cook' qi before circulating.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Build and refine jing→qi→shen→emptiness; health, longevity, and ultimately return to the Dao / spiritual immortality.
practices.feltExperience: Warmth/fullness in the lower belly; sense of qi moving along the back and front; calm.
practices.safetyNotes: Teachers stress not forcing; recommend qualified supervision, especially for those with emotional/mental-health vulnerability, as mis-practice is said to disrupt qi flow.

practices.name: Muraqaba on the lataif / dhikr
practices.aliases: lataif meditation; sultan-ul-azkar; pas anfas (breath-awareness)
practices.tradition: Sufism (esp. Naqshbandi-Mujaddidi, Kubrawi, Chishti)
practices.description: Directing concentrated awareness and remembrance of God to each subtle center to purify and illuminate it.
practices.howPracticed: After initiation (bay'a) and transmission (tawajjuh) from a shaykh, the seeker focuses on each latifa in order (often Qalb first), holding its bodily point and color while performing silent dhikr (e.g., repeating 'Allah' or a divine Name, sometimes coordinated with the breath/heartbeat).
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Purify the nafs and activate each subtlety so the seeker becomes al-insan al-kamil (the Complete Human) in nearness/union with Allah.
practices.feltExperience: Subtle warmth, presence, opening of the heart, visionary unveiling (kashf) — understood as divine gift.
practices.safetyNotes: Requires a living shaykh and silsila (lineage); practiced within Islamic law and devotion. Equating the lataif with chakras is regarded by many practitioners as a category error.

practices.name: Contemplation of the sefirot / kavvanah
practices.aliases: hitbonenut (contemplation); tzeruf (letter-permutation, ecstatic Kabbalah)
practices.tradition: Jewish Kabbalah
practices.description: Meditative contemplation of the divine emanations and Hebrew letters/Names, directing devotion and intention to draw down divine light.
practices.howPracticed: Prayer and mitzvot performed with kavvanah (focused intention) aimed at specific sefirot; visualization of the Tree of Life, the Hebrew letters of divine Names, and their colors/positions; in ecstatic Kabbalah (Abraham Abulafia), breathing, chanting, and head-movements accompany letter permutations.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Devekut (cleaving to God), tikkun (rectification/repair of the cosmos), and channeling shefa (divine flow) into the world.
practices.feltExperience: Primarily devotional/intellectual absorption; in ecstatic streams, trance and prophetic-type states.
practices.safetyNotes: Traditionally restricted to mature, learned (often male, married, 40+) students; warnings against premature or unguided 'entering the orchard' (pardes).

practices.name: Kosha discrimination (Vedantic self-inquiry)
practices.aliases: panchakosha viveka; neti-neti
practices.tradition: Advaita Vedanta / Yoga
practices.description: Successively identifying and 'setting aside' each sheath to discern the Atman beneath.
practices.howPracticed: Contemplative analysis (often after meditation/pranayama) moving attention inward from the food-body, to the breath-sheath, mind, intellect, and bliss-sheath, recognizing 'I am not this' (neti-neti) at each layer.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Realize the Self (Atman) as identical with Brahman, beyond all five sheaths.
practices.feltExperience: Progressively subtler interiority and detachment from identification with body and mind.
practices.safetyNotes: Conceptual/contemplative rather than energetic; low physical risk but doctrinally specific to Vedanta.

visualMotifs: Hindu chakras: lotus flowers (padma) with fixed petal-counts, each enclosing a geometric yantra (yellow square=earth, crescent=water, downward red triangle=fire, hexagram/interlaced triangles=air), a Sanskrit bija syllable, presiding deity and Shakti; the vertical spine-axis with ida/pingala spiraling around sushumna (visually echoing a caduceus).
The serpent: kundalini depicted as a coiled snake at the base of the spine, uncoiling and rising — a central iconographic motif (hence Woodroffe's title 'The Serpent Power').

Tibetan vajra body: thangka and manual diagrams of three vertical channels (central blue/dark, right red, left white) with chakras drawn as wheels/lotuses at crown, throat, heart, navel; seed-syllables and the inner flame at the navel.

Daoist 'Neijing Tu' (Chart of the Inner Landscape/Warp): the torso depicted as a mountain-and-river landscape with the three dantian as furnaces/fields, waterwheels, and a plowing figure — qi circulation shown as flowing water; yin-yang taiji symbol; the microcosmic orbit as a loop up the back and down the front.

Sufi lataif: colored points mapped on a schematic torso/head (yellow, red, white, black/blue, green), sometimes arranged with associated prophets; calligraphic divine Names; generally aniconic (no figural deities), favoring geometry, light, and the heart as a polished mirror.

Kabbalah: the Tree of Life (Etz Chaim) — ten sefirot as circles connected by 22 paths in three vertical pillars, overlaid on the body of Adam Kadmon (cosmic man); color schemes per the 'Four Worlds' (e.g., the Golden Dawn 'King/Queen/Emperor/Empress' scales in Hermetic versions); Hebrew letters and divine Names.
Koshas: concentric rings/onion-layers or nested human silhouettes around a luminous core (the Atman) — a layered, not point-based, image.

Modern syncretic motif (to flag with care): the rainbow-colored seven-chakra ladder (red-base to violet-crown) is a 20th-century Theosophical/New Age visual, NOT classical iconography; classical chakra colors differ (e.g., Manipura red, not yellow; Vishuddha not blue in the old texts).
research.attributionNotes: CORE CAUTION — avoid false equivalence. These five systems are not interchangeable and lining them up as a single 'master chart' (chakra=sefirah=lataifa=dantian=kosha) is a modern Theosophical/New Age construction, not how any source tradition understands itself. Key differences the app must preserve: (1) NUMBER — 3 dantian vs 5 koshas vs 5–6 lataif vs 7 chakras vs 10 sefirot; (2) KIND — chakras/dantian/tsa-lung are located centers; lataif are psycho-spiritual organs/faculties; sefirot are divine emanations (theology of God first, body second); koshas are concentric sheaths, not points; (3) GOAL — moksha/liberation (Hindu), Buddhahood/bliss-emptiness (Tibetan), return to the Dao/immortality (Daoist), union with Allah / al-insan al-kamil (Sufi), devekut & cosmic tikkun (Kabbalah), Self-realization (Vedanta). Where sources disagree (e.g., lataif colors and locations vary by order; chakra petal/element details vary by text), say so explicitly. LINEAGE & SACREDNESS: Tibetan tummo and the lataif require initiation/empowerment and a living teacher (lama/shaykh) within a transmission lineage (silsila/wang); Kabbalah was traditionally restricted to mature, learned students. Presenting these as free-floating self-help techniques strips their sacred, initiatory, and ethical context. APPROPRIATION: the familiar rainbow chakra system reflects colonial-era Theosophical reinterpretation (Blavatsky, Leadbeater) rather than Indian tradition; scholar Kurt Leland notes the rainbow-colors-plus-qualities package first appears together only c.1977. Likewise 'Hermetic Kabbalah' is a 19th-c. occult reinvention distinct from living Jewish Kabbalah. The app should attribute each model to its own tradition, flag the modern synthesis as modern, mark contested points as contested, and present everything as living belief and practice — explicitly NOT as medical or scientific fact. Use correct, consistent transliterations (e.g., Sat-Cakra-Nirupana, Svadhisthana, lataif-e-sitta, sefirot/sephiroth, dantian/tan-t'ien, pancha-kosha) and honor each tradition's self-description over cross-tradition mapping.
