research.topic: Hesychasm and the Jesus Prayer: Eastern Orthodox Christian breath-prayer (Philokalia, the heart, Mount Athos)
research.summary:
Hesychasm (from Greek hēsychia, "stillness/quiet/silence") is the contemplative-mystical tradition of Eastern Orthodox Christianity centered on unceasing inner prayer, watchfulness of the heart, and the experiential knowledge of God. Its signature practice is the Jesus Prayer — "Lord Jesus Christ, Son of God, have mercy on me, a sinner" — repeated continuously until it "descends from the lips into the mind, and from the mind into the heart" and becomes self-acting, like the rhythm of breathing. The tradition traces to the Egyptian and Sinai desert fathers (4th–5th c.; the prayer's seed appears in Diadochos of Photiki, c. 450), is shaped by Symeon the New Theologian (949–1022), systematized with psychophysical (posture + breath) methods by Nikephoros the Hesychast (13th c.) and Gregory of Sinai (c. 1260s–1346), and given its theological charter by Gregory Palamas (1296–1359). The corpus was anthologized as the Philokalia (Venice, 1782) by Nikodimos of the Holy Mountain and Makarios of Corinth, and carried into the Slavic world via Paisius Velichkovsky's Dobrotolubiye (1793) and into popular Russian piety through the anonymous 19th-c. The Way of a Pilgrim.

The practice involves sitting quietly, bowing the head toward the chest/heart (in some texts gazing toward the "middle of the body" or navel), slowing and coordinating the breath with the words of the prayer (commonly inhaling on "Lord Jesus Christ, Son of God" and exhaling on "have mercy on me, a sinner"), and "bringing the mind (nous) down into the heart" so that intellect and heart are unified in attention before God. The goal is the threefold movement of katharsis (purification), theoria (illumination/vision of God's uncreated light), and theosis (deification — participation in God's life by grace).

In the 14th-century Hesychast controversy, the Calabrian monk Barlaam mocked the monks as omphalopsychoi ("people whose soul is in their navel") and charged them with the Messalian heresy, denying that the light they reported could be the genuine, uncreated divine light. Palamas defended them in the Triads with the essence-energies distinction: God's essence (ousia) is utterly unknowable and imparticipable, but his uncreated energies (energeiai) — including the Light of Tabor seen at Christ's Transfiguration — are truly God and can be experienced by the purified. Councils at Constantinople (1341, 1347, 1351) vindicated Palamas, and Palamism became Orthodox dogma.

Crucially, Orthodox authorities themselves insist the bodily/breath technique is strictly secondary — an "accessory" subordinate to humility, repentance, and grace — and warn that pursuing psychophysical methods without an experienced spiritual father (geron/starets) risks prelest (spiritual delusion) or physical harm. This is a devotional, ecclesial practice aimed at communion with the personal God of Christian faith, not a stress-reduction or wellness technique. Scholars note striking structural parallels with Sufi dhikr and Indian japa/yogic breath-discipline, but treat any claim of direct historical borrowing as unproven and contested.
research.historyAndOrigin:
Roots: the desert monasticism of 4th–5th c. Egypt, Sinai, and Palestine — the ideal of "pray without ceasing" (1 Thessalonians 5:17) and short "arrow prayers." John Cassian (c. 360–435) transmits the formula "O God, make speed to save me; O Lord, make haste to help me" (Ps 70:1); Diadochos of Photiki (c. 450) is the earliest to recommend continual invocation of the name of Jesus, and the Sinai abbots John Climacus (Ladder of Divine Ascent) and Hesychios elaborate watchfulness (nepsis). The prayer rope (komboskini) tradition is attributed to St. Anthony the Great (c. 251–356).

Byzantine flowering: Symeon the New Theologian (949–1022) stressed direct, conscious experience of divine light. The explicit psychophysical method (coordinating breath, posture, and the gaze with the prayer to "bring the mind into the heart") is documented by Nikephoros the Hesychast (Nikephoros the Monk, 13th c., Mount Athos) in his treatise "On Watchfulness and the Guarding of the Heart," and by Gregory of Sinai (c. 1260s–1346), who systematized and spread the method on Athos. A short treatise "On the Three Methods of Prayer and Attention" circulated under Symeon's name and became a key technical text (its attribution is debated by scholars).

The 14th-c. controversy: from c. 1337 Barlaam of Seminara (of Calabria) attacked the Athonite hesychasts; Gregory Palamas (1296–1359, later Archbishop of Thessalonica) defended them in the Triads. Synods of Constantinople (May 1341, 1347, May 1351), plus the Hagioritic Tome drafted under Palamas and the political triumph of John VI Kantakouzenos, vindicated hesychasm; Palamism became Orthodox dogma and Palamas was canonized (commemorated the 2nd Sunday of Great Lent).

Anthologizing and dissemination: The Philokalia (full title Philokalia tōn hierōn nēptikōn, "The Philokalia of the Holy Neptic/Watchful Fathers") was compiled by St. Nikodimos of the Holy Mountain (the Hagiorite) and St. Makarios of Corinth and first printed in Venice in 1782. Paisius (Paisii) Velichkovsky produced a Church Slavonic selection, Dobrotolubiye (Moscow, 1793), shaping Russian elder (starets) spirituality (e.g., Optina). Theophan the Recluse made an expanded Russian translation (5 vols., 1877). The anonymous Russian The Way of a Pilgrim (Otkrovennye rasskazy strannika..., first published Kazan, 1884) popularized the Jesus Prayer for a lay audience and carried it into 20th-c. Western awareness.

coreConcepts.aliases: hesychasm; isychia; Greek ἡσυχία; adj. hesychast (ἡσυχαστής)
coreConcepts.meaning: Inner and outer stillness/silence/quiet; the contemplative state and the tradition built around it. A hesychast is one who pursues this stillness.

coreConcepts.aliases: Prayer of the Heart; Greek 'Kyrie Iēsou Christe, Hyie tou Theou, eleēson me ton hamartōlon'; Russian 'Gospodi Iisuse Khriste, Syne Bozhiy, pomilui mya greshnago'
coreConcepts.meaning: The invocation 'Lord Jesus Christ, Son of God, have mercy on me, a sinner.' Variant forms shorten it ('Lord Jesus Christ, have mercy on me'; 'Jesus, have mercy'); a Greek form omits 'a sinner.' Combines Christological confession (Phil 2; Lk 1) with the publican's plea (Lk 18).
The contemplative faculty of the soul — not discursive reason but the organ of spiritual perception. Hesychasm seeks to gather the scattered nous and 'bring it down into the heart.'

coreConcepts.aliases: Greek καρδία; 'the place of the heart'
coreConcepts.meaning: In Hesychasm the heart is the spiritual center of the whole person — the meeting point of body, soul, and spirit — where the nous is to dwell and pray. Described by Kallistos Ware as 'the point of convergence between mind and matter.'

coreConcepts.aliases: Greek νῆψις; 'watchfulness,' 'sobriety,' 'vigilance'; the Fathers are called 'neptic'
coreConcepts.meaning: Sober inner attentiveness that guards the heart against intrusive thoughts (logismoi); the practical discipline underlying the prayer.
Focused inward attention paired with prayer in the technical treatises ('attention and prayer').

coreConcepts.aliases: κάθαρσις / θεωρία / θέωσις; purification / illumination(-contemplation) / deification
coreConcepts.meaning: The threefold spiritual path: cleansing from passions; contemplation and vision of God's uncreated light; and union with God by participation in his energies (deification/divinization).

coreConcepts.aliases: Tabor Light; Greek 'Phōs tou Thabōr'; Taboric light
coreConcepts.meaning: The divine radiance seen at Christ's Transfiguration on Mount Tabor; held by Palamas to be an uncreated divine energy that the purified can perceive — the visible sign of theoria.

coreConcepts.aliases: ousia (οὐσία) and energeiai (ἐνέργειαι); the Palamite/essence-energies distinction; Palamism
coreConcepts.meaning: Palamas's teaching that God's essence is utterly unknowable/imparticipable, while his uncreated energies (operations, including the Light of Tabor) are truly God and are genuinely experienced — safeguarding both divine transcendence and real union.
Illusory spiritual states — mistaking imagination, sensual warmth, or demonic suggestion for grace. A central danger the Fathers warn against, especially when forcing technique without guidance.

coreConcepts.aliases: Greek γέρων (geron); Russian старец (starets); 'elder,' spiritual father/guide
coreConcepts.meaning: The experienced spiritual director under whose obedience the prayer (and any breath technique) must be practiced; obedience and humility are deemed essential safeguards.
Intrusive thoughts/impulses the practitioner watches for and cuts off, returning attention to the prayer.

coreConcepts.aliases: Greek κομποσκοίνι (komboskini); Russian чётки (chotki), вервица (vervitsa); prayer rope
coreConcepts.meaning: Knotted wool/silk rope (commonly 33, 50, or 100 knots) used to count repetitions of the Jesus Prayer; each knot tied as interlocking crosses.

practices.name: The Jesus Prayer (oral / mental / prayer of the heart)
practices.aliases: Prayer of the Heart; monologistos (single-phrase) prayer
practices.tradition: Eastern Orthodox Christianity (Byzantine/Athonite hesychasm; Russian/Slavic starchestvo)
practices.description: Continuous, rhythmic repetition of 'Lord Jesus Christ, Son of God, have mercy on me, a sinner,' aiming for ceaseless prayer (1 Thess 5:17). Theophan the Recluse describes three progressive degrees: (1) oral/vocal prayer (spoken with the lips, attention may wander); (2) mental/focused prayer (the mind attends to the words as one's own); (3) prayer of the heart (the prayer becomes 'self-acting,' continuing spontaneously in the heart, even during other activity). Some Fathers enumerate finer gradations (up to nine).
practices.howPracticed: Sit in a quiet place (Gregory of Sinai: on a low stool ~9 inches high; head and shoulders bowed; eyes lowered toward the 'place of the heart'). Recite the prayer slowly and attentively, often counting on a komboskini (prayer rope). The Way of a Pilgrim describes a rule increasing from 3,000 to 6,000 to 12,000 repetitions a day under an elder's direction until the prayer becomes continuous. The mind (nous) is gathered and 'brought down into the heart' so that attention rests within rather than wandering. Imagination and mental pictures are deliberately rejected (imageless prayer).
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Unceasing remembrance of God; purification of the heart from passions (katharsis); union of mind and heart; ultimately theoria (vision of the uncreated light) and theosis (deification — participation in God's energies). The aim is communion with the living, personal God, not relaxation or self-improvement.
practices.feltExperience: Texts describe the prayer first feeling laborious and dry ('darkness and an impenetrable density' within), then gradually warming: a sense of compunction, tears, then a quiet 'warmth' in the region of the heart and an 'unceasing joy'/sweetness as the prayer 'descends into the heart' and seems to pray itself, like the heartbeat or breath. The Fathers warn that sought-after sensible warmth/sweetness can be deceptive (see safety).
practices.safetyNotes: Must be practiced under an experienced spiritual father (geron/starets); humility, repentance, and obedience are primary, technique strictly secondary. Pursuing intense feelings or 'descending into the heart' prematurely risks prelest (spiritual delusion), even madness. Brianchaninov and Theophan warn against deliberately warming the 'lower heart' (linked to lust/passion) and against relying on the more physical methods; Theophan even discouraged some readers from the technical passages of The Way of a Pilgrim.

practices.name: Psychophysical / breath-coordinated method ('bringing the mind into the heart')
practices.aliases: the 'psychosomatic method'; the 'artistic'/technical method; the Third Method of Prayer and Attention (attrib. Symeon the New Theologian); method of Nikephoros the Hesychast / Gregory of Sinai
practices.tradition: Byzantine hesychasm, esp. 13th–14th c. Mount Athos; recorded in the Philokalia
practices.description: An optional bodily aid in which posture, gaze, and breathing are coordinated with the Jesus Prayer to help the scattered mind settle in the heart. The 'Three Methods' text contrasts a faulty first method (eyes/mind raised to heaven, imagining), a faulty second (attention in the head), and the recommended third method (attention in the heart).
practices.howPracticed: Sit on a low seat in a quiet cell; bow the head, resting the chin/beard on the chest; lower the gaze (some texts: toward the chest/heart, others 'toward the middle of the body' or the navel). Restrain/slow the breath ('hold your breath slightly,' breathe gently through the nose) and, while breathing in, send the nous inward 'with the breath' to search out the 'place of the heart,' keeping the mind there in attention while repeating the prayer. A common breath pairing: inhale on 'Lord Jesus Christ, Son of God,' exhale on 'have mercy on me, a sinner'; recitation may also be timed to the heartbeat.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: To collect the wandering intellect, unite mind and heart, and support continuous, attentive, imageless prayer — a means to inner stillness, not an end in itself.
practices.feltExperience: Initially inward 'darkness' and resistance; with persistence 'as though miraculously, an unceasing joy,' and the intellect is said to behold the 'open space within the heart' as 'luminous.' A gentle, natural warmth in the heart region is reported by adepts (to be received, not chased).
practices.safetyNotes: Strongly hedged by the tradition. Kallistos Ware: the physical technique is 'no more than an accessory' and needs 'the personal guidance of an experienced master.' Bishop Ignatius Brianchaninov: 'Many, wishing to learn it by experience, have damaged their lungs and gained nothing.' Theophan the Recluse reportedly said such bodily/breathing methods were 'virtually forbidden' in his youth to keep beginners from harming their lungs or falling into delusion. Holding/forcing the breath and forcing attention into the lower heart are specifically cautioned against.

energyCenters.name: The Heart (kardia) — 'the place of the heart'
energyCenters.location: The physical region of the chest/heart, understood simultaneously as the spiritual center of the whole person (body, soul, spirit). Practitioners lower the gaze and direct attention here; some seek the precise 'place of the heart' just below/around the physical heart.
energyCenters.associations: Associated with the nous (when 'brought down' into it), with watchfulness (nepsis), with compunction and tears, and — for the purified — with sensible 'warmth' and the inner perception of the uncreated light. Brianchaninov distinguishes an 'upper' heart (coinciding with the spiritual heart, the proper focus) from a 'middle/lower' heart linked respectively to the powers of anger and lust, which must NOT be deliberately stimulated. No color/seed-syllable symbolism — Hesychasm is imageless and Christ-centered.
energyCenters.meaning: In Orthodox anthropology the heart is the innermost altar of the person where God is met; 'prayer of the heart' means the whole self, unified, standing consciously before God. It is a theological-anthropological center, explicitly NOT a chakra-style energy node.

research.energyFlowModel: Hesychasm is NOT framed as a system of subtle energy channels or rising energy; what 'moves' is attention/the nous and the breath that carries it, and ultimately divine grace. The governing model is a downward and inward movement: the scattered mind, normally dispersed through the senses and 'in the head,' is gathered and made to 'descend' into the heart — often pictured as the nous traveling inward 'with the breath' drawn in through the nostrils. Once united with the heart, the nous 'stands guard' there, watching and repelling intrusive thoughts (logismoi) and continually offering the Name from 'the depths of the heart.' The progression is described as the prayer moving from the lips, to the mind, to the heart, where it finally becomes 'self-acting' and continuous, synchronized with breath and heartbeat. Blockage is the unpurified heart (full of passions and 'darkness'); 'opening' is its gradual purification, at which point grace is experienced as warmth and the heart is perceived as 'luminous' — illumined by the uncreated divine energies/light. Critically, the light and grace are understood to come from God (uncreated energies), not to be generated by the body; the breath technique merely disposes the person to receive. The theological frame is the essence-energies distinction: God's essence stays transcendent while his energies (light, grace, deifying power) flow to and indwell the purified heart.
research.feltSenseAndTransitions: Beginners are told the prayer feels effortful, mechanical, and 'dry,' and that turning attention inward reveals 'darkness and an impenetrable density' — the still-unpurified heart. With patient, attentive repetition (and, in the technical method, gentle slowing of the breath through the nose with the head bowed toward the chest), attention gradually settles from the head into the chest. The transition the texts emphasize is the prayer 'descending from the mind into the heart': it stops being something one does and becomes something happening within, continuing on its own — likened to the prayer beating with the pulse or moving with the breath ('like breathing'). Affectively, sources describe a sequence of compunction and tears, then a quiet, gentle warmth gathering in the region of the heart, then a deep, steady joy/sweetness and peace, and (for advanced adepts) an inner perception of luminosity. The Fathers stress these felt states are gifts to be received passively, not sensations to be manufactured: deliberately working up heat or feeling — especially in the 'lower heart' — is treated as a danger sign (a path to passion and prelest) rather than progress. The healthy felt-sense is described as calm, sober, humble, and Christ-focused, not ecstatic or dramatic.
visualMotifs: The icon of Christ (esp. Christ Pantocrator) as the focus of the invocation — the Name made visible
The icon of the Transfiguration: Christ in a mandorla of radiant white/gold uncreated light on Mount Tabor, with Moses, Elijah, and the dazzled apostles Peter, James, and John below
The mandorla and halo/nimbus of gold light depicting the uncreated divine energies/glory
The prayer rope (komboskini / chotki / vervitsa): a black knotted wool loop, commonly 33, 50, or 100 knots, often with a tassel and a small cross
The heart as inner sanctuary/altar where the nous dwells (a conceptual, devotional image rather than a diagrammed energy center)
The seated, head-bowed hesychast monk (chin toward chest, eyes lowered) in monastic black, in a cell or on Mount Athos
Imagelessness itself as a 'motif': advanced prayer rejects mental pictures, so Hesychast prayer is intentionally aniconic in the mind even while venerating icons externally
Gold, white, and radiant light as the dominant palette of divine presence; deep stillness and dark monastic interiors as the human counterpart
research.attributionNotes: This is a living, sacred devotional practice of the Eastern Orthodox Church, not a secular wellness or relaxation technique, and it should never be presented as a 'breathing hack.' Its purpose is repentance and communion with the personal God of Christian faith (the name and saving work of Jesus Christ are essential content, not interchangeable mantra-sound). Represent it respectfully: (1) Keep technique subordinate — Orthodox authorities (Kallistos Ware, Ignatius Brianchaninov, Theophan the Recluse) are emphatic that breathing/posture is merely an 'accessory,' that humility, repentance, sacramental life, and obedience are primary, and that the method should be undertaken only under an experienced spiritual father (geron/starets). Note the real warnings of physical harm and prelest (spiritual delusion). (2) Do not flatten the theology: the essence-energies distinction and the uncreated Light of Tabor are specific Orthodox dogmatic teachings (vindicated at the 14th-c. councils), not generic 'inner light.' (3) On parallels: scholars observe genuine structural similarities with Sufi dhikr (invocation of the divine Name, breath/posture, descent into the heart) and with Indian japa/yogic pranayama, and there were zones of historical contact; but direct borrowing is unproven and contested in either direction — present the resemblance as comparative phenomenology, NOT as evidence that Hesychasm 'comes from' yoga or Sufism (a framing Orthodox writers explicitly reject; Ware's caution that shared 'picture-frames' may contain 'utterly different' pictures). (4) The 'navel-gazing' (omphaloskepsis / omphalopsychoi) label is a hostile caricature coined by Barlaam; use it only with that context, not as a neutral description. (5) Honor that the prayer rope (komboskini) and icons are blessed sacred objects, and Mount Athos is a specific, restricted monastic community — avoid trivializing or commercializing imagery.

research.topic: Jain breath, prana, and Preksha Dhyana meditation (a distinct dharmic contemplative tradition)
research.summary:
Jainism is an independent dharmic tradition (neither Hindu nor Buddhist), centered on the eternal individual soul (jiva), the bondage of subtle material karma, and an extraordinarily expansive ethic of non-harm (ahimsa). Its understanding of "breath" and "vital energy" is doctrinally distinct from Hindu prana-as-cosmic-life-force. In classical Jain metaphysics, prana means the "vitalities" or life-functions of a being. There are ten dravya-pranas (material/physical vitalities) — including respiration (shvasocchvasa) — possessed in differing numbers by beings from one-sensed to five-sensed; these are contrasted with the bhava-pranas, the soul's intrinsic conscious-energetic vitalities. Breath is thus one vitality among ten, not a unifying cosmic energy. Crucially, because Jainism recognizes microscopic one-sensed life (including air-bodied beings, vayukaya, and nigoda colonies) pervading air, water, earth, and fire, the very act of breathing is understood to risk harming subtle life-forms. This grounds an ethic of restraint (samvara) — gentle, careful, minimal action rather than forceful breath manipulation — which is why classical Jainism was historically ambivalent or restrained about vigorous pranayama in a way Hindu yoga was not.

Classical Jain meditation (dhyana) is codified in the Tattvartha Sutra (Umasvati, c. 2nd–5th c. CE) and earlier canon (Acaranga, Sutrakritanga, Sthananga) and later the Dhyanasataka of Jinabhadra. Dhyana is defined as concentration of thought lasting up to one antarmuhurta (~48 minutes) and is divided into four kinds: two inauspicious (arta = sorrowful/anxious; raudra = cruel/violent) which bind karma, and two auspicious (dharmya = virtuous, with four objects of contemplation; shukla = pure, in four progressively stiller stages) which lead to liberation. Notably, the canon itself holds that the highest shukla-dhyana is unattainable in the present degenerate age — a point that later motivated reform movements to create accessible practice.

Preksha Dhyana ("meditation of perception/seeing") is the major modern systematization. It was developed within the Svetambara Terapanth sect, conceived under Acharya Tulsi (1914–1997) and elaborated by his successor Acharya Mahaprajna (also spelled Mahapragya, 1920–2010) from c. 1970–1975, publicly named in 1974 at Jaipur, with the eight-limb model finalized around 1996. Mahaprajna explicitly presented it as a rediscovery/revival of techniques he traced to Mahavira, synthesized with modern physiology (nervous and endocrine systems) and color/aura concepts. Its breath components — shvas-preksha (perception of breathing), deergha-shvas (long deep breathing), and samavritti-shvas (alternate-nostril) — together with leshya dhyana (meditation on psychic colors) and the 13 chaitanya kendras (centers of consciousness, mapped onto endocrine glands and explicitly compared-yet-distinguished from yogic chakras) are 20th-century systematizations built on ancient doctrinal vocabulary (preksha, leshya, kayotsarga, anupreksha all appear in the canon).
research.historyAndOrigin:
Jainism traces to the 24 Tirthankaras, the last being Mahavira (c. 599–527 BCE), a contemporary of the Buddha; it is one of the oldest continuous Indian (shramana) traditions and is doctrinally independent of Vedic/Hindu and Buddhist lineages. Two major sects: Digambara ("sky-clad") and Svetambara ("white-clad"), the latter further including reform orders such as Sthanakvasi and, branching from it, the Terapanth (founded 1760 by Acharya Bhikshu).

Classical meditation doctrine: the Acaranga Sutra (c. 3rd c. BCE) describes Mahavira's standing ascetic meditation and kayotsarga ("abandonment of the body"); the Sutrakritanga references preksha (self-observation); the Sthananga Sutra and Tattvartha Sutra (Umasvati, c. 2nd–5th c. CE — the one text revered across sects) codify the four dhyanas; Jinabhadra's Dhyanasataka (Jhanajjhayana, c. 6th c.) elaborates them. Later, tantric-influenced texts — Subhacandra's Jnanarnava (c. 11th c.) and Hemacandra's Yogashastra (12th c.) — introduced mantra, visualization, elemental concentrations, and breath/chakra material previously marginal in Jainism. Haribhadra (8th c.) engaged Patanjali's yoga in a Jain frame (Yogadrishtisamuccaya).

Preksha Dhyana (modern): conceived by Acharya Tulsi, ninth (or by some counts tenth-line) acharya of the Svetambara Terapanth, and systematically developed by his designated successor Acharya Mahaprajna/Mahapragya. Mahaprajna researched and experimented for roughly two decades; the system was named "Preksha Dhyana" in 1974 (Jaipur, during the Mahavira Nirvana 2500th anniversary), formalized from 1975, and its definitive eight-limb structure presented around 1996 ("Preksha Year"). Its institutional home is Jain Vishva Bharati, Ladnun (Rajasthan), and Adhyatma Sadhana Kendra (near Delhi). Mahaprajna deliberately framed it in modern scientific/therapeutic language (nervous system, endocrine glands, stress reduction) for lay and global audiences.

coreConcepts.aliases: atman (Jain sense); the soul / living substance
coreConcepts.meaning: The eternal, conscious individual soul — the locus of life, knowledge and bliss. Each jiva is distinct and beginningless. In Jain dualism, jiva (sentient) is opposed to ajiva (insentient matter/pudgala). Liberated souls (siddhas) rise to the apex of the universe.

coreConcepts.aliases: prana / prana; the ten vitalities; bala (forces)
coreConcepts.meaning: In Jainism, prana means a 'vitality' or life-function, NOT a unifying cosmic life-breath. The ten dravya-pranas (material vitalities) are: the 5 sense-faculties (touch, taste, smell, sight, hearing), 3 powers/balas (of mind, speech, body), respiration (shvasocchvasa), and life-span/lifespan-energy (ayushya). Beings possess 4 to 10 of these depending on their grade.

The soul's intrinsic, non-material vitalities — infinite consciousness, perception, bliss and energy. These are the 'real' life-powers; the dravya-pranas are their material correlates in embodied beings. Severing another's pranas out of passion constitutes himsa (harm).
Respiration, counted as the 9th of the ten material vitalities. One of the few life-functions, not a master energy. Its observation (shvas-preksha) is the breath component of modern Preksha Dhyana.

The supreme Jain vow; non-injury to any jiva, including one-sensed microscopic beings. Stricter than Hindu/Buddhist forms because of the vast inventory of subtle life. Motivates restraint in all action, including breathing and movement.
Severance or injury of vitalities (pranas) of any being through passion-driven (or careless) act of mind/speech/body. The thing ahimsa avoids.

Unlike Hindu/Buddhist karma, Jain karma is subtle PHYSICAL matter that adheres to the soul through activity (yoga) and passion (kashaya), obscuring its innate qualities. Meditation aims at samvara (stopping influx) and nirjara (shedding karma).

coreConcepts.aliases: lesya; psychic color; 'color of the soul'
coreConcepts.meaning: The coloration of the soul produced by the interaction of karmic matter and passion; a marker of moral-emotional state. Six types in ascending purity. In Preksha it is reframed as the aura/radiation of consciousness and worked with via color visualization.
Focused concentration of thought, classically up to one antarmuhurta (~48 min). Four kinds (arta, raudra, dharmya, shukla); only the latter two are spiritually auspicious.
Standing or seated motionless relaxation with full awareness, dissolving identification with the body. Ancient canonical practice; in Preksha it is the foundational relaxation limb.
Periodic vow of equanimity (often ~48 min) renouncing harmful activity; the core accessible meditative-ritual practice for laity and ascetics; includes pratikramana, kayotsarga, etc.
Deep repeated contemplation (e.g., on impermanence, solitariness, the body's impurity). Canonical; in Preksha it is the contemplation limb, and bhavana/auto-suggestion is treated separately.

To perceive carefully and impartially — to see/observe one's own psychic states without like (raga) or dislike (dvesha). Root term of the modern system; the word itself appears in the canon (Sutrakritanga: 'sampikkhae appagamappaenam' — see the self by the self).

coreConcepts.aliases: caitanya kendra; psychic centre / centre of consciousness
coreConcepts.meaning: In Preksha, 13 zones of 'dense consciousness' in the body used for perception/meditation. Mahaprajna correlates them with endocrine glands and compares them to (but distinguishes them from) yogic chakras.
Preksha practice of moving attention/'vital energy' upward along the spinal cord (sushumna-equivalent), from the shakti kendra at the spine's base to the gyan kendra at the head's top.

energyCenters.name: Shakti Kendra (Center of Energy)
energyCenters.location: Base/lower end of the spinal cord (perineum region)
energyCenters.associations: Reservoir of vital energy; correlated with the gonads/reproductive endocrine system; starting point of the antaryatra upward journey. Roughly parallels the muladhara chakra but framed as a center of consciousness, not a deity-seat.

energyCenters.name: Swasthya Kendra (Center of Health/Bliss)
energyCenters.associations: Health and well-being; correlated with gonadal/lower endocrine activity.

energyCenters.name: Tejas Kendra (Center of Bio-electricity / Fire)
energyCenters.associations: Fire/heat, vigor, digestion, the 'taijasa' (electric/fiery) body; correlated with the adrenal glands; loosely parallels manipura. Associated with the red/tejo spectrum.

energyCenters.name: Anand Kendra (Center of Bliss)
energyCenters.associations: Bliss, emotional equanimity; correlated with the thymus; the seat used in green-color leshya practice (visualizing green light radiating from the Centre of Bliss). Parallels anahata.

energyCenters.name: Vishuddhi Kendra (Center of Purity)
energyCenters.associations: Purification of impulses; correlated with the thyroid; parallels vishuddha.

energyCenters.name: Brahma Kendra (Center of the Universe / Self-restraint)
energyCenters.location: Tip of the tongue / mouth region
energyCenters.associations: Linked to taste-control, speech and dietary restraint (rasendriya). No close standard-chakra equivalent.

energyCenters.name: Prana Kendra (Center of Vital Energy / Life)
energyCenters.associations: Respiration and vital breath; the natural focus of shvas-preksha at the nostrils.

energyCenters.name: Apramada Kendra (Center of Vigilance / Prudence)
energyCenters.associations: Alertness, non-negligence (apramada), self-vigilance.

energyCenters.name: Chakshu Kendra (Center of Sight)
energyCenters.associations: Visual perception; sense-restraint of sight.

energyCenters.name: Darshan Kendra (Center of Intuition / Vision)
energyCenters.associations: Intuition; correlated with the pituitary gland; in color meditation the 'color of the rising sun' is visualized here for intuition. Parallels ajna chakra.

energyCenters.name: Jyoti Kendra (Center of Enlightenment / Light)
energyCenters.associations: Emotional regulation and the emergence of feelings; correlated with the pineal gland (some sources hypothalamus); bright WHITE color is visualized here for peace. Associated with leshya purification.

energyCenters.name: Shanti Kendra (Center of Peace)
energyCenters.associations: Channeling and calming of feelings; correlated with the hypothalamus; associated with cool/peaceful color (white).

energyCenters.name: Gyan Kendra (Center of Knowledge / Wisdom)
energyCenters.associations: Highest center; involves cerebrum, cerebellum, brain-stem; the destination of the antaryatra. Parallels sahasrara, but framed as the seat of pure knowledge of the soul, not union with a deity.

research.energyFlowModel: Two layered models must be distinguished. (1) CLASSICAL Jain doctrine has no Hindu-style nadi/prana circulation. What "flows" is not cosmic prana but karmic matter (pudgala): activity of mind, speech and body (yoga) plus passions (kashaya) cause influx (asrava) and bondage (bandha) of subtle karma to the soul; meditation produces samvara (stoppage of influx) and nirjara (shedding) until the soul's innate energy (virya, a bhava-prana) is fully unobscured. Breath here is simply one of ten vitalities sustaining the embodied jiva; the soteriological 'direction' is toward stillness — the final shukla-dhyana stage ceases even subtle bodily vibration and respiration, and the liberated soul rises straight up to the siddha-shila at the universe's summit. (2) The MODERN Preksha model overlays a subtle-body circulation: vital energy/consciousness is concentrated at the shakti kendra (base of spine) and is consciously raised upward through the spinal cord (antaryatra, an analogue of sushumna) to the gyan kendra at the crown, passing through the 13 chaitanya kendras. Breath is used as the vehicle of perception and as a carrier of 'colored' energy in leshya practice (e.g., inhaling streams of bright green light into the Centre of Bliss and radiating it outward to purify the aura). Mahaprajna further maps the body's right side to the sympathetic and left to the parasympathetic nervous system, and the centers to endocrine glands, so 'flow' is reframed as balancing nervous and hormonal activity. Blocks/disturbances are described as imbalances of energy and as inauspicious leshyas (dark colors) clouding the aura; the practice 'opens'/purifies by perception, relaxation, color visualization and auto-suggestion, transmuting dark leshyas (krishna/nila/kapota) into bright ones (tejo/padma/shukla).
research.feltSenseAndTransitions: Preksha instructions emphasize impartial PERCEPTION of sensation rather than forcing experience. In kayotsarga the body is felt to grow heavy, warm, loose and 'dissolved,' with a progressive sense of separation between the gross physical body and a subtler inner body; awareness of a fine tingling/vibration or current of vital energy is cultivated. In shvas-preksha the practitioner is told to simply feel the touch of the incoming/outgoing breath at the rims of the nostrils (cool on inhalation, warm on exhalation) and the rise/fall at the navel — observing, not controlling. Deergha-shvas (long breath) makes breathing slow, deep, rhythmic and abdominal, producing a felt calming, lengthening and settling. In chaitanya-kendra practice, sustained attention on a center is said to be felt as warmth, pulsation, pressure or subtle vibration ('dense consciousness') at that spot. In antaryatra a sensation of energy or warmth is felt to travel up the spine from the base to the crown. In leshya dhyana the practitioner is guided to vividly feel a colored light entering with the breath and bathing/radiating from a center (e.g., bright green at the heart, bright white at the forehead/peace center), with a felt shift from agitation toward coolness, lightness and equanimity. Transitions are sequential and hierarchical: relaxation enables breath-perception, which steadies attention enough for body- and center-perception, which in turn supports color work and contemplation — calmer, subtler states unfolding as gross activity quiets.

practices.name: Kayotsarga (relaxation with self-awareness)
practices.aliases: kausagga; 'abandonment of the body'
practices.tradition: Ancient canonical (Acaranga etc.); foundational limb of modern Preksha (Svetambara Terapanth)
practices.description: Total relaxation of the body while remaining fully, alertly aware — dissolving identification with the body.
practices.howPracticed: Sit (e.g., cross-legged/sukhasana or in a chair) or lie/stand motionless, spine erect, eyes gently closed. Systematically move attention through body parts, releasing muscular tension, often aided by auto-suggestion of relaxation. Classically performed standing motionless for set durations by ascetics.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Release of physical and mental tension; samvara (stilling of activity); preparation for deeper perception; experiential separation of gross and subtle bodies.
practices.feltExperience: Heaviness, warmth, looseness, a sense of the body 'dissolving,' subtle tingling/vibration; calm alert stillness.
practices.safetyNotes: Generally gentle and low-risk; lying down may induce sleep, which the tradition treats as a lapse of awareness rather than a hazard.

practices.name: Shvas-preksha (perception of breathing)
practices.aliases: shvas preksha; breath awareness
practices.tradition: Modern Preksha (built on canonical 'preksha' + the vitality shvasocchvasa)
practices.description: Non-judgmental perception of the natural breath as an object of awareness.
practices.howPracticed: With body relaxed and spine erect, place attention either at the navel (feeling the rise and fall) or at the rims of the nostrils, feeling the touch of breath at the nasal junction. Observe each inhalation and exhalation without controlling them.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Concentration, present-moment awareness, calming of the mind, stress reduction; a gateway to subtler perception.
practices.feltExperience: Felt touch of breath (cool in, warm out) at the nostrils; gentle abdominal movement; growing inner quiet.
practices.safetyNotes: Pure observation, not breath-holding; considered safe.

practices.name: Deergha-shvas-preksha (perception of long/deep breathing)
practices.aliases: dirgha-svasa-preksha; long breath
practices.tradition: Modern Preksha
practices.description: Deliberately slow, deep, rhythmic (typically abdominal/diaphragmatic) breathing, then perceiving it.
practices.howPracticed: Lengthen and deepen the breath so it becomes slow, smooth and rhythmic, the abdomen rising on inhalation and falling on exhalation; keep attention on the long stream of air. (Preksha sources generally do not prescribe a fixed numeric ratio; emphasis is on slow, even, complete breaths.)
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Develop the 'inherent capacities' of the subconscious mind; deepen calm; increase vitality and steadiness.
practices.feltExperience: A felt lengthening and settling; relaxation, coolness, mental spaciousness.
practices.safetyNotes: Should be gentle and effortless; avoid strain or hyperventilation.

practices.name: Samavritti-shvas-preksha (alternate-nostril breathing perception)
practices.aliases: samavrtti-svasa; alternate breathing
practices.tradition: Modern Preksha (acknowledged as resembling Hatha-yoga nadi-shodhana)
practices.description: Breathing alternately through left and right nostrils with perception of the flow.
practices.howPracticed: Inhale through one nostril, exhale through the other and reverse, perceiving the breath; aimed at balancing the two sides (linked by Mahaprajna to balancing sympathetic/parasympathetic activity).
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Balance of nervous-system activity; equanimity and harmonization of the body's two sides.
practices.feltExperience: Balanced, even airflow; calming, centering sensation.
practices.safetyNotes: Keep gentle and unforced; not aimed at vigorous breath retention.

practices.name: Sharir-preksha (perception of the body)
practices.aliases: sarira-preksha; body scan
practices.tradition: Modern Preksha
practices.description: Systematic inward perception of bodily sensations across the three bodies (gross/audarika, fiery/taijasa, karmic/karmana).
practices.howPracticed: After relaxation, move awareness through the body perceiving subtle sensations and the flow along sensory/motor nerves.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Sensitivity to subtle vital flows; deeper self-awareness; integration.
practices.feltExperience: Tingling, warmth, pulsation, subtle currents.
practices.safetyNotes: Gentle and safe.

practices.name: Chaitanya-kendra-preksha (perception of psychic centers)
practices.aliases: caitanya-kendra-preksha; center-of-consciousness meditation
practices.tradition: Modern Preksha (Mahaprajna's mapping to endocrine glands)
practices.description: Concentration on each of the 13 centers of consciousness in turn, perceiving its sensations and 'activating' it.
practices.howPracticed: After kayotsarga, focus on a center (e.g., darshan kendra between the brows), often combining concentration (dharana), brief breath-holding, color visualization, and auto-suggestion; can proceed upward via antaryatra.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Harmonize the endocrine/nervous systems; regulate emotions and impulses; develop an integrated personality and access deeper consciousness.
practices.feltExperience: Warmth, pressure, pulsation or vibration at the focused center.
practices.safetyNotes: Mild; intensive concentration/breath-holding should be moderate.

practices.name: Leshya-dhyana (perception of psychic colors)
practices.aliases: lesya-dhyana; color/aura meditation
practices.tradition: Modern Preksha (term and six colors are canonical; the visualization technique is modern)
practices.description: Visualization of bright, auspicious colors at specific centers to purify the soul's coloration (aura) and transmute negative states.
practices.howPracticed: At a chosen center visualize and 'breathe in' a bright color (e.g., bright green particles/light at the Anand kendra/Centre of Bliss; bright white at the Jyoti/Shanti kendra for peace; rising-sun color at the Darshan kendra for intuition), letting the colored light spread through and radiate from the body.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Replace inauspicious leshyas (black/blue/grey) with auspicious ones (red/yellow/white); emotional purification; balance of energy; purification of the aura.
practices.feltExperience: Vivid felt sense of colored light, coolness/warmth, lightness, calm, emotional uplift.
practices.safetyNotes: Imaginative/gentle; no special contraindications noted.

practices.name: Anupreksha & Bhavana (contemplation and auto-suggestion)
practices.aliases: anuvekkha; the twelve bhavanas; auto-suggestion
practices.tradition: Anupreksha is canonical (12 reflections); bhavana as auto-suggestion is modern framing
practices.description: Sustained reflective contemplation (impermanence, solitariness, etc.) and repeated positive resolve/verbal suggestion to cultivate virtues.
practices.howPracticed: After perception practices, contemplate chosen themes or repeat affirmations of virtue/equanimity to generate 'counter-vibrations' to negative impulses; Mahaprajna developed 25+ contemplations.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Detachment, equanimity, character transformation, dissolution of negative tendencies.
practices.feltExperience: Calm conviction, settling of agitation, sense of inner resolve.
practices.safetyNotes: Safe; mental practice.

practices.name: Classical dhyana: dharmya & shukla (Tattvartha Sutra)
practices.aliases: dharma-dhyana; sukla-dhyana
practices.tradition: Ancient canonical (Tattvartha Sutra 9.27–46; Sthananga; Dhyanasataka)
practices.description: The two auspicious meditations leading to liberation, contrasted with the karma-binding arta- and raudra-dhyana.
practices.howPracticed: Dharmya-dhyana contemplates four objects: ajna-vicaya (the truth as taught by the Jina), apaya-vicaya (the causes of suffering/wrong belief), vipaka-vicaya (the fruition of karmas), samsthana-vicaya (the structure of the universe/loka). Shukla-dhyana proceeds in four ever-subtler stages: (1) prithaktva-vitarka-savicara (multi-aspect contemplation with shifting), (2) ekatva-vitarka-avicara (single-aspect, no shifting), (3) sukshma-kriya-apratipati (only subtle bodily activity/respiration remains), (4) vyuparata-kriya-anivritti (cessation of all activity — perfect stillness). Held up to one antarmuhurta (~48 min).
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Shedding karma and progress to omniscience (kevala-jnana) and liberation; the final two shukla stages occur at/after omniscience and lead to final emancipation.
practices.feltExperience: Described doctrinally as progressively deeper equanimity and stillness — likened to a steady flame in a windless place and to the immovability of Mount Meru — rather than as somatic sensation.
practices.safetyNotes: The texts themselves hold that true shukla-dhyana is unattainable in the present age (the first two stages require lost purva scriptures), so it is doctrinal/aspirational, not a lay technique.

visualMotifs: Loka-purusha / Loka-akasha: the cosmos depicted as a giant standing/striding 'cosmic man' (or figure with hands on hips and legs apart) — lower body = seven hells (naraka), narrow waist = the middle world of humans and animals, broad chest = heavens (deva-loka), and the head/crown = the siddha-shila of liberated souls. The soul's soteriological 'upward' direction is shown by this vertical cosmography.
Siddha-shila: a slim crescent/umbrella-like white arc at the very top of the loka where liberated, bodiless siddhas dwell eternally; the destination of the rising liberated soul.

Swastika: a central auspicious symbol; its four arms denote the four states/destinies of rebirth (heavenly beings, humans, animals/plants, hell-beings) within samsara. Often topped by three dots = ratnatraya (right faith, right knowledge, right conduct) and a crescent-with-dot = the siddha-shila/liberated state.

Ahimsa hand: a raised open palm (abhaya/'stop' gesture) with a dharma-wheel in the center and the word 'Ahimsa' inscribed; the 24-spoked wheel evokes the 24 Tirthankaras and the wheel of samsara — a call to pause and harm no being.

The official emblem of Jainism (adopted 1975): combines the loka-purusha outline, the raised ahimsa hand with wheel, the swastika with three dots and crescent, framing the motto 'Parasparopagraho Jivanam' (souls render service to one another).

Leshya color spectrum: the six psychic colors visualized as a ladder from dark to light — black (krishna), blue (nila), grey/dove (kapota) [inauspicious] rising to red (tejo/pita), yellow/lotus (padma), and pure white (shukla) [auspicious]; in Preksha used as radiant colored light/aura around the body.

Centers-of-consciousness diagrams (modern Preksha): the seated human figure marked with the 13 chaitanya kendras up the spine and head, often shown alongside endocrine glands — a deliberate fusion of subtle-body and biomedical imagery.
Tirthankara iconography: serene seated (padmasana) or standing (kayotsarga) Jina figures, the kayotsarga standing posture itself being a visual emblem of motionless meditative body-abandonment.
research.attributionNotes: Attribute carefully by sect and by era. (1) SECT: Preksha Dhyana is specifically a Svetambara Terapanth creation (Acharya Tulsi and Acharya Mahaprajna/Mahapragya); it is institutionally tied to Jain Vishva Bharati, Ladnun. Do not present it as pan-Jain or as Digambara practice — Digambara traditions historically emphasize svadhyaya (scriptural self-study) and the mysticism of Kundakunda rather than this systematized method. The classical four-dhyana doctrine (Tattvartha Sutra) is, by contrast, shared across sects. (2) ERA — clearly flag ancient vs. modern: the VOCABULARY (preksha, leshya, kayotsarga, anupreksha, the ten pranas, the six leshyas, the four dhyanas) is genuinely ancient/canonical, but the SYSTEM — the eight limbs, shvas-/deergha-/samavritti-preksha as structured techniques, the 13 chaitanya kendras, color-visualization leshya dhyana, and the mapping to endocrine glands and the autonomic nervous system — is a 20th-century systematization (c. 1970s–1996). Mahaprajna himself framed it as a 'rediscovery' of Mahavira's practice; scholars generally describe it as a modern revival/innovation rather than a literal continuous transmission, so it is honest to say its antiquity is asserted by the tradition but contested/qualified by academics. (3) DISTINCTNESS: do not conflate Jain prana with Hindu prana — in Jainism prana is the ten vitalities (breath is just one), not a single cosmic life-force; and chaitanya kendras are explicitly NOT identical to chakras (Mahaprajna compares them but reframes them as centers of consciousness correlated with glands, and the model is non-theistic — no deities seated in the centers, unlike many tantric chakra systems). (4) SACREDNESS/ETHICS: ahimsa is the tradition's defining sacred commitment; the idea that breathing/movement can harm subtle one-sensed beings (air-bodied beings, nigoda) is doctrinally serious, not a quaint detail — represent it respectfully as an ethic of restraint, not as superstition. Avoid implying Jain meditation is a 'relaxation technique' only; for the tradition it is soteriological (karma-shedding toward moksha). The motto Parasparopagraho Jivanam and the ahimsa emblem should not be used flippantly or commercially. Spelling/transliteration varies (Mahaprajna/Mahapragya; leshya/lesya; Svetambara/Shvetambara; shukla/sukla) — note variants rather than 'correcting' source forms.

research.topic: Sikh breath and sound practice: Naam Simran, swaas-swaas simran, and Anahad Naad
research.summary:
Sikh contemplative practice centers on Naam Simran / Naam Japna — the loving remembrance and repetition of the divine Name (most commonly "Waheguru") — rather than on body-centric breath manipulation. Founded by Guru Nanak (1469–1539) in Punjab and elaborated by the nine Gurus who followed, Sikhi treats Naam (the divine Name/Word/Presence) as the means by which the human mind is purified, the ego (haumai) dissolved, and the soul (aatma) united with the Creator (Parmatma / Waheguru / Akal Purakh). Naam Simran is one of the three pillars of ethical Sikh life, alongside Kirat Karni (honest labour) and Vand Chhakna (sharing) — it is explicitly a householder's, in-the-world discipline, not a renunciate's.

Breath enters Sikh practice not as a thing to be forcibly controlled, but as the natural carrier of remembrance: the teaching of swaas-swaas simran (also saas giraas — "with every breath and every morsel") holds that the Name should be repeated with each inhalation and exhalation, so that no breath passes "empty" of God. This is grounded directly in Gurbani (e.g. Guru Granth Sahib Ang 295: "saas saas simrahu Gobind" — "with each and every breath remember the Lord of the Universe"; and Ang 980: a breath that escapes without the Naam is "useless"). A widely practiced technique (revived in the 20th century by Bhai Randhir Singh and rooted in earlier Rehat-namas) syncs the four syllables Wa-he-Gu-ru to inhalation and exhalation, but this is gentle breath-linked attention, NOT the forced retention (kumbhak) of hatha-yogic pranayama.

A second pillar is the experience of Anahad Naad / Anahad Shabad — the "unstruck sound" or celestial, unending melody heard inwardly in deep absorption. Sikhi borrows this vocabulary from the wider Indic milieu but pointedly reinterprets it: where Nath/yogic nada-yoga ties the unstruck sound to the rising of kundalini through chakras, the Gurus locate it in devotional union and the grace-opened Dasam Duar ("Tenth Gate"), insisting that what matters is not the sound but its Source — God. This reframing is dramatized in Guru Nanak's Siddh Gosht (Guru Granth Sahib pp. 938–946), a dialogue with Nath Siddhas in which Nanak rejects hatha-yoga, austerities, and renunciation, redefining "true yoga" as union with God through the Shabad while living an ordinary life.

Important attribution caution: the Western "Kundalini Yoga as taught by Yogi Bhajan" / 3HO movement markets Sikh symbols, dress, and the Waheguru mantra but is widely rejected by mainstream Sikhs as a syncretic new religious movement (described by historian Trilochan Singh as "a sacrilegious hodgepodge" and by Time as "synthetic Sikhism"); Sikhism proper has no chakra/kundalini system and no living guru, and Yogi Bhajan was later found by an independent investigation to have "more likely than not" committed serious abuse. Mainstream Sikh contemplative practice must not be conflated with it.
research.historyAndOrigin:
Origin: the Punjab region of north India (and present-day Pakistan), beginning with Guru Nanak Dev Ji (1469–1539), the first of ten human Gurus. The practice and theology were developed across the Guru period (1469–1708) and codified in the Adi Granth / Guru Granth Sahib, compiled by the fifth Guru, Guru Arjan, in 1604 (Sukhmani Sahib composed by Guru Arjan c.1602). After Guru Gobind Singh (the tenth Guru, d. 1708), authority passed to the scripture itself, the Guru Granth Sahib, as the eternal living Guru — hence Sikhism's rejection of any living human guru.

Naam Simran and swaas-swaas remembrance are taught throughout Gurbani, so they are not later inventions: the verses "saas saas simrahu Gobind" (Ang 295) and the teaching that a breath without Naam is wasted (Ang 980) are scriptural. The specific breath-by-breath technique syncing the syllables of "Waheguru" to inhalation/exhalation is recorded in early Rehat-namas (codes of conduct), e.g. the Chaupa Singh Rehat-nama (a contemporary of the 10th Guru records the instruction to utter "Vaheguru" with each breath) and the Prem Sumarg; in the 20th century it was prominently revived and taught by Bhai Randhir Singh (1878–1961) and figures in the Akhand Kirtani Jatha tradition, and by sant lineages such as Baba Nand Singh, Baba Attar Singh, and Baba Ishar Singh. The contested point in scholarship/community debate is not whether swaas-swaas simran is in Gurbani (it is) but the degree to which any single fixed breath-counting technique should be regarded as mandatory.

The Siddh Gosht (Guru Nanak's debate with Nath/Gorakhnath-lineage Siddhas, including Charpatnath, Bhangarnath, and Loharipa) reflects the 16th-century Punjab context in which Nath hatha-yogis held wide influence; Guru Nanak's reframing positions Sikhi deliberately against forced breath-control and renunciation.

coreConcepts.aliases: Nam; the Divine Name; the Word; Naam (ਨਾਮ)
coreConcepts.meaning: The divine Name / immanent presence and creative power of God; not merely a word but the realized connection with Akal Purakh. The object of remembrance.

coreConcepts.aliases: Simaran; Naam Simran; from Sanskrit smarana ('remembrance')
coreConcepts.meaning: Loving, continual remembrance of God, typically through quiet/mental repetition of the Name. Contemplative absorption rather than mechanical chanting.
Repetition/recitation of the Name; jap = quieter individual repetition, jaap = louder/communal. Pillar of Sikh life alongside Kirat Karni and Vand Chhakna.
Principal Name used in Simran. Wah(e) = wonder/awe; Guru = the dispeller of darkness. 'Wondrous Lord/Enlightener.' Four syllables Wa-he-Gu-ru used in breath practice.
Remembering God with every breath (and every mouthful of food); breath-linked Name-remembrance taught in Gurbani.
The divine Word/utterance of the Guru/God; vehicle of union. Surat (consciousness) is to be merged with Shabad (surat-shabad).

coreConcepts.aliases: Anhad, Anahat, Anhad Naad, Anhad Dhuni, Anhad Bani, Anhad Toora, Anhad Jhunkara; 'unstruck sound'
coreConcepts.meaning: The limitless, unending celestial melody heard inwardly in deep absorption — sound 'not struck' by any two objects, contrasted with ahat naad (struck/worldly sound). Sign of God's omnipresence.
Egoism/self-centredness — the core spiritual disease that Naam Simran dissolves, freeing one of the 'five thieves' (lust, anger, greed, attachment, pride).

coreConcepts.aliases: Dasam Dvar / Dasam Dwaar; the 'Tenth Gate'
coreConcepts.meaning: A subtle tenth 'opening' (beyond the nine bodily orifices) opened by Guru's grace, where the devotee perceives God within and hears the Anahad Shabad. Not a physical chakra.
State of effortless, natural equipoise and spontaneous bliss — Guru Nanak's reframing of 'yoga' as union attained through Naam and grace, not technique.
The faculty of attention/awareness that is to be tuned to and merged with the Shabad (surat-shabad-dhun).
The Timeless Being / Supreme formless God who is remembered in Simran; immanent and transcendent.
Devotional singing of Gurbani set to raga; communal, breath-borne form of Naam remembrance.

energyCenters.name: Dasam Duar (the Tenth Gate)
energyCenters.location: A subtle, non-physical 'opening' traditionally associated with the crown/forehead region (often described as opened in the area of the head/'tenth door'), beyond the nine bodily orifices (eyes, ears, nostrils, mouth, anus, genitals).
energyCenters.associations: Symbol/geometry: a hidden 'gate' or 'door' rather than a lotus or chakra. Opened only by the Guru's grace (gurprasad), not by technique. Associated experiences: hearing the Anahad Shabad/Naad, inner light, darshan (vision) of the Divine, sahaj (equipoise) and ecstatic union of aatma with Parmatma. Note: Sikhi does NOT teach a fixed colour/element/seed-syllable/deity scheme for it — unlike a yogic chakra. It is a metaphor for a state of God-realization, not an anatomical energy node.

energyCenters.name: (Comparative, NOT Sikh doctrine) Anahata chakra of Hindu/yogic nada-yoga
energyCenters.location: Heart region, central channel of the spine — cited here only because Sikh 'anahad' shares vocabulary with it.
energyCenters.associations: In yogic tradition: element air/vayu; twelve-petalled lotus; intersecting triangles (shatkona); bija (seed) mantra 'Yam'; deity Vayu/Isha with shakti Kakini; animal the antelope; 'unstruck sound' heard when kundalini reaches this centre. IMPORTANT: the Sikh Gurus explicitly do NOT adopt this chakra/kundalini map; they use 'anahad' for the sound of God's omnipresence, not for a heart-chakra activation. Included only for contrast/attribution clarity.

research.energyFlowModel:
Sikhism does not teach the chakra/nadi/kundalini anatomy of hatha and nada yoga; Guru Nanak in the Siddh Gosht and elsewhere deliberately sets Sikh practice apart from such body-mechanics. The Sikh 'flow' model is devotional and attentional rather than physiological:

1) Breath (prana / pavan / swaas) is treated as the natural vehicle of remembrance. The teaching saas-saas / saas-giraas holds that the Name should ride every inhalation and exhalation so that breathing itself becomes continuous worship. There is no forced retention (kumbhak), bandhas, or counted ratios as in pranayama — Gurbani in fact criticizes those who 'control the breath' as missing the point.

2) Attention/consciousness (surat) is the thing that 'moves': in simran the restless mind is gathered and the surat is tuned to and merged with the Shabad (surat-shabad-dhun). With deepening absorption, attention 'rises/settles' inward and the Shabad is said to 'pull' the surat toward the Dasam Duar (Tenth Gate).

3) The Dasam Duar, opened by the Guru's grace, is the inward 'opening' at which the devotee hears the Anahad Naad (the unstruck, unending celestial sound) and experiences union of the soul (aatma) with God (Parmatma). The blockage to be removed is haumai (ego) and the wandering of the mind; the 'opening' is grace, not technique.

In practitioner accounts of the breath-by-breath Wahe-Guru method, concentration is described as gradually internalizing — moving from the tongue to silent inner repetition, often felt as concentrated 'between the eyes' — and, after long practice, possibly culminating in the opening of the Dasam Duar. But mainstream theology stresses that this is the fruit of devotion and grace, and warns against treating it as a guaranteed mechanical outcome.
research.feltSenseAndTransitions:
How it is taught to feel: The progression begins with audible or whispered repetition of "Waheguru" to corral a restless mind, then moves inward to silent, mental simran. Practitioners describe the breath becoming smooth and unforced as the Name rides each inhalation/exhalation; a settling of anxiety (Gurbani: 'the anxiety within your mind shall depart', Ang 295) and the loosening of ego.

In the breath-syllable technique the four syllables are felt on the breath: WA on inhalation, HE on exhalation, GU on inhalation, RU on exhalation (one common scheme; variants exist). Over time the verbalization drops away and the simran is felt as an internal, effortless 'dhun' (vibration/current) of the Name, with attention often described as gathering 'between the eyebrows.'

Deeper transitions are described in terms of sahaj (a spontaneous, effortless bliss and equipoise) and, in advanced absorption, the inward hearing of the Anahad Naad — an unstruck, unending celestial melody/vibration 'which cannot be explained' and is not produced by any external sound. This is portrayed as ecstatic union (vismaad — wonder/awe) of the soul with God, accompanied by inner light and a sense of the Divine presence (darshan) at the Dasam Duar. Mainstream teaching frames these as gifts of grace experienced in devotion, not states to be forced.

practices.name: Naam Simran (silent/whispered remembrance of Waheguru)
practices.aliases: Naam Japna, Naam Japo, Simran, Jap
practices.tradition: Mainstream Sikhism (all schools)
practices.description: The core practice: continual loving remembrance of the divine Name, most often 'Waheguru,' done mentally, in a whisper, or aloud.
practices.howPracticed: Sit comfortably (commonly cross-legged, spine upright; can be done anytime, anywhere — walking, working, lying down). Repeat 'Waheguru' continuously, letting the mind rest on the Name. Often begun aloud to focus a restless mind, then taken inward to silent mental repetition. No mandatory posture, mudra, or breath ratio; mala (beads) sometimes used to count.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Dissolve haumai (ego), conquer the 'five thieves,' attain equipoise (sahaj), align with God's Will (Hukam), and ultimately union of the soul with God.
practices.feltExperience: A quieting of mental anxiety, growing absorption, and a felt inner 'dhun' (current) of the Name; at depth, sahaj bliss and inner stillness.
practices.safetyNotes: Gentle and non-coercive; no breath retention. Considered safe for anyone. The aim is loving attention, not strain.

practices.name: Swaas-swaas / Saas Giraas simran (breath-linked remembrance)
practices.aliases: Saas saas simran; breath-by-breath simran
practices.tradition: Mainstream Sikhism; the syllable-on-breath method emphasized in the Akhand Kirtani Jatha / Bhai Randhir Singh tradition and various sant lineages
practices.description: Synchronizing remembrance of the Name with the breath so that no breath passes without the Name. Rooted in Gurbani (Ang 295, 980) and early Rehat-namas.
practices.howPracticed: One common technique: split 'Wa-he-Gu-ru' across the breath — WA (inhale), HE (exhale), GU (inhale), RU (exhale); or simply hold the whole Name on each inhalation and exhalation. Breathing stays natural; the breath carries the Name rather than being held or counted into ratios.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Make remembrance continuous and effortless ('with every breath and every morsel'), so the Name becomes the 'life-breath' (prana) of the devotee; progress toward internalized simran and, by grace, opening of the Dasam Duar.
practices.feltExperience: Breath becomes smooth and unforced; attention internalizes and is often felt concentrated between the eyes; deepening calm and absorption.
practices.safetyNotes: NOT pranayama: no forced inhalation/retention/exhalation ratios, no kumbhak, no bandhas. The breath should remain natural. Distinguish clearly from hatha-yogic breath-control, which Guru Nanak critiqued.

practices.name: Gurbani Kirtan (devotional singing)
practices.aliases: Shabad Kirtan; Keertan
practices.tradition: Mainstream Sikhism
practices.description: Communal/solo singing of hymns from the Guru Granth Sahib set to raga — a breath-borne form of Naam remembrance.
practices.howPracticed: Hymns (shabads) sung in their prescribed ragas, often with harmonium, tabla, and traditional stringed instruments; congregation listens and joins. Singing naturally lengthens and steadies the breath.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Absorption in the Shabad, collective remembrance, emotional/spiritual upliftment, merging of surat with the Word.
practices.feltExperience: Emotional resonance, tears of devotion, a sense of communal sangat (fellowship) and absorption; calming, rhythmic breath.
practices.safetyNotes: No contraindications; an accessible, embodied devotion.

practices.name: Recitation of Sukhmani Sahib
practices.aliases: Sukhmani; 'Prayer/Psalm of Peace'
practices.tradition: Mainstream Sikhism
practices.description: Recitation of Guru Arjan's composition (24 ashtpadis / 192 padas) in the Guru Granth Sahib, set in Gauri Sukhmani raga. Sukh = peace, mani = jewel/treasure of the mind.
practices.howPracticed: Recited (paath) or sung individually or in groups at home or gurdwara; a full reading takes ~60–90 minutes. Tradition holds it was composed in response to a request that God's Name be remembered with every breath and morsel.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Peace of mind (sukh), constant remembrance of God, emotional healing, dissolution of ego and the five thieves.
practices.feltExperience: Deep calm, mental tranquility, meditative absorption through melodic recitation.
practices.safetyNotes: None; a gentle devotional discipline.

practices.name: Mool Mantar / Gurmantar contemplation
practices.aliases: Mul Mantra; Ik Onkar ... Gur Prasad
practices.tradition: Mainstream Sikhism
practices.description: Contemplation of the opening creedal statement of the Guru Granth Sahib (Ik Onkar Sat Naam ...), and of the Gurmantar 'Waheguru.'
practices.howPracticed: Repeated and reflected upon as a meditative anchor; often given at Amrit Sanchar (initiation). Jaap Sahib (Guru Gobind Singh) offers many divine names for contemplation.
practices.intendedEffectOrGoal: Establish the foundational understanding of One God and focus the mind for simran.
practices.feltExperience: Centering, reverence (vismaad/awe), focus.
practices.safetyNotes: None.

visualMotifs: Ik Onkar (ੴ) — the foundational symbol/calligraphic glyph meaning 'One God / God is One,' opening the Guru Granth Sahib; the single most pervasive visual of Sikh belief (the numeral 'one' + 'Onkar').

Khanda — the emblem of Sikhi: a central double-edged sword (khanda, signifying truth/divine knowledge and the cutting away of ego), encircled by the chakkar (a circle representing the eternal, beginningless and endless God), flanked by two crossed kirpans representing Miri-Piri (the union of spiritual and temporal authority).

The Harmandir Sahib (Golden Temple), Amritsar — the central shrine, surrounded by the Amrit Sarovar (pool of nectar); imagery of its golden form reflected in water evokes serenity, equality (four doors open to all directions/castes), and continuous kirtan.
Nishan Sahib — the triangular saffron/blue Sikh flag bearing the Khanda, marking gurdwaras.
Gurmukhi script and illuminated Guru Granth Sahib folios — Gurbani depicted as sacred sound made visible; the scripture enthroned under a canopy (palki/chanani).

Depictions of sangat in kirtan (harmonium, tabla, congregation) and of an individual in simran with mala beads — breath/remembrance shown as devotional posture and communal song rather than as chakra diagrams.

Imagery of light/radiance (jot) and the wondrous (vismaad) — sometimes a luminous halo around the Gurus — to evoke the inner light and the unstruck sound; Sikhi generally avoids chakra-lotus charts (those belong to yogic, not Sikh, iconography).
research.attributionNotes:
CRITICAL attribution points for respectful, accurate presentation:

1) Distinguish Sikh practice from yogic body-mechanics. Although Sikhi uses Indic vocabulary (anahad/anahat, naad, prana/pavan), it deliberately reinterprets these. Guru Nanak's Siddh Gosht rejects hatha-yoga, forced pranayama (breath-control), austerities, and renunciation. Do NOT present Naam Simran as kundalini awakening, chakra activation, or pranayama. The Dasam Duar is a metaphor of grace-given God-realization, not a physical chakra; mainstream Sikhi has no chakra/nadi system. Where a 'chakra'-stage account of breath-simran appears (in some sant/practitioner literature), flag it as a particular lineage's interpretation, not normative Sikh doctrine.

2) The '3HO / Kundalini Yoga as taught by Yogi Bhajan' attribution issue MUST be flagged clearly. 3HO (founded 1969, Los Angeles, by Harbhajan Singh Khalsa / 'Yogi Bhajan') markets Sikh symbols, white dress/turbans, and the Waheguru mantra but is a syncretic new religious movement that the mainstream Sikh community largely rejects. The SGPC (via president Gurcharan Singh Tohra, 1977) denied Bhajan's leadership claims; historian Trilochan Singh called his Sikhism-Tantrism synthesis 'a sacrilegious hodgepodge'; Time called it 'synthetic Sikhism,' noting 'their religion does not include yoga' and Sikhs 'do not revere living gurus.' Historian Philip Deslippe documented that Bhajan's 'Kundalini Yoga' was assembled from other teachers and reframed. An independent 2020 investigation (An Olive Branch) concluded it was 'more likely than not' that Yogi Bhajan committed serious sexual and other abuse. Therefore: never label 3HO/Kundalini-Yoga content as 'Sikhism,' and do not borrow its color/chakra/mantra schemes as if they were Sikh.

3) Sacredness/handling. The Guru Granth Sahib is regarded as the living, eternal Guru; Gurbani and the Name are sacred. Present Naam Simran, kirtan, and Sukhmani Sahib reverently, with correct Gurmukhi/transliteration. Avoid trivializing the Name as a generic 'mantra for stress relief.'

4) Living tradition, householder ethic. Frame these as practices of a living world religion (~25–30 million Sikhs), rooted in equality, honest work (kirat karni), and sharing (vand chhakna) — not as exotic relics or as a 'technique' detached from ethics and grace.

5) Contested points: scholars/communities agree swaas-swaas simran is scriptural, but debate whether any one fixed breath-counting method is obligatory; present the Wahe-Guru-on-breath technique as one respected method (notably Bhai Randhir Singh / Akhand Kirtani Jatha and certain sant lineages), not the only or mandatory form.
